syllabus_desc: the International System (SI) units for length, mass, time, temperature (K and °C);
concept: The SI system is built on a handful of base units that everything else derives from. For length it's the metre (m), for mass it's the kilogram (kg), for time it's the second (s), and for temperature you've got both kelvin (K) and degrees Celsius (C). Kelvin and Celsius use the same size degree -- the only difference is where zero sits. Zero kelvin is absolute zero (-273.15C), the coldest anything can theoretically get. Zero Celsius is the freezing point of water. In aviation, you'll mostly use Celsius for weather and performance calculations, but kelvin shows up in gas law problems and ISA calculations.
exam_tip: To convert Celsius to kelvin, add 273. To convert kelvin to Celsius, subtract 273. The exam loves testing this simple conversion.
key_facts: Length: metre (m)
Mass: kilogram (kg) -- note it's the kilogram, not the gram, that's the SI base unit
Time: second (s)
Temperature: kelvin (K) and degrees Celsius (C); K = C + 273
Kelvin and Celsius degrees are the same size; only the zero point differs

syllabus_desc: the derivation of the SI units for force, pressure, power, and the non-SI units;
concept: Once you've got the base SI units, you can derive everything else. Force is measured in newtons (N) -- one newton is the force needed to accelerate one kilogram at one metre per second squared. Pressure is force per unit area, measured in pascals (Pa) -- one pascal is one newton per square metre. In aviation, we commonly use hectopascals (hPa), which are identical to millibars. Power is the rate of doing work, measured in watts (W) -- one watt is one joule of work per second. Non-SI units you'll encounter include pounds of force, pounds per square inch (psi), inches of mercury (inHg), and horsepower for engine output.
exam_tip: Remember the derivations: N = kg x m/s squared, Pa = N/m squared, W = J/s. Exam questions test whether you understand how these units relate to each other.
key_facts: Force: newton (N) = kg x m/s squared
Pressure: pascal (Pa) = N/m squared; 1 hPa = 1 millibar = 100 Pa
Power: watt (W) = joule/second; 1 horsepower = 746 watts approximately
Work/energy: joule (J) = N x m
Non-SI units include psi, inHg, pounds-force, and horsepower

syllabus_desc: foot, nautical mile, knot and horsepower.
concept: Here's where aviation gets charmingly old-fashioned. Despite the SI system being the scientific standard, pilots worldwide measure altitude in feet, distance in nautical miles, speed in knots, and engine power in horsepower. A foot is 0.3048 metres. A nautical mile is 1,852 metres -- it's based on one minute of arc of latitude, which is why it's so useful for navigation. A knot is one nautical mile per hour. And horsepower, dating back to James Watt comparing steam engines to horses, equals 746 watts. You'll use these units every single flight, so they need to be second nature.
exam_tip: Know that 1 NM = 1,852 m, 1 ft = 0.3048 m, 1 kt = 1 NM/hr, and 1 HP = 746 W. These conversions appear regularly in exam calculations.
key_facts: 1 foot = 0.3048 metres (altitude measurement worldwide)
1 nautical mile (NM) = 1,852 metres = 1 minute of latitude
1 knot (kt) = 1 nautical mile per hour = 1.852 km/h
1 horsepower (HP) = 746 watts approximately
These non-SI units persist in aviation due to international convention and practical utility

syllabus_desc: explain and or apply vector addition and subtraction;
concept: When you add vectors, you can't just add the numbers like you would with scalars. If two forces of 3 N and 4 N act on an object, the resultant could be anything from 1 N to 7 N depending on their directions. Vector addition uses the triangle method or parallelogram method: place the vectors head-to-tail (or side-by-side from a common point), and the resultant is the closing side. Vector subtraction is the same as adding a negative vector -- you reverse the direction of the vector being subtracted, then add normally. In the cockpit, you're doing vector addition every time you calculate a wind correction angle -- your heading vector plus the wind vector gives your track vector.
exam_tip: For vectors at right angles, use Pythagoras: resultant = square root of (A squared + B squared). For the angle, use trigonometry. Draw it out -- diagrams save time in the exam.
key_facts: Triangle method: place vectors head-to-tail; resultant closes the triangle
Parallelogram method: place vectors from common point; resultant is the diagonal
Same-direction vectors: resultant = sum of magnitudes
Opposite-direction vectors: resultant = difference of magnitudes
Right-angle vectors: use Pythagoras' theorem to find the resultant magnitude

syllabus_desc: demonstrate understanding and ability to resolve vector diagrams or problems.
concept: Resolving vectors is the reverse of adding them -- you break a single vector into two components, usually at right angles to each other. This is incredibly useful in aerodynamics. For example, in a climb, you resolve the weight vector into a component along the flight path (which drag has to help overcome) and a component perpendicular to it (which lift must balance). To resolve a vector, you project it onto your chosen axes using sine and cosine. The component adjacent to the angle uses cosine; the component opposite uses sine. Practice drawing these diagrams until they're automatic -- they're the key to understanding forces in flight.
exam_tip: Adjacent = hypotenuse x cosine of the angle. Opposite = hypotenuse x sine of the angle. Sketch the right triangle every time -- it prevents sign errors.
key_facts: Resolving means breaking one vector into two perpendicular components
Component along the axis = magnitude x cos(angle)
Component perpendicular to the axis = magnitude x sin(angle)
Used extensively in climb, descent, and turning force diagrams
The two resolved components, when added back together, recreate the original vector

syllabus_desc: Define speed, velocity and acceleration.
concept: Speed, velocity, and acceleration are related but distinct concepts, and the differences matter. Speed is a scalar -- it's how fast you're going, measured in metres per second (or knots in the cockpit). Velocity is a vector -- it's speed with a direction attached, like '150 knots heading 270'. Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. Here's the CPL-level catch: because velocity includes direction, you can be accelerating even if your speed doesn't change. An aeroplane in a level turn at constant speed is accelerating because its direction is continuously changing. That's why you feel the increased G-force in a turn -- acceleration is happening.
exam_tip: Any change in speed OR direction counts as acceleration. An aeroplane in a constant-speed turn is accelerating. This is a favourite exam trick.
key_facts: Speed: rate of change of distance (scalar), measured in m/s or knots
Velocity: rate of change of displacement (vector) -- speed plus direction
Acceleration: rate of change of velocity, measured in m/s squared
Acceleration occurs with any change in speed OR direction (or both)
Constant-speed turning flight involves acceleration (direction is changing)

syllabus_desc: explain inertia;
concept: Inertia is the tendency of a body to resist any change in its state of motion. If it's sitting still, it wants to stay still. If it's moving at 120 knots in a straight line, it wants to keep doing exactly that. The more massive an object is, the more inertia it has -- which is why a fully loaded aeroplane takes longer to accelerate on the runway than a lightly loaded one. Inertia isn't a force; it's a property of mass. You can feel it when you push the throttle forward for take-off -- your body tries to stay where it was while the aeroplane accelerates beneath you. That's inertia at work, straight from Newton's first law.
exam_tip: Inertia depends only on mass, not weight. A heavy aeroplane has the same inertia whether it's on the ground at sea level or in flight at FL250.
key_facts: Inertia is the resistance of a body to any change in its motion
Inertia is a property of mass -- more mass means more inertia
Inertia is not a force; it is a property described by Newton's first law
A body with greater inertia requires more force to produce the same acceleration
Inertia explains why heavier aircraft need longer take-off runs

syllabus_desc: differentiate between mass and weight;
concept: Mass and weight are often used interchangeably in everyday language, but in physics they're fundamentally different. Mass is the amount of matter in an object, measured in kilograms. It doesn't change regardless of where you are. Weight is the force that gravity exerts on that mass, measured in newtons. Weight = mass x gravitational acceleration (W = mg). Your mass is the same whether you're at Ardmore or on the moon, but your weight changes because gravitational acceleration is different. In aviation, we usually work with weight as a force because it's weight that lift has to overcome. At CPL level, you need to be precise about this distinction.
exam_tip: Mass is in kilograms, weight is in newtons. Weight = mass x g. If a question asks about mass, the answer is in kg; if it asks about weight, it's in N (or sometimes pounds-force).
key_facts: Mass: amount of matter, measured in kg, does not change with location
Weight: gravitational force on a mass, measured in newtons (N)
Weight = mass x gravitational acceleration (W = mg)
Mass is a scalar quantity; weight is a vector (acts toward centre of Earth)
At sea level on Earth, a 1 kg mass weighs approximately 9.81 N

syllabus_desc: state the value of the acceleration caused by the earth’s gravity; and
concept: The acceleration due to Earth's gravity, given the symbol 'g', is approximately 9.81 metres per second squared at sea level. What this means is that if you dropped a ball in a vacuum (no air resistance), it would gain 9.81 m/s of speed every second it falls. After one second it's doing 9.81 m/s, after two seconds it's doing 19.62 m/s, and so on. The value of g varies slightly with latitude and altitude -- it's fractionally higher at the poles than the equator, and decreases with altitude -- but for all practical aviation calculations, 9.81 m/s squared (often rounded to 9.8 or even 10) is the number you'll use.
exam_tip: Use g = 9.81 m/s squared unless told otherwise. Some questions round to 10 m/s squared for simplicity -- follow whatever the question specifies.
key_facts: g = 9.81 m/s squared (approximately) at sea level
g varies slightly with latitude (higher at poles, lower at equator) and altitude
g is the acceleration experienced by a freely falling body in a vacuum
For practical aviation calculations, g is treated as constant at 9.81 m/s squared
1g is the standard gravitational acceleration experienced in unaccelerated flight

syllabus_desc: define momentum.
concept: Momentum is the product of an object's mass and its velocity: p = mv. It's a vector quantity because velocity has direction. The concept matters in aviation more than you might think. Newton's second law can actually be restated as: force equals the rate of change of momentum. A propeller produces thrust by accelerating a mass of air backward -- it's changing the momentum of that air, and the reaction force is thrust. The heavier and faster a moving object is, the more momentum it has and the harder it is to stop or redirect. That's why a heavy, fast aeroplane needs more force (and therefore more distance) to change its state of motion.
exam_tip: Momentum = mass x velocity (p = mv). It's a vector quantity. A question might test whether you know momentum changes when direction changes, even at constant speed.
key_facts: Momentum (p) = mass x velocity (p = mv)
Momentum is a vector quantity (has magnitude and direction)
SI unit of momentum is kg.m/s
Force = rate of change of momentum (Newton's second law in momentum form)
Conservation of momentum applies in collisions and propeller thrust generation

syllabus_desc: differentiate between centripetal force and centrifugal reaction;
concept: Centripetal force is the real force acting toward the centre of the curved path. It's what causes the object to follow the curve rather than fly off in a straight line. Centrifugal reaction is the apparent outward force you feel as a passenger in a turning car -- it feels like you're being pushed outward, but you're actually just experiencing your body's inertia wanting to continue straight ahead while the car turns underneath you. In a banked aeroplane, the 'centrifugal force' you feel pressing you into the seat during a turn is this inertial reaction. Technically, centrifugal force is a fictitious force -- it only appears in a rotating reference frame -- but it's a useful concept for understanding what you feel in the cockpit.
exam_tip: Centripetal force is real and acts inward; centrifugal reaction is apparent and acts outward. Exam questions may test which one is the 'real' force.
key_facts: Centripetal force is real -- it acts toward the centre of curvature
Centrifugal reaction is apparent -- it's the inertial tendency to continue straight
Centrifugal force is a fictitious force that only appears in a rotating reference frame
In a turn, centripetal force = horizontal component of lift
The occupant feels centrifugal reaction as being pushed outward/into the seat

syllabus_desc: explain the factors affecting centripetal force and rate of turn.
concept: The centripetal force needed for a turn depends on three things: the mass of the aeroplane, the square of its velocity, and the radius of the turn. The formula is F = mv squared / r. So doubling your speed quadruples the centripetal force needed for the same radius turn. Halving the radius doubles the required force. Rate of turn (how many degrees per second you're turning) increases with more centripetal force and decreases with higher speed. This is why a slow aeroplane can turn more tightly than a fast one for the same bank angle -- it needs less centripetal force and achieves a smaller radius. At CPL level, understanding this relationship is essential for the turning flight chapter.
exam_tip: F = mv squared / r. Speed is squared, so it has the biggest effect. Rate of turn = V/r (in radians per second). Slower speed at the same bank angle gives a higher rate of turn.
key_facts: Centripetal force = mv squared / r
Doubling speed quadruples the required centripetal force for the same radius
Halving the radius doubles the required centripetal force
Rate of turn (omega) = V / r; increases with lower speed for a given radius
For a given bank angle, slower speed gives smaller radius and higher rate of turn

syllabus_desc: Describe the trig functions for the sine, cosine and tangent of an angle.
concept: Trigonometry is your friend in aerodynamics, even if it didn't feel that way at school. For a right-angled triangle, the three trig functions relate the angles to the sides. Sine of an angle = opposite side divided by the hypotenuse. Cosine = adjacent side divided by the hypotenuse. Tangent = opposite divided by adjacent (or equivalently, sine divided by cosine). The mnemonic SOH-CAH-TOA makes this easy to remember. You'll use these constantly -- resolving weight into components in a climb, finding the horizontal component of lift in a turn, calculating bank angles, and working with forces along and perpendicular to the flight path.
exam_tip: SOH-CAH-TOA: Sine = Opposite/Hypotenuse, Cosine = Adjacent/Hypotenuse, Tangent = Opposite/Adjacent. Know that sin 30 = 0.5, cos 60 = 0.5, sin 45 = cos 45 = 0.707.
key_facts: sin(angle) = opposite / hypotenuse
cos(angle) = adjacent / hypotenuse
tan(angle) = opposite / adjacent = sin / cos
SOH-CAH-TOA is the standard mnemonic
Common values: sin 30 = 0.5, cos 30 = 0.866, sin 45 = cos 45 = 0.707, sin 60 = 0.866, cos 60 = 0.5

syllabus_desc: Describe the moment of a force, and the moment of a couple.
concept: A moment is the turning effect of a force about a point. It's calculated as force multiplied by the perpendicular distance from the line of action of the force to the pivot point: Moment = Force x Distance. The units are newton-metres (Nm). A longer arm or a bigger force means a bigger moment -- that's why a long spanner makes it easier to undo a tight bolt, and why the tailplane, positioned well aft of the CG, can produce large pitching moments with relatively small forces. A couple is a special case: two equal and opposite parallel forces that aren't in line with each other. A couple produces pure rotation with no translation. The moment of a couple equals one force multiplied by the distance between them.
exam_tip: Moment = Force x perpendicular distance. A couple = two equal, opposite, parallel forces producing pure rotation. The tailplane-wing pitch interaction is a classic couple example.
key_facts: Moment = Force x perpendicular distance from the pivot (Nm)
A moment produces rotation about a point
A couple consists of two equal and opposite parallel forces separated by a distance
Moment of a couple = one force x distance between the forces
A couple produces pure rotation with no net translational force

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions required for translational equilibrium and for rotational equilibrium.
concept: Translational equilibrium exists when an object has no net force acting on it -- all forces balance out and there's no acceleration in any direction. In straight and level flight at constant speed, lift equals weight and thrust equals drag, giving translational equilibrium. Rotational equilibrium exists when there's no net moment (torque) about any point -- all the clockwise moments equal the anticlockwise moments, so there's no rotation. For an aeroplane to be truly in equilibrium, both conditions must be met simultaneously. If all forces balance but the moments don't, the aeroplane won't accelerate linearly but it will start rotating -- not a stable situation.
exam_tip: Translational equilibrium: net force = 0 (no acceleration). Rotational equilibrium: net moment = 0 (no rotation). Both are required for complete equilibrium in flight.
key_facts: Translational equilibrium: vector sum of all forces = 0, no linear acceleration
Rotational equilibrium: sum of all moments about any point = 0, no angular acceleration
Both conditions must be satisfied simultaneously for complete equilibrium
Straight and level flight at constant speed satisfies both conditions
An unbalanced moment causes rotation even if forces are balanced

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of centre of gravity (centre of mass).
concept: The centre of gravity (CG) -- also called the centre of mass -- is the single point at which the entire weight of the aeroplane can be considered to act. It's the balance point. If you could support the aeroplane at its CG, it would balance perfectly level. The CG position shifts as you load passengers, baggage, and fuel, and it changes during flight as fuel burns off. Its position relative to the wing's centre of pressure and the tailplane determines the aeroplane's pitch stability and controllability. A CG that's too far forward makes the aeroplane nose-heavy and hard to flare for landing. Too far aft and it becomes dangerously unstable in pitch. Every flight, the CG must be within the manufacturer's approved limits.
exam_tip: CG position affects stability, control authority, stall speed, and trim drag. Forward CG = more stable but higher stall speed and more trim drag. Aft CG = less stable, lighter control forces.
key_facts: CG is the point where total weight is considered to act
CG position moves with changes in loading and fuel burn
CG must be within manufacturer's approved limits for safe flight
Forward CG: more stable, heavier control forces, higher stall speed
Aft CG: less stable, lighter control forces, potential pitch-up divergence

syllabus_desc: Explain the meanings of work, power and energy; and describe kinetic energy and potential energy.
concept: Work, power, and energy are closely related concepts that underpin aircraft performance. Work is done when a force moves an object through a distance: Work = Force x Distance, measured in joules. Power is the rate of doing work: Power = Work / Time, measured in watts. Energy is the capacity to do work. There are two key forms: kinetic energy (energy of motion) = half x mass x velocity squared, and potential energy (energy of position/height) = mass x g x height. In aviation, these trade off constantly -- when you climb, you're converting kinetic energy and engine energy into potential energy. When you descend, potential energy converts back to kinetic energy. The kinetic energy formula shows that speed is squared, so doubling your speed quadruples your kinetic energy -- which is why landing distance increases dramatically with even a small increase in approach speed.
exam_tip: KE = 0.5 x m x V squared means doubling speed gives 4x the kinetic energy and roughly 4x the stopping distance. This is a favourite performance exam question.
key_facts: Work = Force x Distance (joules)
Power = Work / Time (watts); also Power = Force x Velocity
Kinetic energy = 0.5 x mass x velocity squared
Potential energy = mass x g x height
Speed is squared in the KE formula -- small speed increases have large energy effects

syllabus_desc: Interpret simple graphs.
concept: Graphs are the language of aerodynamics at CPL level. You'll encounter drag versus speed curves, power required versus speed curves, CL versus angle of attack curves, and many more. Being able to read and interpret these graphs is non-negotiable. Key skills include identifying the gradient (slope) of a line -- a steeper slope means a faster rate of change. You need to find maximum and minimum points, read off values at specific conditions, and understand what the area under a curve represents (often work or energy). A straight line through the origin means a directly proportional relationship. A curve means the rate of change itself is changing. When two curves intersect, the quantities are equal at that point -- like where power available meets power required, giving you maximum and minimum speed in level flight.
exam_tip: In drag/power curves, the bottom of the curve is the minimum value. Where two curves cross, the values are equal. Practice reading values and identifying gradients from graphs.
key_facts: Gradient (slope) represents rate of change -- steeper means faster change
Maximum/minimum points occur where the curve flattens (gradient = 0)
Intersection of two curves means the plotted quantities are equal at that point
Area under a force-distance graph represents work done
Proportional relationships appear as straight lines through the origin

syllabus_desc: Explain the air density, and describe the effect of temperature, pressure and humidity on atmospheric density.
concept: Air density is the mass of air per unit volume, and it's one of the most important variables in aviation because it directly affects lift, drag, and engine performance. Three factors change air density: temperature, pressure, and humidity. Higher pressure squeezes more molecules into a given volume, increasing density. Higher temperature makes molecules move faster and spread apart, decreasing density. Higher humidity means some of the heavier nitrogen and oxygen molecules are replaced by lighter water vapour molecules, so humid air is actually less dense than dry air at the same temperature and pressure. On a hot, humid, low-pressure day -- think Ardmore in February -- your aeroplane performs noticeably worse because the air is thinner than a cool, dry, high-pressure winter morning.
exam_tip: Remember: high pressure increases density, high temperature decreases density, high humidity decreases density. Humid air is LESS dense than dry air -- this catches people out.
key_facts: Air density = mass per unit volume (kg/m cubed)
Increased pressure increases density (direct relationship)
Increased temperature decreases density (inverse relationship)
Increased humidity decreases density (water vapour is lighter than N2 and O2)
Density directly affects lift production, drag, and engine power output

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal changes in pressure, temperature and density with increased altitude in the atmosphere.
concept: As you climb away from the surface, pressure, temperature, and density all decrease -- but not at the same rate. Pressure drops roughly exponentially: it halves approximately every 18,000 feet. Temperature drops at a fairly steady rate of about 2 degrees Celsius per 1,000 feet (the ISA lapse rate of 1.98C/1,000 ft) in the troposphere, which extends up to about 36,000 feet (11 km) at mid-latitudes. Above the tropopause, temperature stabilises at about -56.5C in the ISA model. Density, being a function of both pressure and temperature, also decreases with altitude. The practical upshot: as you climb, the air gets thinner, your engine produces less power, your wings need a higher TAS to generate the same lift, and eventually you reach a ceiling where you can't climb any more.
exam_tip: ISA lapse rate = approximately 2C per 1,000 ft. Pressure halves roughly every 18,000 ft. Temperature stops decreasing at the tropopause (36,090 ft ISA).
key_facts: Pressure decreases roughly exponentially with altitude (halves every ~18,000 ft)
Temperature decreases at ~2C per 1,000 ft (ISA lapse rate = 1.98C/1,000 ft) in the troposphere
Density decreases with altitude as both pressure and temperature decrease
The tropopause is at approximately 36,090 ft (11 km) in the ISA model
Above the tropopause, ISA temperature remains constant at -56.5C

syllabus_desc: State the ISA sea-level pressure and temperature conditions, and the approximate lapse rates in the lower atmosphere.
concept: The International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) is a mathematical model that gives us a common baseline for comparing aircraft performance. At sea level, ISA conditions are: pressure 1013.25 hPa (29.92 inHg), temperature 15C (288.15 K), and density 1.225 kg/m cubed. The temperature lapse rate is approximately 1.98C per 1,000 feet up to the tropopause. These numbers aren't meant to represent any real day's weather -- they're a reference standard. When someone says an aeroplane's service ceiling is 14,000 feet, they mean in ISA conditions. Real performance will differ depending on how far the actual conditions deviate from ISA. Learning ISA sea-level values is like learning your times tables -- they're the foundation everything else builds on.
exam_tip: ISA sea level: 1013.25 hPa, 15C, 1.225 kg/m cubed. Lapse rate approximately 2C/1,000 ft. Know these numbers absolutely cold -- they appear in almost every performance question.
key_facts: ISA sea-level pressure: 1013.25 hPa (29.92 inHg)
ISA sea-level temperature: 15C (288.15 K)
ISA sea-level density: 1.225 kg/m cubed
ISA temperature lapse rate: approximately 1.98C per 1,000 ft
ISA is a mathematical model for standardising performance comparisons, not real weather

syllabus_desc: Describe the approximate altitude bands in which atmospheric pressure and density are reduced to 75, 50 and 25% of their normal sea level values.
concept: The atmosphere isn't uniform -- it's layered, and the layer we care about most is the troposphere, where all weather occurs and where most general aviation takes place. The troposphere extends from the surface to about 36,000 feet at mid-latitudes (higher at the equator, lower at the poles). In this layer, pressure drops from 1013 hPa at sea level to roughly 226 hPa at the tropopause. Density follows a similar pattern, dropping from 1.225 kg/m cubed to about 0.365 kg/m cubed. The key takeaway is that by about 20,000 feet, you've lost roughly half the sea-level air density, which means your engine is producing roughly half its sea-level power and your wings need significantly higher TAS to generate the same lift.
exam_tip: By 18,000 ft, pressure is about half sea-level value. The troposphere contains about 75% of the atmosphere's total mass. Performance degrades progressively with altitude.
key_facts: Troposphere: surface to ~36,000 ft (most weather and GA operations occur here)
Pressure drops from 1013 hPa at sea level to ~226 hPa at the tropopause
Density drops from 1.225 kg/m cubed to ~0.365 kg/m cubed at the tropopause
By ~18,000 ft, pressure and density are approximately half their sea-level values
The troposphere contains roughly 75% of the atmosphere's total mass

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of density altitude (DA) and, in broad terms, the effect of pressure, temperature and humidity on DA and thus aerodynamic and engine performance.
concept: Density altitude is the altitude in the ISA that corresponds to the actual air density you're experiencing. It's the single best indicator of how your aeroplane will perform. If you're sitting on the runway at Queenstown (elevation 1,171 ft) on a hot day with low pressure, the air might be as thin as ISA air at 4,000 feet -- so your density altitude is 4,000 ft, and your aeroplane will perform as though it's at 4,000 ft regardless of what the altimeter reads. High density altitude means poor performance: longer take-off runs, reduced climb rates, and lower service ceilings. Density altitude increases with higher elevation, higher temperature, lower pressure, and higher humidity. On a hot Canterbury day with a nor'wester dropping the pressure, density altitude can be surprisingly high even at low-elevation aerodromes.
exam_tip: Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature. Hot, high, and humid = high density altitude = poor performance. This is a cornerstone of performance planning.
key_facts: Density altitude is the ISA altitude corresponding to the actual air density
Higher temperature, lower pressure, and higher humidity all increase density altitude
Density altitude is the best single measure of expected aircraft performance
High density altitude = reduced lift, reduced engine power, longer take-off distance
Calculated from pressure altitude corrected for temperature deviation from ISA

syllabus_desc: Explain the term viscosity, when related to air.
concept: Viscosity is the internal friction or 'stickiness' of a fluid. Even though air seems thin and insubstantial, it does have viscosity -- and it matters for aerodynamics. Air viscosity is why we get a boundary layer on the wing surface: the air molecules immediately next to the skin are effectively stationary (the no-slip condition), and viscosity transmits this friction effect outward through the boundary layer. Higher viscosity means more internal friction. Here's the counterintuitive bit: unlike liquids, air viscosity increases with temperature. Hotter air is more viscous. However, the effect on aircraft performance is relatively small compared to density changes. The Reynolds number, which predicts whether airflow will be laminar or turbulent, is directly related to viscosity -- lower viscosity (or higher speed/larger chord) means higher Reynolds number and a greater tendency toward turbulent flow.
exam_tip: Air viscosity increases with temperature (opposite to liquids). Viscosity creates the boundary layer through the no-slip condition. Reynolds number relates viscosity to flow behaviour.
key_facts: Viscosity is the internal friction or resistance to flow within a fluid
Air viscosity increases with temperature (unlike liquids)
Viscosity causes the boundary layer through the no-slip condition at the surface
Reynolds number = (density x velocity x chord) / viscosity
Higher Reynolds number favours turbulent flow; lower Reynolds number favours laminar flow

syllabus_desc: Describe the terms freestream static pressure, dynamic pressure (including the term ½ρV²) and total (or pitot) pressure.
concept: Freestream static pressure is the ambient air pressure well ahead of the aircraft, unaffected by the aircraft's presence. Dynamic pressure is the additional pressure created by the kinetic energy of the moving airflow relative to the aircraft. It depends on air density and the square of the true airspeed: q = 0.5 x rho x V squared. This is why speed matters so much -- double your speed and you get four times the dynamic pressure. Total pressure (also called stagnation or pitot pressure) is the sum of static and dynamic pressure. Bernoulli's equation states that in a streamline flow, total pressure remains constant: static + dynamic = constant. Where the air speeds up over the wing, dynamic pressure increases and static pressure drops -- and that pressure drop is what generates lift.
exam_tip: Dynamic pressure q = 0.5 x rho x V squared. Total pressure = static + dynamic (Bernoulli). Speed is squared, so it dominates the dynamic pressure equation.
key_facts: Static pressure: ambient pressure of the undisturbed airstream
Dynamic pressure: q = 0.5 x rho x V squared (kinetic energy per unit volume)
Total (pitot/stagnation) pressure = static pressure + dynamic pressure
Bernoulli's principle: total pressure is constant along a streamline
Where airflow accelerates, static pressure decreases and dynamic pressure increases

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of airspeed indication, and indicate the relationship between indicated, calibrated, equivalent and true airspeeds (IAS, CAS, EAS, and TAS).
concept: The airspeed indicator (ASI) works by measuring the difference between total pressure and static pressure. A pitot tube faces into the airflow and captures total pressure (static + dynamic). A static port, flush with the fuselage, senses static pressure alone. The ASI capsule expands or contracts based on the pressure difference -- which is dynamic pressure -- and moves the needle. Since dynamic pressure depends on both density and TAS, the ASI effectively reads what the aeroplane 'feels' in terms of aerodynamic force, regardless of altitude. This is incredibly useful: indicated airspeed (IAS) directly relates to the aerodynamic forces acting on the aircraft. Your stall speed, for instance, occurs at the same IAS regardless of altitude. Calibrated airspeed (CAS) corrects IAS for instrument and position errors. Equivalent airspeed (EAS) corrects for compressibility. TAS corrects for density.
exam_tip: IAS relates to dynamic pressure and aerodynamic forces. Stall speed is the same IAS at any altitude. TAS increases with altitude for the same IAS because density decreases.
key_facts: ASI measures the difference between pitot (total) and static pressure
This pressure difference equals dynamic pressure (q = 0.5 x rho x V squared)
IAS relates directly to the aerodynamic forces on the aircraft
CAS = IAS corrected for instrument and position error
TAS = CAS corrected for density (altitude and temperature); TAS > CAS at altitude

syllabus_desc: With respect to aerofoils, describe the meanings of the following terms: section, leading edge, trailing edge, chord, chord line, thickness, thickness/chord ratio, camber.
concept: An aerofoil cross-section is defined by several key geometric features. The chord line is a straight line from the leading edge to the trailing edge -- it's your primary reference for measuring angles of attack. The mean camber line (or mean line) is the line equidistant between the upper and lower surfaces. Camber is the curvature of the aerofoil, measured as the maximum distance between the chord line and the mean camber line. Thickness is the maximum distance between the upper and lower surfaces. The leading-edge radius describes how blunt or sharp the front of the aerofoil is. Fineness ratio is the ratio of chord to maximum thickness. These dimensions aren't just academic -- they determine how the aerofoil behaves: how much lift it generates, at what angle it stalls, and how much drag it produces.
exam_tip: Chord line = straight line LE to TE. Camber = curvature measured from chord line to mean camber line. A symmetrical aerofoil has zero camber.
key_facts: Chord line: straight line from leading edge to trailing edge
Mean camber line: line equidistant between upper and lower surfaces
Camber: maximum distance between chord line and mean camber line
Thickness: maximum distance between upper and lower surfaces
Leading-edge radius, fineness ratio, and thickness-to-chord ratio further define the shape

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between high-lift, general purpose (GP) and high-speed aerofoil sections.
concept: Aerofoil sections are designed for different jobs. High-lift aerofoils have pronounced camber and relatively thick sections -- they generate lots of lift at low speeds but also produce more drag. They're ideal for slow-speed aircraft, agricultural planes, or STOL operations. General-purpose (GP) aerofoils offer a compromise between lift and drag -- moderate camber and thickness, good all-round performance. Most training and light GA aircraft use GP sections. High-speed aerofoils are thin with little or no camber (often symmetrical or near-symmetrical) -- they produce less lift at low speeds but minimise drag at higher speeds, which is what fast jets and high-performance aircraft need. Laminar flow aerofoils are a special design where the maximum thickness is moved aft to maintain laminar boundary layer flow over a larger portion of the chord, reducing skin friction drag.
exam_tip: High-lift = high camber, high CLmax, lower stalling speed. High-speed = thin, low camber, lower drag at speed. GP = compromise. Laminar flow = max thickness aft, lower drag.
key_facts: High-lift aerofoils: high camber, thick, high CLmax, more drag, for low-speed operations
General-purpose (GP) aerofoils: moderate camber and thickness, good all-round performance
High-speed aerofoils: thin, low/zero camber, low drag at high speed, lower CLmax
Laminar flow aerofoils: maximum thickness positioned aft to maintain laminar flow longer
Symmetrical aerofoils produce zero lift at zero angle of attack

syllabus_desc: Define relative airflow and angle of attack ().
concept: Relative airflow (also called the freestream or relative wind) is the direction of the airflow relative to the aircraft, well ahead of any disturbance caused by the aircraft itself. In still air, it's exactly opposite to the aircraft's flight path direction. If you're flying straight and level, the relative airflow comes from directly ahead. If you're climbing, it comes from slightly below the nose. Angle of attack (alpha) is the angle between the chord line of the wing and the direction of the relative airflow. This is not the same as pitch attitude -- an aeroplane can have a nose-high attitude but a low angle of attack (think of a steep climb at high power), or a level attitude but a high angle of attack (slow flight). Angle of attack is the pilot's primary tool for controlling lift, and every aerofoil stalls at a specific critical angle of attack, regardless of speed, weight, or configuration.
exam_tip: Angle of attack is measured from the relative airflow to the chord line, NOT from the horizon. The stalling angle is constant regardless of weight, speed, or bank -- only the stalling speed changes.
key_facts: Relative airflow: airflow direction relative to aircraft, opposite to flight path in still air
Angle of attack (alpha): angle between chord line and relative airflow
Angle of attack is NOT the same as pitch attitude
Increasing angle of attack increases lift (up to the stalling angle)
The stalling angle of attack is essentially constant for a given aerofoil configuration

syllabus_desc: State Bernoulli’s theorem in simple terms, and describe streamline flow, turbulent flow, and the application of Bernoulli’s theorem to the streamline flow around an aerofoil.
concept: Bernoulli's theorem states that in a steady, incompressible, frictionless airflow along a streamline, the total pressure remains constant. In simple terms: where air speeds up, its static pressure drops, and where it slows down, static pressure rises. Streamline flow (or laminar flow in context of the freestream) is smooth, orderly flow where adjacent layers don't mix. Turbulent flow is chaotic, with eddies and mixing between layers. The transition point is where streamline flow breaks down into turbulent flow. On a wing, air accelerates over the curved upper surface, reducing static pressure above the wing relative to below -- and that pressure difference creates lift. Bernoulli's principle explains how this happens, though the full picture also involves Newton's third law and the downward deflection of airflow.
exam_tip: Bernoulli: static + dynamic pressure = constant along a streamline. Faster flow = lower static pressure. This is the foundation of lift generation on a wing's upper surface.
key_facts: Bernoulli: static pressure + dynamic pressure = constant (along a streamline)
Where airflow accelerates, static pressure decreases
Where airflow decelerates, static pressure increases
Streamline flow: smooth, orderly, layers don't mix
Turbulent flow: chaotic, eddies, mixing between layers; transition point is where laminar becomes turbulent

syllabus_desc: Describe a venturi and explain venturi effect.
concept: A venturi is a tube that narrows in the middle and then expands again. When air flows through the narrow throat, it must speed up (by continuity -- the same mass of air has to pass through a smaller area). By Bernoulli's principle, this increase in speed causes a drop in static pressure at the throat. This is the venturi effect, and it's exactly what happens over the curved upper surface of a wing. The wing's camber acts like one side of a venturi, accelerating the air over the top surface and creating the low-pressure region that produces lift. Venturi tubes are also used in some aircraft instruments -- the attitude indicator and heading indicator in older aircraft are often driven by a venturi-powered vacuum system, where the pressure drop at the venturi throat creates suction to spin the gyroscope.
exam_tip: Venturi effect: constriction speeds up airflow and drops static pressure. This is the same principle that creates low pressure over the wing's upper surface.
key_facts: A venturi is a convergent-divergent tube (narrows then widens)
Continuity: air speeds up through the narrow throat (same mass flow, smaller area)
Bernoulli: increased speed at the throat means decreased static pressure
The wing's upper surface acts like one wall of a venturi
Venturi tubes are used in some aircraft vacuum systems for instrument power

syllabus_desc: Explain the changes to the airflow and pressure distribution around a typical aerofoil in a low- subsonic speed airflow as  is increased from the zero-lift angle to beyond the stalling angle.
concept: As angle of attack increases from zero, the pressure distribution around an aerofoil changes dramatically. The low-pressure area on the upper surface expands and the suction peak near the leading edge intensifies, generating more lift. The stagnation point (where airflow splits between upper and lower surfaces) moves progressively aft and downward from the leading edge. On the lower surface, pressure increases, adding a small contribution to lift. At moderate angles of attack, the flow remains attached and orderly. But as you approach the stalling angle (typically 15-16 degrees for a GP aerofoil), the airflow can no longer follow the increasingly steep upper surface curvature. The boundary layer separates from the surface starting at the trailing edge, the low-pressure area collapses, and lift drops dramatically. That's the stall.
exam_tip: As alpha increases: suction peak grows, stagnation point moves aft, lift increases -- until the critical angle where flow separates and lift drops sharply.
key_facts: Increasing alpha intensifies the suction peak on the upper surface
The stagnation point moves progressively aft and below the leading edge
Upper surface contributes roughly two-thirds of total lift through suction
Lower surface contributes roughly one-third through increased pressure
Beyond the critical angle of attack, flow separation causes the stall

syllabus_desc: Explain the terms upwash and downwash in an airflow.
concept: Upwash is the upward deflection of air ahead of the wing's leading edge. As the wing approaches, air is drawn upward to flow over the top surface. Downwash is the downward deflection of air behind the trailing edge. The wing turns the airflow downward as it generates lift -- Newton's third law in action: the wing pushes air down, and the air pushes the wing up. The amount of downwash increases with angle of attack and lift coefficient. Downwash has important practical effects: it reduces the effective angle of attack of the tailplane (which affects longitudinal stability), it creates induced drag (the rearward tilt of the lift vector), and the downwash field from a large aircraft's wings can be hazardous to smaller aircraft following behind -- that's wake turbulence.
exam_tip: Downwash increases with angle of attack and CL. It reduces the effective alpha at the tailplane and is a key factor in induced drag and wake turbulence.
key_facts: Upwash: airflow deflected upward ahead of the leading edge
Downwash: airflow deflected downward behind the trailing edge
Downwash increases with angle of attack and lift coefficient
Downwash reduces the effective angle of attack at the tailplane
Downwash is directly related to induced drag and wake turbulence

syllabus_desc: Explain the term centre of pressure (CP); and describe typical movement of the CP with increasing angle of attack ().
concept: The centre of pressure (CP) is the single point on the chord line where the total aerodynamic force can be considered to act. Think of it as the 'balance point' of all the pressure forces on the aerofoil. For a cambered aerofoil, the CP moves forward as angle of attack increases and aft as angle of attack decreases. At the stall, the CP typically makes a sudden rearward shift as the pressure distribution collapses. This CP movement is significant for stability -- on a cambered wing without a tailplane, an increase in alpha would move the CP forward of the CG, creating a nose-up pitching moment that increases alpha further, which is unstable. That's one reason we need a tailplane. For a symmetrical aerofoil, the CP stays at approximately the quarter-chord point (25% of chord from the leading edge) regardless of angle of attack.
exam_tip: Cambered aerofoil: CP moves forward with increasing alpha, aft with decreasing alpha. Symmetrical aerofoil: CP stays at ~25% chord. CP movement affects pitch stability.
key_facts: CP is the point where total aerodynamic force is considered to act
Cambered aerofoil: CP moves forward as alpha increases, aft as alpha decreases
At the stall, CP moves sharply rearward
Symmetrical aerofoil: CP remains at approximately 25% chord (quarter-chord point)
CP movement relative to CG determines pitch stability contribution from the wing

syllabus_desc: Define the total aerodynamic reaction force (TR) of an aerofoil;
concept: The total aerodynamic reaction (TR) is the single resultant force produced by the pressure distribution around the aerofoil. Every bit of pressure acting on every square centimetre of the wing surface can be added up (vectorially) into this one force. The TR acts through the centre of pressure and its magnitude, direction, and point of application all change with angle of attack. At low angles of attack, the TR is relatively small and acts nearly perpendicular to the chord. As alpha increases, the TR grows larger and tilts progressively more rearward relative to the freestream direction. The TR is a useful theoretical concept, but in practice we resolve it into two more useful components: lift and drag.
exam_tip: TR is the single resultant of all aerodynamic forces on the aerofoil. It acts through the CP. We resolve TR into lift (perpendicular to relative airflow) and drag (parallel to it).
key_facts: TR is the vector sum of all pressure and friction forces on the aerofoil
TR acts through the centre of pressure
TR magnitude increases with angle of attack (up to the stall)
TR direction tilts rearward as alpha increases
TR is resolved into lift (perpendicular to relative airflow) and drag (parallel to it)

syllabus_desc: Describe how TR varies with increasing angle of attack ().
concept: As angle of attack increases from zero, the total reaction force grows in magnitude and changes direction. At small angles of attack, the TR is modest and acts roughly perpendicular to the relative airflow. As alpha increases toward the stalling angle, the TR continues to grow -- both the lift and drag components increase. However, the growth isn't proportional: lift increases roughly linearly at first, while drag increases more slowly at low alpha but accelerates as alpha gets higher. The TR tilts progressively more rearward (increasing the drag component relative to lift). At the critical angle of attack, the flow separates, the TR suddenly decreases in magnitude and changes direction significantly. Past the stall, the TR has a much larger drag component relative to lift -- the wing has gone from being a lifting surface to something closer to a flat plate being pushed through the air.
exam_tip: TR grows with alpha up to the stall, then drops sharply. The L/D ratio peaks at a moderate alpha and decreases as the TR tilts increasingly rearward approaching the stall.
key_facts: TR magnitude increases with alpha up to the critical (stalling) angle
TR direction tilts increasingly rearward (more drag relative to lift) as alpha grows
At the stall, TR drops sharply and changes direction significantly
The ratio of lift to drag component (L/D) peaks at a moderate alpha
Beyond the stall, the drag component dominates the diminished TR

syllabus_desc: Define the TR components lift and drag.
concept: Lift and drag are the two components we get when we resolve the total reaction force into useful directions. Lift is defined as the component of TR that acts perpendicular to the relative airflow (not perpendicular to the horizon -- perpendicular to the relative airflow). Drag is the component that acts parallel to and opposing the relative airflow. In straight and level flight, lift acts vertically and drag acts horizontally rearward, which keeps things simple. But in a climb, the relative airflow comes from below, so lift tilts slightly backward from vertical and drag tilts slightly downward from horizontal. The distinction between 'perpendicular to relative airflow' and 'perpendicular to horizon' becomes critical when analysing climbing, descending, and turning flight.
exam_tip: Lift is always perpendicular to the relative airflow, not to the horizon. Drag is always parallel to and opposing the relative airflow. Get this definition right -- it's tested frequently.
key_facts: Lift: component of TR perpendicular to the relative airflow
Drag: component of TR parallel to and opposing the relative airflow
In level flight, lift is vertical and drag is horizontal
In a climb or descent, the lift and drag vectors are tilted relative to the horizon
These definitions are critical for resolving forces in non-level flight

syllabus_desc: Identify the factors affecting lift (low-subsonic speed airflow).
concept: Lift depends on several factors, all captured in the lift formula. At low subsonic speeds (where compressibility effects are negligible), the factors are: air density, the square of the airspeed, the wing area, and the coefficient of lift (which itself depends on angle of attack, aerofoil shape, and surface condition). You can't change wing area in flight (ignoring Fowler flaps for the moment), and density is largely dictated by altitude and temperature. That leaves angle of attack and airspeed as the pilot's two main tools for controlling lift. Increase either one and lift goes up. The key insight is that airspeed is squared -- doubling your speed quadruples the available lift, while halving your speed cuts it to a quarter.
exam_tip: The four lift factors are: density (rho), velocity squared (V squared), wing area (S), and CL. Speed is squared, so it has the dominant effect on lift.
key_facts: Lift depends on air density (rho), velocity squared, wing area, and coefficient of lift
Airspeed is squared -- doubling speed gives four times the lift (at the same CL)
CL depends on angle of attack, aerofoil shape, and surface condition
Pilot controls lift primarily through angle of attack and airspeed
At low subsonic speeds, compressibility effects are negligible

syllabus_desc: State the lift formula, and the three basic functions contained within it.
concept: The lift formula is L = CL x 0.5 x rho x V squared x S. Let's break that down into its three basic functions. First, CL (coefficient of lift) captures the aerodynamic efficiency of the wing at a given angle of attack -- it's the 'shape and angle' factor. Second, 0.5 x rho x V squared is the dynamic pressure (q) -- it's the 'energy of the airflow' factor, combining air density and speed into a single pressure value. Third, S is the wing area -- the 'size' factor. So lift equals aerodynamic efficiency times airflow energy times wing size. In level flight, lift must equal weight, so if you increase altitude (reducing rho), you need either more speed or more CL (higher alpha) to compensate.
exam_tip: L = CL x q x S, where q = 0.5 x rho x V squared. The three functions are: CL (aerofoil efficiency), q (dynamic pressure), and S (wing area). Know this formula cold.
key_facts: Lift formula: L = CL x 0.5 x rho x V squared x S
CL = coefficient of lift (aerodynamic shape/angle factor)
q = 0.5 x rho x V squared = dynamic pressure (airflow energy factor)
S = wing area (size factor)
In level flight, L = W, so any change in one variable requires compensation from others

syllabus_desc: Describe the meaning of the term, coefficient of lift (CL).
concept: The coefficient of lift (CL) is a dimensionless number that describes how effectively a wing is producing lift at a given angle of attack. It captures everything about the wing's aerodynamic efficiency that isn't already covered by dynamic pressure and wing area. CL varies primarily with angle of attack -- it increases approximately linearly as alpha increases, reaching a maximum value (CLmax) at the critical angle of attack, then drops sharply at the stall. CL also depends on aerofoil shape, camber, surface condition, Reynolds number, and the use of high-lift devices. A CL of 1.0 doesn't mean the wing is generating one newton of lift -- it means the lift equals the dynamic pressure times the wing area. Typical CLmax values range from about 1.2 to 1.6 for clean wings, and up to 3.0 or more with full flaps and slats.
exam_tip: CL is dimensionless. It increases linearly with alpha until CLmax at the stalling angle. Higher CLmax means a lower stalling speed for a given weight.
key_facts: CL is a dimensionless coefficient representing the wing's lift-producing efficiency
CL increases approximately linearly with angle of attack up to CLmax
CLmax occurs at the critical (stalling) angle of attack
Beyond the stalling angle, CL drops sharply
Typical CLmax: 1.2-1.6 clean, up to 3.0+ with high-lift devices deployed

syllabus_desc: the zero lift angle;
concept: The zero-lift angle is the angle of attack at which the aerofoil produces zero lift -- where the CL versus alpha curve crosses the horizontal axis. For a cambered aerofoil, this is a small negative angle, typically around -2 to -4 degrees. This makes intuitive sense: a cambered wing naturally curves air downward even at zero geometric angle of attack, so you have to pitch it slightly nose-down (negative alpha) to stop it producing any lift at all. For a symmetrical aerofoil, the zero-lift angle is zero degrees -- at zero alpha there's no camber to deflect air asymmetrically, so no lift is generated. The zero-lift angle shifts more negative with increasing camber.
exam_tip: Cambered aerofoil: zero-lift angle is a small negative angle (typically -2 to -4 degrees). Symmetrical aerofoil: zero-lift angle is 0 degrees. More camber = more negative zero-lift angle.
key_facts: Zero-lift angle: the alpha where CL = 0 on the CL vs alpha curve
Cambered aerofoils have a negative zero-lift angle (typically -2 to -4 degrees)
Symmetrical aerofoils have a zero-lift angle of 0 degrees
Greater camber shifts the zero-lift angle more negative
At angles of attack above the zero-lift angle, the aerofoil generates positive lift

syllabus_desc: the angle for maximum CL (CLmax).
concept: CLmax is the maximum value of the coefficient of lift, and it occurs at the critical angle of attack -- the stalling angle. For a typical GP aerofoil, the stalling angle is around 15-16 degrees and CLmax might be about 1.4 to 1.6. The practical significance of CLmax is enormous: it determines the minimum speed at which the wing can generate enough lift to support the aircraft's weight. From the lift formula rearranged, stalling speed is proportional to the square root of (weight / (CLmax x rho x S)). So a higher CLmax means a lower stalling speed, which means shorter take-off and landing distances. That's exactly what high-lift devices are designed to achieve -- they increase CLmax.
exam_tip: CLmax occurs at the stalling angle (~15-16 degrees for GP aerofoils). Higher CLmax = lower stall speed. Stall speed varies as the square root of weight and inversely as the square root of CLmax.
key_facts: CLmax occurs at the critical (stalling) angle of attack
Typical stalling angle for a GP aerofoil: 15-16 degrees
Typical CLmax clean: 1.4-1.6 for GP aerofoils
Higher CLmax results in lower stalling speed
Stall speed = square root of (2W / (rho x S x CLmax))

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of a high CLmax.
concept: A high CLmax means the wing can generate a lot of lift relative to the dynamic pressure and wing area available. In practical terms, a high CLmax gives you a lower stalling speed, which translates directly to shorter take-off and landing distances, better low-speed handling, and a wider speed range. For a given weight, wing area, and air density, the stalling speed is inversely proportional to the square root of CLmax. So if you can increase CLmax by 44% (say from 1.5 to 2.16), you reduce your stalling speed by about 17%. That's exactly what trailing-edge flaps do -- they increase the camber and therefore CLmax. High-lift devices like slats increase the stalling angle, allowing even higher CLmax values to be achieved before the flow separates.
exam_tip: High CLmax = lower stall speed = better short-field performance. The relationship is: Vs varies as 1 / square root of CLmax. A 44% increase in CLmax reduces Vs by about 17%.
key_facts: High CLmax allows the wing to produce more lift at a given dynamic pressure
Higher CLmax directly reduces the stalling speed
Vs = square root of (2W / (rho x S x CLmax))
Flaps increase CLmax by increasing effective camber
Slats increase CLmax by allowing a higher stalling angle before flow separation

syllabus_desc: increased camber;
concept: Increasing camber shifts the entire CL versus alpha curve upward and to the left. This means higher CL values at every angle of attack, a higher CLmax, and the zero-lift angle becomes more negative. The stalling angle itself may decrease slightly, but the net result is still a higher CLmax. This is exactly what happens when you extend trailing-edge flaps -- you're effectively increasing the wing's camber. The practical payoff is clear: more lift at lower speeds, which means lower stalling speed and better short-field performance. It's also why high-lift aerofoils used on STOL aircraft have pronounced camber -- they're designed to maximise CLmax at the expense of higher drag.
exam_tip: Increased camber: CL curve shifts up and left, CLmax increases, zero-lift angle becomes more negative, stalling angle may decrease slightly. Flaps are the most common way to increase camber in flight.
key_facts: Increased camber shifts the CL curve upward and to the left
CLmax increases with greater camber
Zero-lift angle becomes more negative
Stalling angle may decrease slightly despite higher CLmax
Trailing-edge flaps increase effective camber, demonstrating this effect in flight

syllabus_desc: surface roughness (e.g. contamination).
concept: Surface roughness from contamination -- frost, ice, rain, insects, or even dirt -- reduces CLmax and can cause the wing to stall at a lower angle of attack. Roughness disrupts the boundary layer, promoting earlier transition from laminar to turbulent flow and earlier flow separation. The CL curve slope remains roughly the same at low angles of attack, but the peak is lower and the stall occurs sooner. This is why even a thin layer of frost on the wings is dangerous and must be removed before flight. The reduction in CLmax directly increases the stalling speed, and the earlier stall angle gives you less margin between cruise alpha and stall alpha. On a frosty winter morning at Christchurch, clearing your wings isn't optional -- it could easily mean the difference between a normal take-off and not making the end of the runway.
exam_tip: Surface roughness reduces CLmax and lowers the stalling angle. Even thin frost can increase stalling speed by 10-20%. Always de-ice/defrost before flight.
key_facts: Surface roughness reduces CLmax and lowers the stalling angle
The CL curve slope at low alpha is relatively unaffected, but the peak is lower
Contamination promotes early boundary layer transition and flow separation
Even thin frost can significantly increase stalling speed
Roughness from ice, frost, insects, or dirt must be removed before flight

syllabus_desc: Describe three-dimensional flow over a wing and explain how wingtip and trailing edge vortices are formed.
concept: Real wings aren't infinite -- they have tips, and that changes everything. In two-dimensional theory (infinite wing), air flows smoothly over the aerofoil section. But on a real wing, the higher pressure beneath the wing and lower pressure above it create a pressure difference at the wingtips. Air spills from the high-pressure region below around the tip to the low-pressure region above, creating wingtip vortices -- rotating spirals of air that trail behind each wingtip. These vortices induce a downward component of airflow (downwash) across the entire span. Additionally, the spanwise flow along the wing creates trailing-edge vortices along the full span. The result is that the real wing is less efficient than the infinite-wing theory predicts: effective angle of attack is reduced, lift is slightly less, and a new component of drag -- induced drag -- is created.
exam_tip: Wingtip vortices form because of the pressure difference between upper and lower wing surfaces at the tips. They create induced downwash, reducing effective alpha and creating induced drag.
key_facts: Pressure difference between upper and lower surfaces causes air to spill around wingtips
This creates wingtip vortices -- rotating spirals trailing behind each tip
Vortices induce downwash across the entire wingspan
Downwash reduces the effective angle of attack of the wing
Three-dimensional flow effects reduce lift and create induced drag compared to 2D theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of induced downwash on .
concept: The induced downwash from wingtip vortices tilts the effective relative airflow downward at the wing. This means the wing 'sees' a lower effective angle of attack than the geometric angle between the chord line and the freestream. If you set a geometric angle of attack of 8 degrees but the induced downwash tilts the local airflow down by 2 degrees, the effective angle of attack is only 6 degrees. The wing generates lift based on this reduced effective alpha, not the geometric alpha. This is why a finite wing produces less lift than an infinite wing at the same geometric angle of attack. It also tilts the lift vector slightly rearward, creating the rearward component that we call induced drag. The stronger the vortices (higher CL, lower aspect ratio), the greater the induced downwash and the bigger the reduction in effective alpha.
exam_tip: Induced downwash reduces effective alpha below geometric alpha. This means a finite wing needs a higher geometric alpha than a 2D aerofoil to produce the same CL. The effect is greater at high CL and low aspect ratio.
key_facts: Induced downwash tilts the local relative airflow downward at the wing
Effective alpha = geometric alpha minus induced angle of attack
The wing generates lift based on effective alpha, not geometric alpha
Induced downwash tilts the lift vector rearward, creating induced drag
Greater downwash (higher CL, lower AR) means larger reduction in effective alpha

syllabus_desc: Define aspect ratio (AR) and describe the effect of AR on CL.
concept: Aspect ratio (AR) is the ratio of the wingspan to the mean chord, or equivalently, the wingspan squared divided by the wing area (AR = b squared / S). A long, narrow wing (like a glider) has a high aspect ratio; a short, stubby wing (like a fighter jet) has a low aspect ratio. Aspect ratio has a profound effect on the CL versus alpha curve. A high-AR wing produces a steeper lift-curve slope -- it generates more lift per degree of alpha increase -- because the wingtip vortices affect a smaller proportion of the total span, so there's less induced downwash and the effective alpha is closer to the geometric alpha. High AR also means less induced drag at any given CL. Gliders use aspect ratios of 20 or more to maximise efficiency; typical training aircraft are around 7-8; fast jets might be 3-4.
exam_tip: AR = span squared / wing area (or span / mean chord). High AR = steeper CL curve slope, less induced drag, better glide performance. Low AR = shallower CL slope, more induced drag.
key_facts: Aspect ratio = wingspan / mean chord = wingspan squared / wing area
High AR: steeper lift-curve slope, less induced drag, higher L/D ratio
Low AR: shallower lift-curve slope, more induced drag, lower L/D ratio
Gliders: AR 20+; training aircraft: AR 7-8; jets: AR 3-5
High AR reduces the proportion of the wing affected by tip vortices

syllabus_desc: Identify and explain the components of total drag.
concept: Total drag is the sum of all aerodynamic forces opposing the aircraft's motion through the air. At CPL level, we split it into two main categories: parasite drag (also called zero-lift drag or profile drag) and induced drag. Parasite drag exists whenever the aircraft moves through air and includes skin friction drag, form (pressure) drag, and interference drag. It increases with the square of the airspeed. Induced drag is the drag penalty for producing lift -- it's caused by the wingtip vortices tilting the lift vector rearward. Induced drag decreases as speed increases (because you need less angle of attack to support the same weight at higher speed). Total drag has a characteristic U-shaped curve: high at low speed (dominated by induced drag), minimum at a particular speed, and increasing at high speed (dominated by parasite drag).
exam_tip: Total drag = parasite drag + induced drag. Parasite drag increases with V squared. Induced drag decreases with V squared. The speed where they're equal gives minimum total drag.
key_facts: Total drag = parasite drag + induced drag
Parasite drag: skin friction + form drag + interference drag (increases with V squared)
Induced drag: drag due to lift production (decreases with V squared)
Total drag curve is U-shaped with a minimum at a specific speed
At minimum drag speed, parasite drag equals induced drag

syllabus_desc: Explain the term boundary layer.
concept: The boundary layer is the thin layer of air immediately adjacent to the aircraft's surface where the airflow is affected by viscosity. Right at the surface, the air is essentially stationary (the no-slip condition), and the speed increases through the boundary layer until it reaches the freestream velocity at the outer edge. The boundary layer is surprisingly thin -- typically only a few millimetres near the leading edge, growing to perhaps 10-20 mm at the trailing edge. Despite being thin, the boundary layer is critically important. Its behaviour determines skin friction drag, whether the flow separates (causing form drag and stall), and how effective high-lift devices are. The boundary layer can be laminar (smooth, orderly layers) or turbulent (chaotic, mixing), and the transition between them affects both drag and stall characteristics.
exam_tip: The boundary layer is where viscous effects dominate. It starts laminar near the leading edge and transitions to turbulent. Boundary layer separation causes form drag and the stall.
key_facts: Boundary layer: thin layer adjacent to the surface where viscosity slows the airflow
No-slip condition: air velocity is zero at the surface itself
Boundary layer grows in thickness from leading edge toward trailing edge
Boundary layer state (laminar or turbulent) affects drag and flow separation
Boundary layer separation is the mechanism behind form drag and wing stall

syllabus_desc: laminar boundary layer flow;
concept: In a laminar boundary layer, the air flows in smooth, parallel layers that slide over each other without mixing. Think of it like a deck of cards sliding smoothly. Laminar flow produces less skin friction drag because there's minimal energy exchange between layers -- only the molecular viscosity transfers shear stress. However, a laminar boundary layer has a significant weakness: it has less energy near the surface and separates more easily when it encounters an adverse pressure gradient (rising pressure toward the trailing edge). This is why laminar flow aerofoils, despite having lower friction drag, can stall more abruptly than conventional sections. The laminar boundary layer typically exists near the leading edge where the flow is accelerating and the pressure gradient is favourable.
exam_tip: Laminar BL: smooth layers, less skin friction drag, but separates more easily under adverse pressure gradients. Found near the leading edge.
key_facts: Laminar boundary layer: smooth, parallel layers with no mixing
Produces less skin friction drag than turbulent boundary layer
Less energy near the surface, so separates more easily in adverse pressure gradients
Typically found near the leading edge where pressure gradient is favourable
Laminar flow aerofoils try to maintain laminar BL over more of the chord

syllabus_desc: turbulent boundary layer flow;
concept: In a turbulent boundary layer, the air flows chaotically with mixing and eddies between layers. This mixing brings high-energy air from the outer boundary layer down toward the surface, giving the flow more momentum near the skin. The result is more skin friction drag (the mixing wastes energy), but the turbulent boundary layer is much more resistant to separation. It can 'stick' to the surface through much stronger adverse pressure gradients before detaching. This is why vortex generators work -- they deliberately trip the boundary layer to turbulent, increasing friction drag slightly but preventing the much more costly flow separation further downstream. The turbulent boundary layer is thicker than the laminar boundary layer and grows faster along the surface.
exam_tip: Turbulent BL: chaotic mixing, more skin friction drag, but much more resistant to separation. This is why golf balls have dimples -- the turbulent BL delays separation and reduces form drag.
key_facts: Turbulent boundary layer: chaotic flow with mixing between layers
Produces more skin friction drag due to energy-wasting eddies
Brings high-energy air close to the surface, resisting flow separation
Thicker than the laminar boundary layer
Vortex generators deliberately trigger turbulent BL to prevent separation

syllabus_desc: transition point.
concept: The transition point is where the boundary layer changes from laminar to turbulent. On a typical wing, the boundary layer starts laminar at the leading edge, and at some point along the chord it transitions to turbulent. The transition point location depends on the Reynolds number, surface roughness, pressure gradient, and freestream turbulence. Higher speeds, rougher surfaces, and adverse pressure gradients all move the transition point forward (closer to the leading edge), increasing the proportion of turbulent flow and therefore skin friction drag. Laminar flow aerofoils are designed with their maximum thickness further aft, creating a favourable (accelerating) pressure gradient over a greater proportion of the chord, which delays transition and maintains lower-drag laminar flow over more of the surface.
exam_tip: Transition point moves forward with increased speed, roughness, or adverse pressure gradient. Laminar flow aerofoils delay transition by keeping a favourable pressure gradient longer.
key_facts: Transition point: where boundary layer changes from laminar to turbulent
Moves forward (toward LE) with higher speed, rougher surface, adverse pressure gradient
Moves aft (toward TE) with smoother surfaces and favourable pressure gradients
Laminar flow aerofoils delay transition by positioning max thickness aft
Surface contamination (insects, rain, ice) moves transition forward, increasing drag

syllabus_desc: Explain skin-friction drag and state the factors affecting it.
concept: Skin friction drag is caused by the shearing forces between the air and the aircraft surface within the boundary layer. It's the viscous drag of the air 'rubbing' against the skin. The factors affecting it are: wetted area (more surface exposed to the airflow means more friction), surface roughness (rougher surfaces create more friction and promote earlier transition to turbulent flow), airspeed (friction increases with speed), air density, and the proportion of laminar versus turbulent boundary layer. Turbulent boundary layers produce significantly more skin friction than laminar ones -- roughly 3 to 5 times more for the same conditions. This is why aircraft manufacturers invest heavily in smooth finishes, flush riveting, and surface treatments to keep the boundary layer laminar as long as possible.
exam_tip: Skin friction drag depends on wetted area, surface roughness, proportion of laminar/turbulent BL, speed, and density. Turbulent BL has roughly 3-5x more friction drag than laminar.
key_facts: Caused by viscous shearing between air and the aircraft surface
Increases with wetted area, surface roughness, airspeed, and air density
Turbulent boundary layer produces 3-5x more skin friction than laminar
Reduced by smooth surfaces, flush riveting, and laminar flow aerofoil design
Affected by Reynolds number (higher Re promotes turbulent flow and more friction)

syllabus_desc: Explain form drag and state the factors affecting it.
concept: Form drag (also called pressure drag) results from the pressure difference between the front and rear of a body moving through air. The ideal scenario (zero form drag) would require the airflow to close in perfectly behind the object, recovering all the pressure lost at the front. In reality, boundary layer separation prevents this. Where the flow separates, a turbulent wake forms with lower-than-freestream pressure. The result is higher pressure on the front pushing the body rearward and lower pressure on the rear failing to push it forward -- that's form drag. The factors affecting form drag are: the shape of the body (blunt shapes cause more separation), the amount and location of boundary layer separation, and the size of the wake. The more streamlined the shape, the smaller the wake and the less form drag.
exam_tip: Form drag is caused by the pressure difference between front and rear due to flow separation. Streamlining reduces form drag by delaying separation and reducing wake size.
key_facts: Caused by pressure difference between front and rear of a body
Flow separation creates a turbulent, low-pressure wake behind the body
Affected by body shape, boundary layer separation point, and wake size
Blunt bodies have much more form drag than streamlined shapes
Reducing form drag is the primary purpose of streamlining

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of streamlining in reducing form drag.
concept: Streamlining is the practice of shaping a body so that the airflow can follow its contours without separating. A streamlined shape has a smooth, gradual taper toward the rear, allowing the boundary layer to maintain contact with the surface much further aft. This dramatically reduces the size of the wake and the pressure differential between front and rear. A flat plate perpendicular to the airflow produces enormous form drag; the same plate edge-on produces almost none. A sphere has about half the form drag of the flat plate. But a properly streamlined teardrop shape has only about 5% of the flat plate's form drag -- that's the power of streamlining. On an aircraft, struts, undercarriage legs, antennas, and other protrusions are streamlined (or faired) wherever possible to reduce form drag.
exam_tip: Streamlining can reduce form drag by 95% compared to a flat plate of the same frontal area. The key is gradual tapering to prevent flow separation.
key_facts: Streamlining shapes the body to delay or prevent boundary layer separation
A streamlined shape can have as little as 5% of the drag of an equivalent flat plate
The key is a gradual rear taper that allows the boundary layer to stay attached
Fairings over struts, wheels, and antennas are practical examples of streamlining
Streamlining primarily reduces form drag while slightly increasing skin friction drag (more wetted area)

syllabus_desc: Describe interference drag and the measures for reducing it.
concept: Interference drag occurs where two surfaces meet at an angle -- typically the wing-fuselage junction, the tailplane-fuselage junction, or where engine nacelles attach to the wing. At these junctions, the boundary layers from both surfaces merge and interact, creating local turbulence and flow separation that wouldn't exist on either surface alone. The airflow has to negotiate conflicting pressure gradients from adjacent surfaces, which accelerates boundary layer thickening and separation. The result is drag greater than the sum of the individual components' drag. Interference drag is reduced by using fillets (smooth fairings) at junctions, designing appropriate angles between intersecting surfaces, and in some cases using wing-body blending where the wing and fuselage merge gradually rather than meeting at a sharp angle.
exam_tip: Interference drag is extra drag at junctions of intersecting surfaces. It's reduced by fillets and fairings. Total aircraft drag > sum of individual component drag due to interference.
key_facts: Occurs at junctions where two surfaces meet (wing-fuselage, nacelle-wing, etc.)
Caused by merging boundary layers and conflicting pressure gradients
Produces more drag than the sum of the individual components in isolation
Reduced by fillets, fairings, and wing-body blending
Worst at near-perpendicular junctions; better at gradual, faired transitions

syllabus_desc: list the factors affecting it;
concept: The factors affecting induced drag are: CL (or equivalently, angle of attack), aspect ratio, wing planform, and wingspan. Induced drag increases with CL squared -- so doubling CL gives four times the induced drag. Higher weight increases induced drag because you need more CL at any given speed. Higher aspect ratio reduces induced drag because the vortices affect a smaller proportion of the total span. Elliptical wing planforms are theoretically the most efficient (spanwise lift distribution minimises induced drag), but tapered wings with the right taper ratio come very close. Induced drag is proportional to 1/V squared in level flight -- as you fly faster, the required CL drops and so does induced drag.
