Wingspan matters too: a longer span reduces induced drag for a given wing area.
exam_tip: Induced drag varies with: CL squared, 1/AR, weight squared, and 1/V squared. High AR, long span, and elliptical planform all reduce induced drag. Weight has a squared effect.
key_facts: Increases with CL squared (doubling CL quadruples induced drag)
Decreases with higher aspect ratio (AR)
Increases with weight squared (heavier = more CL needed = more induced drag)
Decreases with speed squared (faster = less CL = less induced drag)
Elliptical planform gives minimum induced drag for a given span and lift

syllabus_desc: explain typical measures for reducing it.
concept: There are several design strategies for reducing induced drag, and they all work by weakening the wingtip vortices or spreading the lift more efficiently along the span. High aspect ratio is the most effective -- long, narrow wings have proportionally less tip effect. Winglets (vertical or near-vertical surfaces at the wingtips) work by blocking the tip spillover and recovering some of the vortex energy as forward thrust. They're essentially small wings at the tips that generate a forward-acting aerodynamic force. Elliptical or near-elliptical spanwise lift distribution (achieved through planform shape or twist) minimises induced drag for a given span and lift. Wing end plates serve a similar function to winglets but are less efficient. Washout (wing twist where the tip is at a lower angle of incidence than the root) reduces tip CL and therefore tip vortex strength.
exam_tip: High AR is the most effective way to reduce induced drag. Winglets recover vortex energy. Elliptical loading is the theoretical ideal. Washout reduces tip vortex strength.
key_facts: High aspect ratio: most effective method -- less of the wing affected by tip effects
Winglets: block tip spillover and recover vortex energy as forward force
Elliptical spanwise lift distribution: theoretical minimum induced drag for given span
Washout (tip set at lower incidence than root): reduces tip CL and vortex strength
Taper ratio and planform shape influence how closely real loading matches elliptical ideal

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of the term coefficient of drag (CD); and describe the main features of a typical curve of CD versus .
concept: The coefficient of drag (CD) is a dimensionless number that captures the total drag characteristics of the aircraft or wing at a given angle of attack. Just as CL captures the lift efficiency, CD captures the drag penalty. The CD versus alpha curve rises gradually from a minimum value at a low alpha, then increases more steeply as alpha increases -- accelerating sharply near and beyond the stall as flow separation dramatically increases form drag. The curve is not symmetric: at very low (negative) angles of attack, CD can also be relatively high. The main features of the CD curve are: a minimum value (CDmin, which is roughly the parasite drag coefficient at zero lift), the gradual increase through the normal flight range, and the steep rise near the stalling angle.
exam_tip: CD increases gradually with alpha at low angles, then steeply approaching the stall. CDmin occurs near zero-lift alpha. The shape is roughly parabolic: CD = CD0 + kCL squared.
key_facts: CD is a dimensionless coefficient representing total drag characteristics
CD vs alpha: minimum at low alpha, gradual increase, steep rise near stall
CDmin (minimum drag coefficient) occurs near the zero-lift angle of attack
CD = CD0 + CDi, where CD0 is parasite drag coefficient and CDi is induced drag coefficient
The drag polar (CD vs CL) is roughly parabolic: CD = CD0 + kCL squared

syllabus_desc: State the drag formula, and the three basic functions contained within it.
concept: The drag formula mirrors the lift formula: D = CD x 0.5 x rho x V squared x S. The three basic functions are the same structure: CD (the aerodynamic drag characteristic at the current alpha), dynamic pressure q = 0.5 x rho x V squared (the energy of the airflow), and S (wing area). In straight and level flight where lift equals weight, you can substitute to show that for a given weight and altitude, drag depends on where you are on the drag curve -- which is ultimately controlled by your angle of attack and speed. The minimum drag condition occurs at a specific CL (and therefore a specific speed for each weight), which corresponds to the best lift-to-drag ratio. This speed is fundamental to range performance.
exam_tip: D = CD x 0.5 x rho x V squared x S. Same three components as the lift formula but with CD instead of CL. Minimum drag speed gives maximum range for a jet (and approximately for a piston).
key_facts: Drag formula: D = CD x 0.5 x rho x V squared x S
Three functions: CD (drag coefficient), q (dynamic pressure), S (wing area)
CD includes both parasite and induced drag contributions
At a given weight, minimum drag occurs at a specific speed (VMD)
VMD corresponds to the speed for best L/D ratio and is key for range calculations

syllabus_desc: Describe typical curves of induced drag, all other drag, and total drag versus IAS in straight and level flight.
concept: When you plot induced drag, parasite drag (all other drag), and total drag against IAS, you get the classic drag curves. Parasite drag starts near zero at low speed and increases parabolically with V squared -- it's a steeply rising curve. Induced drag starts high at low speed (where CL is high) and decreases with V squared -- it's a steeply falling curve. Total drag is the sum of these two and forms a U-shaped curve. The bottom of the U is the minimum drag speed (VMD). Below VMD, induced drag dominates (the 'back of the drag curve' or 'region of reversed command'). Above VMD, parasite drag dominates. At VMD, induced drag equals parasite drag. This graph is the foundation for understanding range, endurance, climb, and glide performance.
exam_tip: At VMD: induced drag = parasite drag = half of total drag. Below VMD, slowing down increases total drag (reversed command). The total drag curve is U-shaped.
key_facts: Parasite drag curve: starts low, increases with V squared (parabolic rise)
Induced drag curve: starts high at low speed, decreases with V squared
Total drag curve: U-shaped, sum of parasite and induced
Minimum drag speed (VMD): where induced drag = parasite drag
Below VMD is the region of reversed command (speed unstable)

syllabus_desc: From information provided or a diagram identify the speed for minimum drag and maximum lift/drag ratio.
concept: From a total drag versus IAS graph, or from provided information, you need to be able to identify two key speeds. The minimum drag speed (VMD) is at the bottom of the total drag curve -- this is the speed where total drag is least and the L/D ratio is at its best. Flying at VMD gives maximum range in a glide and approximately maximum range in powered flight for piston aircraft. The minimum power speed (VMP) is slower than VMD and corresponds to the bottom of the power required curve (Power = Drag x TAS). VMP gives maximum endurance and maximum rate of climb. VMD is always faster than VMP. Being able to identify these speeds from a graph and understanding what each one optimises is fundamental to performance planning.
exam_tip: VMD is at the bottom of the drag curve (best L/D, best range). VMP is slower than VMD and is at the bottom of the power required curve (best endurance, best ROC). VMD is always faster than VMP.
key_facts: VMD: minimum drag speed, bottom of the drag curve, best L/D ratio
VMP: minimum power speed, bottom of the power required curve
VMP is always slower than VMD
VMD relates to best range; VMP relates to best endurance and best rate of climb
Both speeds increase with increased weight (proportional to square root of weight ratio)

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between graphs for CD and total drag.
concept: There's an important distinction between a graph of CD (drag coefficient) versus CL and a graph of total drag force versus IAS. The CD versus CL graph (the drag polar) shows the aerodynamic characteristics of the wing independent of speed, weight, or altitude -- it's about the shape's efficiency. The total drag versus IAS graph shows the actual drag force the aircraft experiences at different speeds for a given weight and altitude. The shapes look similar (both roughly parabolic) but they represent different things. On the drag polar, the tangent line from the origin to the curve gives the best L/D ratio. On the total drag versus IAS graph, the minimum point gives VMD. Changing weight or altitude shifts the drag versus IAS curve but doesn't change the drag polar.
exam_tip: CD vs CL (drag polar) is a property of the aerofoil/wing -- it doesn't change with weight or altitude. Total drag vs IAS changes with weight and altitude. Don't confuse them.
key_facts: CD vs CL (drag polar): aerodynamic property of the wing, independent of weight/altitude
Total drag vs IAS: actual drag force, changes with weight and altitude
On the drag polar, tangent from origin gives best L/D ratio
On total drag vs IAS, minimum point gives VMD
Increased weight shifts the drag vs IAS curve upward and to the right

syllabus_desc: Explain a typical graph for lift/drag (L/D) ratio versus .
concept: The L/D ratio versus alpha graph peaks at a moderate angle of attack -- typically around 4 degrees for a GP aerofoil. At this angle, you're getting the most lift for the least drag. Below this angle, both L and D are low but L drops faster; above it, D increases faster than L. The peak L/D ratio is also called the best lift-to-drag ratio and occurs at VMD in level flight. Typical L/D ratios for light training aircraft are around 8-10:1 clean, meaning for every 1 unit of drag, the wing produces 8-10 units of lift. Gliders achieve 30-60:1. The L/D ratio determines glide angle (higher L/D = shallower glide = more range from a given height) and is a key performance parameter. As alpha increases beyond the peak L/D, the ratio drops steadily, and it falls sharply through the stall.
exam_tip: Best L/D ratio occurs at a moderate alpha (about 4 degrees) and corresponds to VMD in level flight. L/D determines glide angle: tan(glide angle) = 1 / (L/D). Higher L/D = flatter glide.
key_facts: L/D ratio peaks at a moderate alpha (~4 degrees for GP aerofoils)
At peak L/D: minimum drag speed (VMD) in level flight
Typical L/D ratios: training aircraft 8-10:1, gliders 30-60:1
Glide angle = arctan(1 / L/D); higher L/D = shallower glide angle
L/D ratio decreases above and below the optimum alpha

syllabus_desc: Explain from a typical graph the most efficient angle of attack, the zero lift position, and the stalling angle.
concept: From a typical graph showing L/D versus alpha, you can identify several critical points. The most efficient angle of attack is where L/D peaks -- this is the alpha for VMD and best range. The zero-lift point is where the curve meets the x-axis at a small negative alpha (for cambered aerofoils) -- no lift means L/D is zero regardless of drag. The stalling angle is where the curve drops steeply as CL collapses. Between the zero-lift angle and the most efficient angle, L/D climbs as lift increases faster than drag. Between the most efficient angle and the stall, L/D declines as drag increases faster than lift. Past the stall, L/D plummets. Understanding this graph tells you exactly where in the angle of attack range your wing is operating most efficiently.
exam_tip: Most efficient alpha (best L/D) is well below the stalling angle. Zero-lift alpha gives L/D = 0. Stalling alpha gives a sharp drop in L/D. Know how to identify all three on a graph.
key_facts: Most efficient alpha: where L/D ratio is maximum (~4 degrees for GP aerofoils)
Zero-lift point: small negative alpha where CL = 0 and L/D = 0
Stalling angle: L/D drops sharply as flow separates
Below best L/D alpha: lift increasing faster than drag
Above best L/D alpha: drag increasing faster than lift

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic purpose of lift augmentation devices.
concept: Lift augmentation devices exist for one simple reason: a clean wing optimised for cruise can't produce enough lift at low speeds for safe take-off and landing without unacceptably long runways. High-lift devices let you temporarily modify the wing to produce more lift at low speeds, then retract them for efficient cruise. They work by increasing the effective camber, increasing the wing area, energising the boundary layer, or some combination of all three. Without high-lift devices, your stalling speed would be much higher, and you'd need to approach at much higher speeds -- which means longer landing distances and higher-speed rejected take-offs. Think of them as the 'low gear' for your wing.
exam_tip: High-lift devices increase CLmax, reducing stall speed and allowing shorter take-off/landing distances. They're retracted for cruise to reduce drag.
key_facts: Purpose: reduce stalling speed for safe take-off and landing
Methods: increase camber, increase wing area, energise boundary layer
Include trailing-edge flaps, leading-edge flaps, slats, and slots
Retracted for cruise to minimise drag
Allow the wing to be optimised for cruise speed while still handling low-speed operations

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principles of trailing and leading-edge flaps.
concept: Trailing-edge flaps are hinged surfaces on the rear portion of the wing that deflect downward. When lowered, they increase the effective camber of the wing and (in some types) the wing area. This shifts the CL curve upward and to the left, giving a higher CLmax at a slightly lower stalling angle. The net result is more lift at any given angle of attack and a lower stalling speed. Leading-edge flaps (such as Krueger flaps or droop nose) work differently -- they extend forward and down from the leading edge, effectively increasing the wing's leading-edge radius and camber. Leading-edge devices primarily delay leading-edge flow separation, allowing the wing to reach a higher angle of attack before stalling. Both types increase CLmax, but trailing-edge devices do it mainly by increasing camber while leading-edge devices do it mainly by increasing the stalling angle.
exam_tip: Trailing-edge flaps: increase camber, increase CLmax, may decrease stalling angle slightly. Leading-edge devices: increase stalling angle, delay LE separation, increase CLmax.
key_facts: Trailing-edge flaps: deflect downward, increase camber, increase CLmax
Leading-edge flaps: extend forward/down, increase LE radius, delay LE separation
Trailing-edge flaps decrease the stalling angle slightly while increasing CLmax
Leading-edge devices increase the stalling angle of attack
Both reduce stalling speed by increasing CLmax

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of lowering trailing edge flap on; CL, CD, L/D ratio, CP movement, angle-of-attack and nose attitude.
concept: Lowering trailing-edge flap has several interconnected effects. CL increases at every angle of attack (the whole CL curve shifts up), giving a higher CLmax but at a slightly lower stalling angle. CD increases significantly -- flaps are draggy, especially at large deflections. The L/D ratio decreases because the drag increase outweighs the lift increase. The CP moves rearward because the increased camber at the trailing edge shifts the pressure distribution aft, creating a nose-down pitching moment. The stalling angle of attack decreases slightly because the changed pressure distribution promotes earlier trailing-edge separation. The stalling speed decreases because CLmax is higher. For take-off, small flap settings give a good CL boost with modest drag penalty. For landing, larger settings give maximum drag (welcome for slowing down) and the lowest stalling speed.
exam_tip: TE flap effects: CL up, CD up significantly, L/D down, CP moves aft, nose-down pitch, stalling angle decreases, stalling speed decreases. More flap = more drag = steeper approach angle.
key_facts: CL increases at all angles of attack (CL curve shifts up)
CD increases significantly, especially at large deflections
L/D ratio decreases (drag increase proportionally greater than lift increase)
CP moves rearward, creating a nose-down pitching moment
Stalling angle decreases slightly; stalling speed decreases

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between the effects of lowering leading-edge flap on angle-of-attack, nose attitude and movement of the CP with those of trailing-edge flap.
concept: Leading-edge flaps have a fundamentally different effect on the CL curve compared to trailing-edge flaps. Where trailing-edge flaps shift the whole curve upward, leading-edge flaps primarily extend the curve -- they allow the wing to reach a higher angle of attack before stalling, effectively stretching the linear portion of the CL curve further before it breaks. The stalling angle increases rather than decreases. CLmax still increases, but the mechanism is different: instead of adding camber at the trailing edge, leading-edge devices prevent the leading-edge flow separation that would otherwise cause the stall. Leading-edge Krueger flaps also add some camber and area, but the dominant effect is the higher stalling angle. The zero-lift angle of attack shifts more negative with leading-edge flaps that add camber.
exam_tip: TE flaps: CL curve shifts UP, stalling angle DECREASES. LE flaps: CL curve EXTENDS to higher alpha, stalling angle INCREASES. This is a classic exam distinction.
key_facts: Leading-edge flaps extend the CL curve to higher angles of attack
Stalling angle increases (opposite to trailing-edge flap effect)
CLmax increases through delayed leading-edge separation
The CL curve slope in the linear region is largely unchanged
Combined with TE flaps, LE devices give the highest possible CLmax

syllabus_desc: Given a description or diagram identify the main types of trailing-edge flap and compare their relative performance (in generating lift and drag).
concept: There are several types of trailing-edge flap, each progressively more effective (and complex). The plain flap is the simplest -- the trailing edge hinges downward, increasing camber but not area. It gives modest CLmax improvement. The split flap has only the lower surface deflecting down, creating a bigger wake and more drag -- useful for landing but draggy. The slotted flap has a gap (slot) between the wing and the flap that channels high-energy air from below the wing to the upper surface of the flap, energising the boundary layer and delaying separation on the flap. This gives a significantly higher CLmax. The Fowler flap moves aft and then rotates down -- it increases both camber and wing area, and usually incorporates a slot. Fowler flaps give the highest CLmax increase of all trailing-edge types. Double and triple slotted Fowler flaps are used on transport aircraft for maximum low-speed performance.
exam_tip: From least to most effective: plain, split, slotted, Fowler. The Fowler flap increases area AND camber AND has a slot. Know the distinguishing feature of each type.
key_facts: Plain flap: simple hinge, increases camber only, modest CLmax improvement
Split flap: lower surface only deflects, high drag, used mainly for landing
Slotted flap: gap channels high-energy air over flap surface, better CLmax
Fowler flap: moves aft (increases area) and rotates down (increases camber), usually slotted
Effectiveness order: plain < split < slotted < Fowler

syllabus_desc: Given a description or diagram identify the main types of leading-edge flap.
concept: Leading-edge flap types include Krueger flaps and drooped leading edges. A Krueger flap is a flat panel that folds out from the lower surface of the wing's leading edge, extending forward and slightly downward. It increases the effective camber and leading-edge radius, delaying leading-edge flow separation and allowing a higher stalling angle. It adds some wing area too. A drooped (or hinged) leading edge tilts the entire leading-edge section downward, increasing camber and keeping the flow attached at higher angles of attack. Both devices are primarily used on swept-wing transport aircraft where the leading-edge separation tendency is more pronounced. They're simpler than slats but less effective at delaying the stall.
exam_tip: Krueger flap: folds out from lower LE surface, increases camber and LE radius. Drooped LE: entire leading edge pivots down. Both increase stalling angle and CLmax.
key_facts: Krueger flap: panel that folds out from lower leading-edge surface
Drooped leading edge: entire LE section hinges downward
Both increase effective camber and leading-edge radius
Both delay leading-edge flow separation, increasing the stalling angle
Simpler than slats but less effective at boundary layer energisation

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principles of slats and slots.
concept: Slats are small, curved aerofoil sections that sit ahead of the wing's leading edge. When deployed (either automatically by aerodynamic forces or by mechanical actuation), they open a slot between the slat and the main wing. This slot channels high-energy air from below the wing over the upper surface, energising the boundary layer and dramatically delaying flow separation. The result is a significant increase in the stalling angle of attack and therefore CLmax. Fixed slots achieve a similar effect without a moving part -- they're permanent gaps in the leading edge that channel air to the upper surface at all times, but they add drag at cruise. Slats are retractable, so they give the boundary layer energisation benefit when needed for low-speed operations but stow away for cruise to avoid the drag penalty.
exam_tip: Slats + slots energise the upper-surface boundary layer, delaying separation and increasing the stalling angle. They're the most effective leading-edge device for increasing CLmax.
key_facts: Slats: small aerofoil sections ahead of the leading edge, retractable
When deployed, they open a slot channelling high-energy air over the upper surface
Energise the boundary layer, dramatically delaying flow separation
Significantly increase stalling angle and CLmax
Fixed slots achieve similar effect but add cruise drag; retractable slats avoid this

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of operating leading-edge slats on CL, stalling angle and nose attitude.
concept: Deploying leading-edge slats has three key effects on the CL curve. First, CLmax increases significantly -- often by 40-50% or more when combined with trailing-edge flaps. Second, the stalling angle of attack increases substantially because the slot energises the boundary layer, keeping the flow attached to much higher angles. Third, the nose-down pitching moment associated with trailing-edge flaps is partially offset, because the increased lift at the leading edge creates a nose-up moment. The CL curve extends further to the right (higher alpha) before breaking. Slats combined with slotted Fowler flaps give the highest CLmax values achievable -- transport aircraft routinely achieve CLmax values of 2.5-3.5 with full slats and flaps, allowing approach speeds that would be impossible with a clean wing.
exam_tip: Slats: increase CLmax (up to 40-50%), increase stalling angle significantly, partially offset TE flap nose-down pitch. Combined slats + Fowler flaps give CLmax of 2.5-3.5.
key_facts: CLmax increases significantly (40-50%+ combined with TE flaps)
Stalling angle increases substantially due to boundary layer energisation
Nose-down pitching moment from TE flaps is partially offset
CL curve extends to higher alpha before the stall break
Combined slats + Fowler flaps: CLmax 2.5-3.5 on transport aircraft

syllabus_desc: Show understanding of the theory of spoilers and give examples of their use.
concept: Spoilers are flat panels on the upper surface of the wing that deploy upward into the airflow. Unlike flaps which add lift, spoilers destroy it -- they disrupt the airflow over the wing, causing flow separation, reducing lift, and increasing drag. Spoilers have several uses. As ground spoilers (lift dumpers), they deploy immediately after touchdown to kill the remaining lift and put the aircraft's weight firmly on the wheels for effective braking. As flight spoilers (speed brakes), they increase drag to allow a steeper descent without increasing speed -- useful for descending into an aerodrome without getting too fast. As roll control devices, differential spoilers (one side only) can be used instead of or in conjunction with ailerons to produce a rolling moment. Spoilers on the descending wing disrupt lift on that side, assisting the roll.
exam_tip: Spoilers disrupt lift and increase drag. Three uses: ground spoilers (dump lift for braking), flight spoilers (increase drag for descent), roll spoilers (differential for roll control).
key_facts: Spoilers are panels on the upper wing surface that deploy upward
They disrupt airflow, reduce lift, and increase drag
Ground spoilers: dump lift after landing for effective wheel braking
Flight spoilers (speed brakes): increase drag for steeper descent without speed increase
Roll spoilers: deployed differentially for roll control, often used with ailerons

syllabus_desc: Identify the three aircraft axes, movement about those axes, and primary flight controls.
concept: An aeroplane has three axes of rotation, all passing through the centre of gravity. The lateral axis runs from wingtip to wingtip -- pitch movement occurs about this axis (nose up/down). The longitudinal axis runs from nose to tail -- roll movement occurs about this axis (wings banking). The normal (vertical) axis runs vertically through the CG -- yaw movement occurs about this axis (nose left/right). Each axis has a primary flight control: the elevator controls pitch, the ailerons control roll, and the rudder controls yaw. All three axes intersect at the CG, so all rotational motion in flight pivots around the CG. There's no other type of rotational motion possible -- even the most complex aerobatic manoeuvre is just combinations of pitch, roll, and yaw.
exam_tip: Lateral axis = pitch (elevator). Longitudinal axis = roll (ailerons). Normal axis = yaw (rudder). All three axes pass through the CG.
key_facts: Lateral axis: wingtip to wingtip; pitch movement; controlled by elevator
Longitudinal axis: nose to tail; roll movement; controlled by ailerons
Normal (vertical) axis: top to bottom; yaw movement; controlled by rudder
All three axes pass through the centre of gravity
All aircraft motion is some combination of pitch, roll, and yaw

syllabus_desc: Explain how control in pitch, roll, and yaw is achieved.
concept: Pitch control is achieved by the elevator (or stabilator on some designs). Deflecting the elevator changes the camber and angle of attack of the horizontal stabiliser, producing an aerodynamic force that pitches the nose up or down about the lateral axis. Roll control comes from the ailerons -- when one goes up and the other goes down, they create differential lift between the two wings. The wing with the lowered aileron generates more lift and rises; the wing with the raised aileron generates less lift and drops. This creates a rolling moment about the longitudinal axis. Yaw control comes from the rudder deflecting left or right on the fin, producing a sideways aerodynamic force on the tail that swings the nose in the desired direction about the normal axis.
exam_tip: Elevator: pitch. Ailerons: roll (differential lift). Rudder: yaw. Each control changes the effective camber/alpha of its respective surface.
key_facts: Pitch: elevator deflection changes tail lift, pivoting aircraft about the lateral axis
Roll: differential aileron creates unequal lift between wings
Yaw: rudder deflection creates sideways force on the tail
All controls work by changing the effective camber/angle of attack of their surface
Control effectiveness depends on airspeed (dynamic pressure) and control deflection

syllabus_desc: the secondary effect of aileron;
concept: When you deflect the ailerons to roll the aeroplane, the resulting yaw about the normal axis is the secondary effect. Once the aeroplane is banked, the horizontal component of lift pulls the nose toward the lower wing, causing the aeroplane to yaw in the direction of the bank. Additionally, the increased lift on the rising wing and decreased lift on the dropping wing create a net sideways component that encourages yaw. This is why a banked aeroplane tends to turn -- the secondary yaw effect of aileron creates a turning tendency. In a correctly banked turn, this is exactly what you want. But the initial yaw that occurs while the bank is being established is adverse yaw (discussed next), which needs to be countered with rudder.
exam_tip: Secondary effect of aileron is yaw toward the direction of bank. This is separate from adverse yaw (which occurs during aileron input). The secondary effect is what makes banked flight produce a turn.
key_facts: Secondary effect of aileron is yaw toward the low wing (direction of bank)
Caused by the horizontal component of lift in a banked attitude
This is what makes a banked aeroplane turn
Different from adverse yaw, which is an initial yaw opposite to the roll
The secondary effect sustains the turn once bank is established

syllabus_desc: adverse yaw and methods used to counteract it;
concept: Adverse yaw is the initial yaw in the opposite direction to the intended turn when ailerons are applied. It happens because the downgoing aileron (on the wing that's rising) increases both lift and drag on that wing, while the upgoing aileron decreases both. The extra drag on the rising wing pulls the nose away from the intended direction of turn. This is the opposite of what you want -- you're trying to turn left but the nose initially swings right. The solution is coordinated rudder: apply rudder in the same direction as the aileron input to counteract the adverse yaw. Design methods to reduce adverse yaw include differential ailerons (upgoing aileron deflects more than downgoing), Frise ailerons (the leading edge of the upgoing aileron protrudes below the wing, creating compensating drag), and spoiler-aileron systems.
exam_tip: Adverse yaw: yaw opposite to aileron input direction, caused by more drag on the rising wing. Corrected with rudder in the direction of turn. Reduced by differential and Frise ailerons.
key_facts: Adverse yaw: initial yaw opposite to intended roll/turn direction
Caused by increased drag on the rising wing (from its lowered aileron)
Corrected by applying rudder in the same direction as the turn
Differential ailerons: upgoing deflects more than downgoing to equalise drag
Frise ailerons: upgoing aileron leading edge protrudes below wing, adding compensating drag

syllabus_desc: the secondary effect of rudder.
concept: The secondary effect of rudder is a roll in the same direction as the applied yaw. When you apply right rudder, the nose yaws right. This means the left wing is now moving faster through the air (because it's on the outside of the yaw) and the right wing is moving slower. The faster-moving left wing generates more lift, and the slower right wing generates less. This differential lift creates a rolling moment to the right -- in the same direction as the yaw. This is the dihedral effect in action, where a sideslip (created by the yaw) produces a rolling response. The secondary roll effect of rudder is why rudder alone can be used to control bank in some situations, and why excessive rudder input can lead to an uncommanded roll.
exam_tip: Secondary effect of rudder is roll in the same direction as the yaw. Right rudder produces right yaw, which causes a roll to the right. This is due to the dihedral effect.
key_facts: Secondary effect of rudder is roll in the direction of the applied yaw
Caused by differential airspeed over the two wings during the yaw
The faster-moving wing (outside of yaw) generates more lift and rises
This is the dihedral effect responding to the sideslip created by yaw
Excessive rudder can produce significant unwanted roll

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of airspeed and slipstream on control effectiveness.
concept: Control effectiveness depends heavily on airspeed and slipstream. Since all flight controls are aerodynamic surfaces, their ability to produce force depends on the dynamic pressure of the air flowing over them -- which means they work better at higher airspeeds and less well at low speeds. At very low speeds (near the stall), the controls may feel mushy and ineffective. The propeller slipstream adds extra airflow over the surfaces it washes -- typically the rudder and elevator on a single-engine aeroplane, and sometimes part of the wing. Even at low forward speed, a high-power setting produces a fast slipstream that keeps the rudder and elevator reasonably effective. This is why you maintain power during a go-around -- the slipstream keeps your controls working. Conversely, in a glide with the engine at idle, you lose that slipstream boost and control effectiveness at low speed is reduced.
exam_tip: Control effectiveness increases with airspeed (dynamic pressure). Slipstream from the propeller enhances rudder and elevator effectiveness at low speeds. Power off = reduced control at low speed.
key_facts: Control effectiveness depends on dynamic pressure (proportional to V squared)
Controls are less effective at low airspeeds, more effective at high speeds
Propeller slipstream enhances rudder and elevator effectiveness at low forward speed
High power setting maintains control effectiveness even at low airspeed
Power-off at low speed gives least control effectiveness -- critical near the stall

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principles of trim tabs and describe the correct method of using trim controls.
concept: Trim tabs are small hinged surfaces on the trailing edge of a primary control surface. Their purpose is to reduce the control force the pilot must hold to maintain a desired flight condition. When you trim, you're deflecting the trim tab, which aerodynamically holds the main control surface in position so you can fly hands-off (or at least with minimal force). The correct method is to first establish the desired flight condition with the primary control, then use the trim to reduce the force to zero. You trim to relieve the force, not to fly the aeroplane. Common mistake: using trim as a primary control -- this leads to overcontrolling and porpoising. The trim tab deflects opposite to the main surface: to trim nose-up, the trim tab moves down, which pushes the elevator up. Trimming is essential for any sustained flight condition to reduce pilot workload and fatigue.
exam_tip: Trim tab deflects opposite to the control surface. Set attitude/speed first with control, then trim to zero the force. Never use trim as a primary flight control.
key_facts: Trim tabs are small surfaces on the trailing edge of primary controls
They reduce pilot holding force for sustained flight conditions
Correct technique: set attitude first, then trim to relieve the force
Trim tab deflects opposite to the main control surface
Never use trim as a primary control -- it leads to overcontrolling

syllabus_desc: Explain the reason for aerodynamic balancing of control surfaces.
concept: As an aircraft flies faster, the aerodynamic forces on the control surfaces increase with V squared. Without some form of aerodynamic balancing, the pilot forces required to move the controls at high speed would become unmanageably heavy. Aerodynamic balancing reduces the hinge moment (the force trying to return the control to neutral) by using aerodynamic forces to assist the pilot's input. This makes the controls lighter and more manageable across the speed range. However, too much aerodynamic balancing is dangerous -- it can make the controls too light, too sensitive, or even cause the control to overbalance (snap to full deflection). The amount of balancing is carefully designed to give appropriate control feel throughout the flight envelope.
exam_tip: Aerodynamic balancing reduces pilot control forces at high speeds. Too little = heavy controls. Too much = overbalanced/too sensitive. The balance must give appropriate feel across the speed range.
key_facts: Control forces increase with V squared without balancing
Aerodynamic balancing reduces the hinge moment on control surfaces
Purpose: maintain manageable control forces across the speed range
Too much balancing risks overbalance (control snaps to full deflection)
Balancing must be designed to give appropriate control feel throughout the flight envelope

syllabus_desc: Describe the main methods for achieving control balance.
concept: Several methods achieve aerodynamic balance. A horn balance extends part of the control surface ahead of the hinge line, so the airflow hitting the horn produces a force that assists the pilot's input. An internal (sealed) balance uses a plate projecting forward from the control surface into a sealed chamber ahead of the hinge, where differential pressure helps move the control. A set-back hinge moves the hinge point aft from the leading edge of the control surface, so that air pressure on the area ahead of the hinge partially balances the force on the area behind it. Each method has its design trade-offs in terms of effectiveness, weight, and susceptibility to overbalance, but they all work on the same principle: using aerodynamic forces ahead of the hinge to reduce the net hinge moment the pilot must overcome.
exam_tip: Main methods: horn balance (portion ahead of hinge), internal (sealed) balance, set-back hinge. All work by creating aerodynamic forces ahead of the hinge line.
key_facts: Horn balance: part of control surface extends ahead of hinge line
Internal (sealed) balance: plate in sealed chamber uses differential pressure
Set-back hinge: hinge line moved aft so some surface area is ahead of it
All methods use aerodynamic force ahead of the hinge to reduce pilot effort
Design must prevent overbalance while providing adequate force reduction

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between a balance tab and an anti-balance tab.
concept: A balance tab moves in the opposite direction to the control surface. When you deflect the elevator up, the balance tab deflects down, creating a force that helps push the elevator up -- assisting the pilot. The more the control deflects, the more the tab helps. This reduces control forces and is a form of aerodynamic balancing. An anti-balance tab (or anti-servo tab) does the opposite -- it moves in the same direction as the control surface. When the elevator goes up, the anti-balance tab also goes up, which creates a force that opposes the elevator deflection. This increases control forces and stick force per g, improving control feel and preventing the pilot from overcontrolling. Anti-balance tabs are commonly found on stabilators (all-moving tailplanes) because stabilators are inherently very sensitive and need the extra resistance to give acceptable control feel.
exam_tip: Balance tab: moves OPPOSITE to control surface, reduces control force. Anti-balance tab: moves SAME direction as control surface, increases force/improves feel. Stabilators use anti-balance tabs.
key_facts: Balance tab: deflects opposite to control surface, reduces pilot control force
Anti-balance tab: deflects same direction as control surface, increases force
Balance tabs assist the pilot; anti-balance tabs resist the pilot
Anti-balance tabs improve control feel and prevent overcontrolling
Stabilators (all-moving tails) commonly use anti-balance tabs for adequate feel

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose for mass balancing.
concept: Mass balancing is about preventing flutter, a potentially catastrophic vibration of control surfaces. Flutter occurs when the aerodynamic forces on a control surface interact with the surface's natural frequency of vibration, creating a self-sustaining and rapidly worsening oscillation. If the centre of gravity of the control surface is behind the hinge line (which it usually is, since the surface extends aft of the hinge), inertia forces during any vibration create moments that worsen the oscillation. Mass balancing adds weight ahead of the hinge line to move the CG of the control surface forward -- ideally onto or ahead of the hinge axis. This breaks the feedback loop that drives flutter. Without mass balancing, flutter can tear a control surface off the aircraft in seconds at high speed. It's one of those design features that's invisible when it works and catastrophic when it fails.
exam_tip: Mass balancing moves the CG of the control surface onto or ahead of the hinge line to prevent flutter. Flutter is a self-amplifying vibration that can be destructive in seconds.
key_facts: Purpose: prevent flutter by moving control surface CG forward
Flutter: self-sustaining, potentially destructive vibration of control surfaces
Occurs when inertia forces and aerodynamic forces create a positive feedback loop
Mass balance weight is added ahead of the hinge line
Without mass balancing, flutter can destroy the control surface in seconds

syllabus_desc: Describe and explain flexural and torsional flutter.
concept: Flutter comes in two main forms. Flexural flutter involves the wing (or tail) bending up and down while the control surface oscillates in opposition. As the wing flexes upward, the inertia of the control surface (with its CG behind the hinge) causes it to deflect downward, which adds aerodynamic force that increases the flex, and the cycle feeds on itself. Torsional flutter involves the wing twisting about its elastic axis while the control surface oscillates. As the wing twists, the changing angle of attack creates aerodynamic forces that further twist the wing, and the control surface's mass behind the hinge amplifies the oscillation. Both types are driven by the same fundamental mechanism: the control surface's CG behind the hinge creating destabilising inertia forces during vibration. Both are prevented by mass balancing and structural stiffness.
exam_tip: Flexural flutter: wing bending + control oscillation. Torsional flutter: wing twisting + control oscillation. Both are prevented by mass balancing (CG forward of hinge) and structural stiffness.
key_facts: Flexural flutter: wing bending combined with control surface oscillation
Torsional flutter: wing twisting combined with control surface oscillation
Both are self-amplifying and potentially destructive
Both are driven by control surface CG behind the hinge line
Prevented by mass balancing and adequate structural stiffness

syllabus_desc: Describe the methods of providing mass balance.
concept: Mass balance can be provided in several ways. The most visible method is external mass balance weights, typically seen as small torpedo-shaped or cylindrical weights on arms extending forward of the control surface leading edge -- you'll often see them ahead of ailerons on light aircraft. Another method is internal mass balance, where lead or other dense material is embedded within the leading edge of the control surface itself, ahead of the hinge line. This is cleaner aerodynamically but harder to adjust. Some designs use the control surface's leading edge structure itself (with the hinge set back) to provide inherent mass balance. The key requirement in all cases is to bring the control surface CG onto or forward of the hinge axis. During maintenance, any alteration to a control surface (repainting, repairs, modifications) must be followed by a mass balance check, because even small changes in weight distribution can affect flutter margins.
exam_tip: Methods: external weights on arms, internal weights in LE, set-back hinge with heavy LE. After any control surface maintenance or repainting, mass balance must be rechecked.
key_facts: External mass balance: weights on arms projecting forward of the control surface
Internal mass balance: dense material embedded in the control surface leading edge
Set-back hinge with heavy LE structure can provide inherent balance
All methods aim to move the control surface CG onto or forward of the hinge axis
Mass balance must be rechecked after any maintenance, repair, or repainting of control surfaces

syllabus_desc: Explain the stalled condition of an aerofoil.
concept: An aerofoil stalls when the angle of attack exceeds the critical angle -- typically around 15-16 degrees for a GP aerofoil. At this point, the airflow can no longer follow the upper surface contour. The boundary layer separates from the surface, starting near the trailing edge and progressively moving forward as alpha increases further. The smooth, accelerated flow over the upper surface is replaced by a turbulent, separated wake. The suction peak collapses, the upper-surface low pressure is lost, and lift drops dramatically -- often by 30-50% from CLmax. Drag increases sharply as the separated wake creates massive form drag. The key thing to remember is that the stall is an angle of attack event, not a speed event. You can stall at any speed if you exceed the critical angle of attack.
exam_tip: The stall occurs at a specific angle of attack (not speed). A wing can be stalled at any airspeed, any attitude, and any power setting if the critical alpha is exceeded.
key_facts: Stall occurs when angle of attack exceeds the critical angle (~15-16 degrees for GP aerofoils)
Boundary layer separates from the upper surface, destroying the suction peak
Lift drops dramatically (30-50%+ from CLmax) and drag increases sharply
The stall is an angle of attack event -- it can occur at any speed
Flow separation starts near the trailing edge and moves forward with increasing alpha

syllabus_desc: Explain basic stall speed and relate it to the lift formula.
concept: The basic stall speed is the minimum speed at which the aircraft can sustain level flight at maximum lift coefficient (CLmax). From the lift formula rearranged: Vs = square root of (2W / (rho x S x CLmax)). This is the 1g stall speed in straight and level flight. The formula tells you that stall speed increases with weight (heavier = need more speed for same lift), decreases with air density (but note: IAS accounts for density, so the IAS stall speed is essentially constant), decreases with wing area (more wing = more lift at same speed), and decreases with CLmax (more efficient wing = less speed needed). At sea level in ISA conditions, a typical training aeroplane might stall at around 48-55 knots IAS clean.
exam_tip: Vs = square root of (2W / (rho x S x CLmax)). Stall speed increases with the square root of weight increase and decreases with higher CLmax. The stall IAS is approximately constant at any altitude.
key_facts: Vs = square root of (2W / (rho x S x CLmax))
Stall speed increases with weight (proportional to square root of weight ratio)
Stall IAS is approximately constant regardless of altitude
CLmax determines the minimum achievable stall speed for a given weight and configuration
This formula gives the 1g stall speed in straight, unaccelerated flight

syllabus_desc: Describe typical symptoms and other indications of the approach to the stall.
concept: As the stall approaches, several symptoms warn the pilot. Decreasing airspeed is the most obvious -- if you're slowing down in level flight without reducing power, you're increasing alpha toward the stall. Reducing control effectiveness occurs because the lower dynamic pressure gives the controls less authority -- the ailerons may feel mushy and the elevator needs larger deflections. Airframe buffet is felt as the turbulent, separated airflow from the wing hits the tailplane, shaking the airframe. Many aircraft have a stall warning device (horn, light, or stick shaker) that activates a few knots above the stall. There may be a nose-down pitching tendency as the CP moves rearward at the stall. Reduced noise from wind over the airframe is another cue. A good pilot recognises these symptoms well before the full stall develops.
exam_tip: Stall symptoms: decreasing airspeed, mushy controls, buffet, stall warning activation, possible nose-drop. Learn to recognise these progressively -- you should recover before the full stall.
key_facts: Decreasing airspeed and mushy control response
Airframe buffet from separated airflow hitting the tail
Stall warning device activation (horn, light, or stick shaker)
Possible nose-down pitch as CP moves aft
Reduced wind noise and high engine-to-airspeed ratio (high power, low speed)

syllabus_desc: Describe the changes in the airflow over the wing, movement of the CP, and aircraft attitude as the point of stall is reached.
concept: As the stall develops, the airflow changes dramatically. The smooth, attached flow over the upper surface begins to separate near the trailing edge. As alpha increases further, the separation point moves forward, enlarging the turbulent wake. The suction peak near the leading edge weakens and eventually collapses. The centre of pressure, which had been moving forward with increasing alpha, suddenly shifts rearward as the pressure distribution breaks down. In most aeroplane designs, this rearward CP shift produces a nose-down pitching moment -- the nose drops, which is actually a helpful natural recovery tendency (it reduces alpha). The aircraft typically pitches nose-down, potentially with a wing-drop if the stall is asymmetric. The buffeting intensifies as more of the wing stalls and the turbulent wake grows.
exam_tip: At the stall: flow separates from TE forward, suction peak collapses, CP shifts rearward, nose pitches down (natural recovery tendency), buffet intensifies.
key_facts: Flow separation starts at trailing edge and moves forward toward leading edge
Suction peak on upper surface collapses
CP shifts rearward suddenly, creating a nose-down pitching moment
The nose-down pitch is a natural stall recovery tendency
Turbulent wake intensifies, causing increased airframe buffet

syllabus_desc: Explain the standard recovery from the stalled condition.
concept: The standard stall recovery has three key steps, in order: reduce the angle of attack, apply full power, and minimise altitude loss. The most critical and immediate action is to reduce alpha below the critical angle by moving the control column forward (lowering the nose). This re-attaches the airflow and restores lift. Simultaneously, apply maximum power to minimise altitude loss and increase airspeed. As speed builds and the wing is flying again, gently raise the nose to resume normal flight, being careful not to pull back too aggressively or you'll re-stall. The recovery should be smooth and decisive. Don't be tentative with the forward stick -- a half-hearted push that doesn't reduce alpha enough will leave you semi-stalled and sinking. The priority is always to fly the wing first, then worry about altitude.
exam_tip: Stall recovery: reduce alpha (lower nose), apply full power, level wings, minimise altitude loss. The first priority is ALWAYS to reduce angle of attack.
key_facts: Step 1: Reduce angle of attack by lowering the nose (push forward on control column)
Step 2: Apply full power simultaneously to minimise altitude loss
Step 3: Level the wings and return to normal flight as speed increases
Reducing alpha is the most critical action -- it must be done first
Avoid pulling back too aggressively during recovery or you may re-stall

syllabus_desc: aircraft weight;
concept: Heavier aircraft stall at higher speeds. From Vs = square root of (2W / (rho x S x CLmax)), stall speed varies as the square root of weight. If weight increases by 21%, stall speed increases by 10%. A fully loaded Cessna 172 stalls at a higher speed than a lightly loaded one because the wing needs to generate more lift to support the greater weight. At any given speed, the heavier aircraft is flying at a higher CL (and therefore higher alpha). It reaches CLmax -- and the stall -- at a higher speed. This is why take-off and approach speeds increase with aircraft weight, and why weight-and-balance calculations aren't just paperwork exercises -- they directly affect your safety margins.
exam_tip: Stall speed varies as the square root of weight. 44% more weight = 20% higher stall speed. Heavier = faster stall = longer take-off/landing distances.
key_facts: Stall speed increases with the square root of weight increase
Heavier aircraft need more CL at any given speed, reaching CLmax sooner
Vs new = Vs old x square root of (new weight / old weight)
44% increase in weight gives approximately 20% increase in stall speed
This is why take-off and approach speeds are adjusted for aircraft weight

syllabus_desc: load factor;
concept: Load factor is the ratio of lift to weight (n = L/W). In straight and level flight, n = 1 (1g). In a turn, lift must be greater than weight (to provide the turning force), so load factor exceeds 1. In a 60-degree banked turn, n = 2 (2g), meaning the wing must produce twice the aircraft's weight in lift. This effectively increases the 'apparent weight' the wing must support, so the stall speed increases. The relationship is: Vs(loaded) = Vs(1g) x square root of n. At 2g (60-degree bank), stall speed increases by 41%. At 3.8g (the positive limit for a normal category aircraft), stall speed increases by 95% -- nearly double. This is why steep turns and aggressive manoeuvres dramatically reduce your stall margin.
exam_tip: Stall speed increases as the square root of load factor. At 60 degrees bank (2g), stall speed is 41% higher. At 45 degrees bank (1.41g), stall speed is 19% higher. Know these numbers.
key_facts: Load factor n = Lift / Weight; in level flight n = 1
Stall speed increases as the square root of load factor: Vs(n) = Vs(1g) x sqrt(n)
30-degree bank: n = 1.15, Vs increases ~7%
45-degree bank: n = 1.41, Vs increases ~19%
60-degree bank: n = 2.0, Vs increases ~41%

syllabus_desc: power;
concept: Power affects stalling speed in two ways. First, at high power settings, the propeller slipstream flows over the wing root area, energising the boundary layer and increasing the effective dynamic pressure over that section. This produces extra lift at the wing root, effectively reducing the stall speed slightly. Second, if the thrust line is inclined upward relative to the flight path, a component of thrust acts vertically, helping to support the aircraft's weight. This reduces the lift required from the wing, which reduces the CL needed, which reduces the stall speed. The combined effect means that the power-on stall speed is typically 5-10 knots lower than the power-off stall speed. However, the power-on stall may be less predictable, with more tendency toward wing-drop due to the asymmetric slipstream effects.
exam_tip: Power reduces stall speed through slipstream effect and the vertical component of thrust. Power-on stall speed is typically 5-10 kt lower than power-off. But power-on stalls may be less predictable.
key_facts: Power reduces stall speed through two mechanisms
Slipstream energises the boundary layer and increases local dynamic pressure
Upward thrust component supports some weight, reducing required CL
Power-on stall speed is typically 5-10 kt lower than power-off
Power-on stalls may produce more wing-drop tendency due to asymmetric slipstream

syllabus_desc: altitude;
concept: Altitude affects the TAS at which the stall occurs but not the IAS. As altitude increases and density decreases, the aircraft must fly at a higher TAS to achieve the same dynamic pressure (and therefore the same IAS). Since the stall occurs at a specific dynamic pressure (corresponding to CLmax at the aircraft's current weight), the stalling IAS stays essentially constant at any altitude. But the stalling TAS increases with altitude. This matters for two practical reasons: your ground speed at the stall is higher at altitude (affecting any forced landing), and your true rate of descent in a stall is greater at altitude. For performance calculations, the IAS stall speed is the reference value since it directly relates to the aerodynamic forces.
exam_tip: Stalling IAS is constant regardless of altitude. Stalling TAS increases with altitude (because air density is lower). Use IAS for stall reference; TAS matters for performance calculations.
key_facts: Stalling IAS remains approximately constant at all altitudes
Stalling TAS increases with altitude due to decreasing air density
The stall occurs at a specific dynamic pressure, which IAS measures directly
Higher TAS at stall means higher ground speed in a forced landing at altitude
Density altitude affects TAS but not IAS at the stall

syllabus_desc: the use of flaps and slats;
concept: Flaps and slats reduce the stalling speed by increasing CLmax. Trailing-edge flaps increase effective camber, raising the CL curve and giving a higher CLmax (though at a slightly lower stalling angle). Leading-edge slats delay flow separation, allowing a higher stalling angle and therefore higher CLmax. The stalling speed reduction follows the formula: new Vs = old Vs x square root of (old CLmax / new CLmax). For example, if flaps increase CLmax from 1.5 to 2.1 (40% increase), stall speed drops by about 15%. With full flaps and slats combined on a transport aircraft, CLmax might increase from 1.5 to 3.0, reducing stall speed by about 29%. This is the whole point of high-lift devices -- they make low-speed flight safe with reasonable runway lengths.
exam_tip: Flaps and slats increase CLmax, reducing Vs. New Vs = old Vs x sqrt(old CLmax / new CLmax). 40% increase in CLmax = about 15% decrease in Vs.
key_facts: Flaps increase CLmax by increasing effective camber (stalling angle decreases slightly)
Slats increase CLmax by delaying separation (stalling angle increases)
New Vs = old Vs x square root of (old CLmax / new CLmax)
Combined slats and flaps give the greatest CLmax increase and lowest Vs
The stalling speed reduction is proportional to the square root of the CLmax ratio

syllabus_desc: contamination of the wing surfaces.
concept: Contamination of the wing surfaces -- frost, ice, rain, snow, insects, or dirt -- increases the stalling speed by reducing CLmax. The rough surface disrupts the boundary layer, promoting early transition to turbulent flow and earlier flow separation. The CL curve peaks at a lower value and the stall occurs at a lower angle of attack. Even a thin layer of frost that looks harmless can reduce CLmax by 10-30%, increasing stalling speed significantly. Ice accretion is worse because it also changes the wing shape, further degrading the pressure distribution. Rain on the wings increases surface roughness and adds weight. The regulations requiring clear wings before take-off aren't being fussy -- they're protecting you from a wing that won't produce the lift you're expecting at the speeds you're planning to use.
exam_tip: Even light frost can reduce CLmax by 10-30% and increase stalling speed significantly. Any contamination must be removed before flight. Ice is worse than frost -- it changes wing shape too.
key_facts: Wing contamination reduces CLmax and increases stalling speed
Even thin frost can reduce CLmax by 10-30%
Contamination disrupts the boundary layer and promotes early flow separation
Ice also changes the wing shape, further degrading lift production
Wings must be clear of all contamination before take-off

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions which encourage a wing-drop at the stall.
concept: A wing-drop at the stall occurs when one wing stalls before the other. The wing that stalls first loses lift suddenly while the other wing is still flying, creating a rolling moment toward the stalled wing. Several conditions encourage this. Yaw at the stall (uncoordinated flight) means one wing is moving faster than the other -- the slower wing reaches CLmax first and drops. Asymmetric contamination (frost on one wing) gives different stall characteristics. Wing manufacturing tolerances can create slight differences between the two wings. Turbulence or gusts can momentarily increase alpha on one wing. Propeller slipstream asymmetry at high alpha affects the two wings differently. Even slight aileron deflection near the stall can trigger asymmetric stalling. The wing-drop is particularly hazardous close to the ground where altitude for recovery is limited.
exam_tip: Wing-drop at the stall is caused by asymmetric stalling. Main causes: uncoordinated flight (yaw), contamination, turbulence, slipstream effects. Very hazardous close to the ground.
key_facts: Wing-drop occurs when one wing stalls before the other
Yaw (uncoordinated flight) is the most common cause -- slower wing stalls first
Asymmetric contamination, turbulence, and slipstream effects can trigger wing-drop
The dropped wing experiences autorotation tendency if not corrected promptly
Particularly hazardous at low altitude where recovery room is limited

syllabus_desc: Explain the design measures taken to reduce the tendency for wing-drop.
concept: Aircraft designers use several measures to ensure the stall progresses from root to tip rather than tip to root, which helps prevent wing-drop. Washout (wing twist where the tip is at a lower angle of incidence than the root) means the root reaches the stalling angle first while the tip is still flying -- this keeps the ailerons effective during the stall. Stall strips (small triangular strips) near the leading edge of the wing root trigger early flow separation at the root. The wing planform matters: a rectangular wing stalls at the root first (good stall characteristics), while an untreated swept wing tends to stall at the tips first (bad). Carefully designed taper ratios and the use of different aerofoil sections along the span also help ensure root-first stall progression. All these measures aim to keep the ailerons effective as long as possible during the stall.
exam_tip: Design goals: root stalls first, tips last, ailerons remain effective. Methods: washout, stall strips, planform design, aerofoil section variation along span. Rectangular wings naturally stall root-first.
key_facts: Goal: root stalls first, tips (and ailerons) stall last
Washout: wing twist so tip is at lower incidence than root
Stall strips: small strips on root leading edge to trigger early root separation
Rectangular planform naturally stalls at the root first
Swept wings need additional measures to prevent tip-first stalling

syllabus_desc: Explain the caution against using aileron near or at the stalling angle.
concept: Using aileron near or at the stalling angle is dangerous because it can make one wing stall before the other, causing a wing-drop in the wrong direction. When you apply aileron to, say, raise the left wing, the left aileron goes down (increasing local alpha on the left wing) and the right aileron goes up (decreasing local alpha on the right wing). Near the stall, the left wing may already be close to CLmax. The down-aileron pushes it past the critical angle, stalling that wing -- which drops instead of rising. You've now got a wing-drop in the opposite direction to your input. The correct action near the stall is to use rudder, not aileron, to control any wing-drop tendency, because rudder won't directly change the wing's angle of attack the way aileron does.
exam_tip: Near the stall: aileron can cause the wrong wing to stall and drop. Use rudder, not aileron, to correct wing-drop tendencies near the stall. The down-aileron increases local alpha, potentially stalling that wing.
key_facts: Near the stall, aileron input can cause the opposite wing to drop
The down-going aileron increases local alpha, potentially exceeding the critical angle
The wing with the lowered aileron may stall and drop instead of rising
Use rudder to control wing-drop tendency near the stall, not aileron
This is critical knowledge for stall recovery and base-to-final turn safety

syllabus_desc: from a stall which has resulted in a wing-drop;
concept: When a stall results in a wing-drop, the priority is to unstall the wing first, then level the wings. The sequence is: simultaneously reduce alpha (push forward on the control column) and apply opposite rudder to prevent the yaw that develops toward the dropped wing. Do not use aileron to pick up the dropped wing -- the down-aileron on the dropped wing will increase its alpha further, deepening the stall on that wing and making the situation worse. Once the wing is unstalled (nose lowered, airflow reattached), you can then use aileron normally to level the wings. Apply full power to minimise altitude loss. The instinct to use aileron to pick up a dropped wing is strong, but at or near the stall it's exactly the wrong input.
exam_tip: Wing-drop stall: reduce alpha first, opposite rudder to stop yaw, DO NOT use aileron until wing is unstalled. Aileron on the stalled wing makes it worse. Apply power to minimise altitude loss.
key_facts: First action: reduce alpha by lowering the nose
Apply opposite rudder to prevent yaw toward the dropped wing
Do NOT use aileron to pick up the dropped wing while stalled
Once unstalled (alpha reduced below critical), use aileron normally to level wings
Apply full power to minimise altitude loss

syllabus_desc: at the onset of the stall.
concept: Recovering at the onset of the stall -- before a full stall or wing-drop develops -- is the preferred technique. As soon as you recognise the approach to the stall (buffet, stall warning, control feel), reduce angle of attack immediately by lowering the nose. Apply full power simultaneously. This arrests the stall before it fully develops and minimises altitude loss. If the wings are level and you catch it early, you may lose only 50-100 feet. The key is prompt recognition and decisive action -- a hesitant response allows the stall to develop further, potentially into a wing-drop or incipient spin. During training, practice both the full stall recovery and the incipient stall recovery, but in real operations, you should always be recovering at the onset, never deliberately entering a full stall.
exam_tip: At onset: recognise symptoms early, reduce alpha immediately, apply full power, minimise altitude loss. Early recognition and prompt action prevents the full stall from developing.
key_facts: Recover at the first sign of stall (buffet, warning, mushy controls)
Immediately reduce alpha by lowering the nose
Apply full power simultaneously
Early recovery minimises altitude loss (50-100 ft vs hundreds of feet)
In practice, always recover at onset -- never let a stall develop to full wing-drop

syllabus_desc: Explain the process of autorotation (leading to the spin).
concept: Autorotation is the self-sustaining rolling motion that leads to a spin. It starts when one wing is more deeply stalled than the other (from a wing-drop at the stall). The more deeply stalled (dropped) wing has a higher angle of attack and is producing less lift and more drag. The other wing, at a lower angle of attack, is producing more lift and less drag. This differential lift and drag creates a rolling moment toward the stalled wing and a yawing moment in the same direction. As the aircraft rolls and yaws, the dropping wing's angle of attack increases further (due to the rolling motion adding to the relative airflow angle), while the rising wing's angle of attack decreases. This drives the dropping wing further past the stall and the rising wing further from it, creating a self-sustaining, self-amplifying rotation. This is autorotation, and if not stopped, it develops into a fully developed spin.
exam_tip: Autorotation: one wing deeper in the stall than the other creates self-sustaining roll and yaw. The dropping wing's alpha increases further (deeper stall), the rising wing's alpha decreases. This is the mechanism leading to a spin.
key_facts: Autorotation is a self-sustaining rolling/yawing motion
Starts when one wing is more deeply stalled than the other
Dropping wing: increasing alpha, less lift, more drag
Rising wing: decreasing alpha, more lift, less drag
The differential creates a self-amplifying rotation that leads to a spin

syllabus_desc: the instrument indications which confirm the fact and direction of a spin;
concept: In a spin, the instruments tell a specific story. The airspeed indicator shows a low, relatively constant reading -- typically in the stall speed range or below, because the high angle of attack and steep descent path affect the pitot-static system. The altimeter unwinds rapidly as you descend at thousands of feet per minute. The vertical speed indicator shows a high rate of descent. The turn coordinator or turn needle shows full deflection in the direction of the spin. The heading indicator rotates continuously in the direction of the spin. The attitude indicator (if not tumbled) shows a steep nose-down pitch with continuous roll. To confirm the spin direction, the turn needle is the most reliable indicator -- it deflects in the direction of spin.
exam_tip: In a spin: low/constant airspeed, rapidly decreasing altitude, full turn needle deflection in spin direction. The turn needle confirms spin direction most reliably.
key_facts: Airspeed: low and relatively constant (near or below stall speed)
Altimeter: unwinding rapidly (high rate of descent)
VSI: showing high rate of descent
Turn needle/coordinator: full deflection in the direction of spin
Heading indicator: rotating continuously in the spin direction

syllabus_desc: the difference between the spin and spiral dive;
concept: A spin and a spiral dive look superficially similar from inside the cockpit (nose down, turning rapidly) but they're aerodynamically opposite situations. In a spin, the aircraft is stalled (high alpha, low airspeed). The airspeed stays low and constant, and the turn needle is at full deflection. In a spiral dive, the aircraft is not stalled -- it's essentially a steep, tightening descending turn. The airspeed increases rapidly, the load factor (G-force) is high and increasing, and the turn needle may not be at full deflection. The key distinguishing indicator is airspeed: if it's low and constant, you're in a spin. If it's increasing rapidly, you're in a spiral dive. The recovery techniques are completely different: spin recovery requires opposite rudder and unstalling the wing, while spiral dive recovery requires rolling wings level and gently pulling out of the dive (easing the load factor).
exam_tip: Spin: low/constant airspeed, stalled. Spiral dive: rapidly increasing airspeed, not stalled. Check the ASI -- it's the most reliable way to tell them apart. Recovery techniques are opposite.
key_facts: Spin: stalled, low constant airspeed, full turn needle deflection
Spiral dive: not stalled, rapidly increasing airspeed, high G-loading
Airspeed is the key distinguishing instrument
Spin recovery: opposite rudder, reduce alpha, then level wings
Spiral dive recovery: level wings, ease out of the dive gently (watch G-limits)

syllabus_desc: the standard recovery technique.
concept: The standard spin recovery technique follows the PARE acronym (or a similar sequence): Power to idle -- remove the propeller effects that may be sustaining the spin. Ailerons neutral -- aileron input can complicate spin recovery. Rudder full opposite to the direction of spin -- this is the key recovery input that stops the autorotation by yawing the aircraft against the spin direction. Elevator forward (reduce alpha) -- after a brief pause for the rudder to take effect, push the control column forward to unstall the wings by reducing the angle of attack below the critical angle. Once rotation stops, centralise the rudder and gently ease out of the resulting dive, being careful not to exceed the aircraft's speed limits or G-limits. The exact sequence and timing varies between aircraft types -- always follow the POH procedure for your specific aircraft.
exam_tip: Standard spin recovery: Power idle, Ailerons neutral, Rudder opposite to spin direction, Elevator forward (unstall). PARE. Always follow the specific aircraft's POH procedure.
key_facts: Power: idle (remove thrust effects)
Ailerons: neutral (remove aileron complications)
Rudder: full opposite to spin direction (stops autorotation)
Elevator: forward after rudder takes effect (unstalls the wing)
After rotation stops: centralise rudder, gently ease out of the dive

syllabus_desc: Explain the four forces acting and the conditions required for steady straight and level flight.
concept: In steady straight and level flight at constant speed, four forces act on the aeroplane: lift, weight, thrust, and drag. For this flight condition, the aircraft must be in both translational and rotational equilibrium. Lift must equal weight (so there's no vertical acceleration), and thrust must equal drag (so there's no horizontal acceleration). Additionally, all pitching moments must balance so the aircraft doesn't pitch nose-up or nose-down. These conditions define what 'straight and level at constant speed' really means aerodynamically. If any of these balances is upset -- say you add power without changing anything else -- the equilibrium breaks and the aeroplane accelerates until a new equilibrium is established.
exam_tip: S&L at constant speed: L = W, T = D, and all moments balanced. If any equality is disturbed, the aircraft accelerates or rotates until a new equilibrium is reached.
key_facts: Lift equals weight (no vertical acceleration)
Thrust equals drag (no horizontal acceleration)
All pitching moments are balanced (no rotation)
Both translational and rotational equilibrium are required
Disturbing any balance causes acceleration toward a new equilibrium

syllabus_desc: the lift/weight and thrust/drag couples;
concept: In most aircraft configurations, the lift acts through the centre of pressure (CP) of the wing and weight acts through the centre of gravity (CG). Unless these points are in exactly the same vertical line, the offset creates a pitching couple. With the CG ahead of the CP (normal forward CG position), the weight/lift couple creates a nose-down pitching moment. Similarly, thrust typically acts along a line below the drag line of action. If thrust is below drag, the thrust/drag couple creates a nose-up pitching moment. The magnitude of each couple depends on the force and the distance between the lines of action. In a well-designed aeroplane, these couples partially offset each other, but there's usually a residual moment that the tailplane must balance.
exam_tip: CG ahead of CP: nose-down couple. Thrust below drag: nose-up couple. These partially cancel, and the tailplane balances the residual moment.
key_facts: Lift/weight couple: nose-down if CG is ahead of CP (normal configuration)
Thrust/drag couple: nose-up if thrust line is below drag line
These couples partially offset each other
The residual unbalanced moment is corrected by the tailplane
The couple magnitudes change with speed, power, and CG position

syllabus_desc: pitching moments and the tailplane stabilising moment;
concept: Pitching moments in flight come from several sources. The wing itself produces a pitching moment that varies with angle of attack (cambered wings have an inherent nose-down pitching moment at most angles). The thrust/drag couple and lift/weight couple contribute as discussed. The tailplane is the primary stabilising surface -- it produces a downward (or occasionally upward) aerodynamic force that creates a pitching moment to balance all the other moments. The tailplane moment depends on its lift coefficient (controlled by the elevator or stabilator position), the dynamic pressure, its area, and its distance from the CG. The further aft the tailplane, the longer the moment arm and the less force it needs to produce the required balancing moment.
exam_tip: The tailplane balances all other pitching moments. Its effectiveness depends on tail area, moment arm (distance from CG), and dynamic pressure. Further aft = more effective per unit force.
key_facts: Wing camber creates an inherent pitching moment
Lift/weight and thrust/drag couples create pitching moments
The tailplane provides the stabilising balancing moment
Tailplane effectiveness = force x moment arm (distance from CG)
Longer moment arm means less force needed for the same stabilising effect

syllabus_desc: pitching moments caused by power changes, undercarriage and flap extension.
concept: Power changes, undercarriage extension, and flap extension all create pitching moments that require trim adjustments. Increasing power on a propeller aircraft creates several pitch effects: the thrust line below the drag line creates a nose-up couple, the slipstream over the tailplane changes its effectiveness, and the increased lift from slipstream over the wing root shifts the CP. The net effect is usually a nose-up pitch with power increase. Lowering the undercarriage increases drag below the thrust line, creating a nose-down pitching moment, and the extra drag itself requires more power to maintain speed. Lowering flaps shifts the CP aft (more camber at the trailing edge), creating a nose-down pitching moment, while also increasing drag. Each of these changes requires the pilot to retrim to maintain the desired attitude and speed.
exam_tip: Power increase: generally nose-up pitch. Undercarriage down: nose-down pitch (drag increase below thrust line). Flaps down: nose-down pitch (CP moves aft). All require retrimming.
key_facts: Power increase: generally produces nose-up pitching moment
Undercarriage extension: nose-down moment from increased drag below thrust line
Flap extension: nose-down moment from aft CP movement and changed pressure distribution
Each configuration change requires retrimming
The specific pitch effects vary between aircraft types -- know your aeroplane

syllabus_desc: Explain the power and attitude relationships at various airspeeds in straight and level flight.
concept: In straight and level flight, there's a direct relationship between power, attitude, and airspeed. At high speed, the aircraft flies with a low angle of attack (low alpha, low CL) and needs relatively low power to overcome drag (which is mostly parasite drag). The nose attitude is relatively low. At low speed, the aircraft needs a high angle of attack (high alpha, high CL) to produce enough lift, and requires more power than you might expect because induced drag is high. The nose attitude is noticeably higher. At minimum drag speed (VMD), the total power required is at its lowest. Below VMD, you enter the 'region of reversed command' or 'back of the drag curve' where slowing down actually requires MORE power to maintain level flight, because induced drag is rising faster than parasite drag is falling. This is where speed is unstable and requires active monitoring.
exam_tip: Below VMD: region of reversed command -- slowing down requires more power, not less. Higher speed = lower alpha = lower attitude. Lower speed = higher alpha = higher attitude = more power needed.
key_facts: High speed: low alpha, low attitude, parasite drag dominates
Low speed: high alpha, high attitude, induced drag dominates
VMD: minimum total power required for level flight
Below VMD: reversed command region -- slower speed needs more power
Power and attitude change together as speed varies in level flight

syllabus_desc: state the power required formula (power = drag x TAS);
concept: The power required formula is straightforward: Power = Drag x TAS. Since drag varies with speed (the U-shaped drag curve), and you're multiplying by speed, the power required curve has a different shape from the drag curve. At low speeds, even though drag is high (induced drag dominating), TAS is low, so the power required is moderate. At high speeds, drag is high again (parasite drag dominating) and TAS is high, so power required is very high.
