The minimum of the power required curve occurs at a lower speed than the minimum of the drag curve. This is because multiplying by the increasing TAS shifts the minimum to the left (slower).
exam_tip: Power = Drag x TAS. The power required curve minimum (VMP) is always slower than the drag curve minimum (VMD) because multiplying by increasing TAS shifts the minimum leftward.
key_facts: Power required = Drag x TAS (True Airspeed)
The power required curve has a different shape from the drag curve
The minimum of the power curve (VMP) is slower than the minimum of the drag curve (VMD)
At very low speeds: moderate drag x low TAS = moderate power
At very high speeds: high drag x high TAS = very high power

syllabus_desc: explain the difference between the drag curve and the power required curve;
concept: The drag curve plots total drag force against IAS and is U-shaped with its minimum at VMD. The power required curve plots power (= drag x TAS) against IAS and has a similar shape but shifted. The key differences: the minimum of the power required curve occurs at VMP, which is slower than VMD. The power curve rises more steeply at high speed (because you're multiplying the already-high drag by an increasingly high TAS). The drag curve is more symmetrical around its minimum. From the drag curve you read off the speed for minimum drag (maximum range). From the power curve you read off the speed for minimum power (maximum endurance). Both curves shift with weight and altitude changes.
exam_tip: Drag curve minimum = VMD (best range speed). Power curve minimum = VMP (best endurance speed). VMP is always slower. The power curve rises more steeply at high speed than the drag curve.
key_facts: Drag curve: U-shaped, minimum at VMD
Power required curve: similar shape but minimum at VMP (slower than VMD)
Power curve rises more steeply at high speeds due to TAS multiplication
Drag curve minimum: best range speed (approximately, for piston aircraft)
Power curve minimum: best endurance speed and best rate of climb speed

syllabus_desc: distinguish between the minimum drag speed and the minimum power speed.
concept: VMD (minimum drag speed) and VMP (minimum power speed) are different speeds for good reason. VMD is where total drag is least -- flying at this speed means the retarding force is minimised, so each unit of fuel carries you the furthest distance (best range). VMP is where the rate of doing work against drag is least -- flying here means fuel burn rate is minimised, so your fuel lasts the longest time (best endurance). VMP is always slower than VMD, typically by about 25-30%. The relationship exists because Power = Drag x TAS; even though drag at VMP is higher than at VMD, the lower TAS more than compensates, giving less power required overall. For a piston aircraft, maximum range speed is close to VMD, and maximum endurance speed is close to VMP.
exam_tip: VMD = best range (minimum fuel per distance). VMP = best endurance (minimum fuel per time). VMP is about 76% of VMD (or roughly 3/4 of VMD). Know which speed optimises which performance parameter.
key_facts: VMD: minimum drag, best range speed (minimum fuel/distance)
VMP: minimum power, best endurance speed (minimum fuel/time)
VMP is always slower than VMD (approximately 76% of VMD)
At VMD: parasite drag = induced drag
At VMP: the ratio of induced to parasite drag is 3:1

syllabus_desc: the maximum and minimum speeds for level flight;
concept: Plotting power available (from the engine/propeller combination) and power required (from the drag curve multiplied by TAS) on the same graph gives you the aircraft's speed envelope. The maximum level flight speed occurs where the power available curve intersects the power required curve at the high-speed end -- beyond this point, the engine doesn't have enough power to overcome the drag. The minimum level flight speed occurs either at the low-speed intersection (where again power available just equals power required on the 'back of the drag curve') or at the stalling speed, whichever is higher. For most light aircraft, the stalling speed is higher than the power-limited minimum speed, so the stall defines the low-speed boundary. Between these two speeds, excess power is available for climbing.
exam_tip: Max speed: high-speed intersection of PA and PR. Min speed: low-speed intersection of PA and PR, or stall speed (whichever is higher). Between them: excess power available for climbing.
key_facts: Maximum speed: where power available just meets power required at high speed
Minimum speed: where PA meets PR at low speed, or stall speed (whichever is higher)
Between max and min speed: excess power is available
Excess power at any speed = PA minus PR at that speed
Maximum excess power occurs near VMP (approximately) and determines best rate of climb

syllabus_desc: the effects of increased weight and altitude.
concept: Increased weight shifts the power required curve upward and to the right -- you need more power at every speed, and VMD/VMP both increase. This narrows the gap between the power available and power required curves, reducing excess power for climbing and reducing the maximum speed. Increased altitude reduces the power available from a normally aspirated engine (thinner air = less fuel/air mixture = less power). It also shifts the power required curve to the right (higher TAS needed for the same IAS). The combined effect at altitude narrows the performance envelope further. At some altitude, the power available curve just touches the power required curve at a single point -- that's the absolute ceiling, where no excess power exists and no further climb is possible.
exam_tip: Increased weight: PR curve shifts up and right, max speed decreases, ceiling decreases. Increased altitude: PA decreases, PR curve shifts right, envelope narrows. At absolute ceiling: PA = PR at one point only.
key_facts: Increased weight: power required increases, VMD/VMP increase, excess power decreases
Increased altitude: power available decreases (NA engine), TAS for same IAS increases
Both effects narrow the speed envelope and reduce climb performance
Absolute ceiling: PA just touches PR at one speed -- no excess power, no climb possible
Service ceiling: where rate of climb drops to 100 fpm

syllabus_desc: Identify the forces acting in a steady climb.
concept: In a steady climb, the forces acting are lift, weight, thrust, and drag -- but their arrangement is different from level flight. Weight still acts vertically downward. Thrust acts along the flight path (inclined upward). Drag acts opposite to the flight path (inclined downward from horizontal). Lift acts perpendicular to the flight path. In a climb, a component of weight acts along the flight path opposing thrust. This means thrust must overcome both drag AND this weight component: T = D + W sin(climb angle). Meanwhile, lift only needs to support the component of weight perpendicular to the flight path: L = W cos(climb angle). So in a climb, lift is actually less than weight, and thrust is greater than drag. This surprises many students, but it makes sense -- the engine is literally pulling the aircraft uphill.
exam_tip: In a climb: T = D + W sin(gamma), L = W cos(gamma). Lift is LESS than weight in a climb. Thrust must overcome drag PLUS a component of weight along the flight path.
key_facts: Weight component along flight path opposes thrust: W sin(climb angle)
Weight component perpendicular to flight path is balanced by lift: L = W cos(climb angle)
Thrust must exceed drag by W sin(climb angle) for steady climb
Lift is less than weight in a climb
The steeper the climb angle, the more thrust must exceed drag

syllabus_desc: how a curve of excess power available (EP) can be derived;
concept: The excess power (EP) curve is derived by subtracting the power required from the power available at each speed. At very low and very high speeds, EP is zero or negative (the engine can't produce enough power for level flight). At speeds between the min and max level flight speeds, EP is positive -- this excess can be used to climb. The shape of the EP curve typically shows a peak somewhere in the mid-speed range. For a piston-propeller aircraft, where power available is roughly constant with speed, the EP peak occurs near the minimum power required speed. The EP curve is a powerful tool: it tells you the rate of climb available at every speed, and its peak tells you the best rate of climb speed.
exam_tip: EP = PA - PR at each speed. The EP curve peaks at or near VMP for a piston aircraft. The peak gives the speed for best rate of climb (Vy).
key_facts: EP = PA - PR at each airspeed
EP is zero at the max and min level flight speeds
EP is positive between these speeds (available for climbing)
Peak EP occurs near VMP for piston-propeller aircraft
Rate of climb (ROC) = excess power / weight (approximately)

syllabus_desc: where the speeds for maximum rate of climb, and maximum angle of climb occur on the EP curve.
concept: Maximum rate of climb (Vy) occurs at the speed where excess power is greatest -- the peak of the EP curve. For a piston-propeller aircraft, this is near but slightly above VMP. Maximum angle of climb (Vx) occurs at a slower speed -- it's where the ratio of excess thrust (or the climb gradient) is maximised. On the PA/PR graph, Vx is found where the greatest vertical distance exists between the thrust available and thrust required curves (not the power curves). Vx is always slower than Vy. On a graph, you can find Vx by drawing a tangent from the origin to the PR curve -- the tangent point gives the speed for best angle of climb. Vy is at the peak of the EP curve. Both speeds increase slightly with altitude, and they converge at the absolute ceiling where both equal the only speed at which level flight is possible.
exam_tip: Vy: peak of EP curve (best ROC). Vx: where excess thrust is maximum (best angle). Vx < Vy always. Both increase with altitude and converge at the absolute ceiling.
key_facts: Best ROC (Vy): speed at peak of EP curve
Best angle (Vx): speed where excess thrust is greatest
Vx is always slower than Vy
Both speeds increase with altitude
At absolute ceiling, Vx = Vy = only possible level flight speed

syllabus_desc: State what the Vx and Vy speeds are and differentiate between these speeds and the normal climb speed.
concept: Vx is the speed for best angle of climb -- it gives the most height gained per unit of horizontal distance. Use Vx when you need to clear an obstacle after take-off, like trees at the end of a short runway. Vy is the speed for best rate of climb -- it gives the most height gained per unit of time. Use Vy when you want to reach your cruise altitude as quickly as possible with no obstacles to worry about. Vx is always slower than Vy. The reason for the difference: Vx maximises the climb gradient (height per distance), which depends on excess thrust. Vy maximises the climb rate (height per time), which depends on excess power. Since power = thrust x speed, these peak at different speeds. The conditions where each is used are different, and using the wrong one in the wrong situation can be costly.
exam_tip: Vx = best angle (obstacle clearance), uses excess thrust. Vy = best rate (fastest altitude gain), uses excess power. Vx < Vy. Use Vx for obstacles, Vy for expediting climb.
key_facts: Vx: best angle of climb, maximum height per horizontal distance
Vy: best rate of climb, maximum height per unit time
Vx is always slower than Vy
Vx: for obstacle clearance after take-off
Vy: for reaching cruise altitude as quickly as possible

syllabus_desc: Define absolute ceiling and service ceiling.
concept: The absolute ceiling is the altitude at which the aircraft can no longer climb at all -- the rate of climb has dropped to zero. At this altitude, the power available just equals the power required at one single speed, and there's no excess power for climbing. The absolute ceiling is a theoretical value because it would take infinite time to actually reach it (the rate of climb asymptotically approaches zero). The service ceiling is a more practical measure -- it's the altitude where the rate of climb has decreased to 100 feet per minute for piston aircraft (or 500 fpm for turbine aircraft in some definitions). Above the service ceiling, the aircraft can technically still climb, but so slowly that it's not practically useful. Both ceilings decrease with increased weight, higher temperature, and lower pressure.
exam_tip: Absolute ceiling: ROC = 0 fpm (theoretical). Service ceiling: ROC = 100 fpm for pistons (practical). Both decrease with increased weight, temperature, and lower pressure.
key_facts: Absolute ceiling: rate of climb = 0, PA just touches PR at one point
Service ceiling: rate of climb = 100 fpm (piston) or 500 fpm (some turbine definitions)
Absolute ceiling is theoretical -- takes infinite time to reach
Both ceilings decrease with increased weight, higher temperature, and lower pressure
At the ceiling, Vx = Vy = only possible level flight speed

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors affecting climb performance (power applied, airspeed, flap and/or undercarriage extension, weight, altitude, temperature, manoeuvring, and wind - in relation to net flight path).
concept: Climb performance is affected by many factors. Power applied: more power means more excess power for climbing -- obviously. But note that on the 'back of the power curve', reducing speed below Vy actually reduces ROC. Airspeed: flying at Vy gives the best ROC; faster or slower reduces it. Flap configuration: flaps increase drag more than they increase lift at climb speeds, reducing excess power. Take-off with minimal flap setting for obstacle clearance, then retract once clear. Altitude: power available decreases with altitude (NA engine), so climb rate drops progressively. Weight: heavier aircraft have less excess power (more PR at every speed) and a lower ceiling. Temperature: hot days reduce air density, reducing both engine power and propeller efficiency. Wind: doesn't affect ROC (which is relative to the air mass), but headwind improves climb gradient over the ground.
exam_tip: Climb performance degrades with: less power, altitude, weight, temperature, flaps. Wind doesn't affect ROC but headwind improves climb angle over the ground. Fly at Vy for best ROC.
key_facts: More power = more excess power = better climb
Vy gives maximum rate of climb; deviating from Vy reduces ROC
Flaps reduce climb performance due to increased drag
Higher altitude, weight, and temperature all reduce climb performance
Headwind improves ground climb gradient but not rate of climb

syllabus_desc: Identify the forces acting in a glide and a steady power-on descent.
concept: In a glide (power off), only three forces act: lift, drag, and weight. There's no thrust. Weight can be resolved into two components: one along the flight path (which replaces thrust as the driving force) and one perpendicular to the flight path (which lift balances). For a steady glide: L = W cos(glide angle), and D = W sin(glide angle). The aircraft descends because the component of weight along the flight path provides the energy to overcome drag. In a steady power-on descent, thrust is present but less than drag, so the aircraft descends at a controlled rate. The forces are the same as in a climb but with thrust less than drag -- the weight component along the flight path makes up the difference.
exam_tip: Glide: L = W cos(gamma), D = W sin(gamma), no thrust. The weight component along the flight path drives the aircraft forward. Power-on descent: thrust is less than drag.
key_facts: Glide: three forces only -- lift, drag, weight (no thrust)
Weight component along flight path replaces thrust
L = W cos(glide angle), D = W sin(glide angle)
Glide angle depends on L/D ratio: tan(glide angle) = D/L = 1/(L/D)
Power-on descent: thrust present but less than drag

syllabus_desc: Explain the connection between L/D ratio, glide angle, airspeed and gliding range.
concept: The glide angle and L/D ratio are directly connected. In a steady glide, tan(glide angle) = drag / lift = 1 / (L/D ratio). So the flattest glide angle occurs at the best L/D ratio, which occurs at the speed for minimum drag (VMD). A typical training aircraft with L/D of 9:1 has a best glide angle of about 6.3 degrees, meaning it covers about 9 units of horizontal distance for every 1 unit of height lost. From 3,000 feet AGL, that's a glide range of about 4.5 NM in still air. The airspeed for best glide is VMD -- the speed where total drag is minimum. Flying faster or slower than best glide speed steepens the glide angle and reduces glide range. This is the speed you'd fly for maximum distance in an engine failure.
exam_tip: Best glide angle at VMD (best L/D ratio). Glide angle = arctan(1/L/D). L/D 9:1 = approximately 6.3 degree glide angle. Glide range from height = height x L/D ratio.
key_facts: Glide angle depends only on L/D ratio: tan(gamma) = 1/(L/D)
Best (shallowest) glide angle occurs at best L/D (VMD)
Best glide speed = VMD (speed for minimum drag)
Glide range in still air = height x L/D ratio
L/D ratio and therefore glide angle are independent of weight (at best glide speed)

syllabus_desc: weight on glide angle and best glide speed;
concept: Weight does not change the best glide angle -- the L/D ratio at the best glide speed is a property of the airframe, independent of weight. However, a heavier aircraft must fly at a higher speed to achieve the best L/D ratio. Best glide speed varies as the square root of the weight ratio: if weight increases by 21%, best glide speed increases by 10%. A heavier aircraft glides at the same angle but faster, arriving at the ground sooner. It covers the same horizontal distance from the same height (same angle), but it gets there quicker and touches down at a higher speed. This matters for forced landing planning -- the glide range is the same, but the landing roll will be longer.
exam_tip: Weight doesn't change the best glide angle or glide range (same L/D). It does increase best glide speed (square root of weight ratio) and descent rate. Heavier = same distance, faster arrival.
key_facts: Best glide angle is independent of weight (L/D ratio unchanged)
Best glide speed increases with square root of weight increase
Heavier aircraft: same glide angle, higher speed, higher sink rate
Glide range in still air is the same regardless of weight
Landing speed and landing roll increase with weight

syllabus_desc: wind on net flight path.
concept: Wind doesn't affect the aircraft's performance through the air mass, but it profoundly affects the flight path over the ground. A headwind reduces the ground distance covered during the glide -- the aircraft is being blown backward relative to the ground, steepening the ground track angle. A tailwind increases the ground distance -- the aircraft is carried forward, flattening the effective glide angle over the ground. For maximum range in a headwind, you should fly slightly faster than the still-air best glide speed (this minimises the time spent being blown backward). In a tailwind, fly slightly slower than best glide speed (this maximises the time the wind is helping). But these are fine adjustments -- the main point is that wind changes your glide range over the ground.
exam_tip: Headwind: reduces ground glide range, fly slightly faster than best glide speed. Tailwind: increases ground range, fly slightly slower. The air distance is unchanged by wind.
key_facts: Headwind reduces glide range over the ground
Tailwind increases glide range over the ground
The glide performance through the air mass is unaffected by wind
In a headwind: fly slightly faster than best glide speed for best ground range
In a tailwind: fly slightly slower than best glide speed for best ground range

syllabus_desc: Explain how the required turning (centripetal) force is generated.
concept: In a turn, the aeroplane needs a centripetal force directed toward the centre of the turn. This is generated by banking the aircraft. When the wings are banked, the lift vector tilts from vertical. The vertical component of lift still supports the aircraft's weight, while the horizontal component provides the centripetal force for the turn. The greater the bank angle, the more the lift vector is tilted, and the greater the centripetal force. But here's the catch: for the vertical component of the tilted lift to still equal weight, the total lift must increase. That's why you need to increase angle of attack (back pressure) in a turn -- to generate more total lift so the vertical component still supports the weight.
exam_tip: Centripetal force in a turn = horizontal component of lift = L sin(bank). Vertical component L cos(bank) must still equal weight. Total lift must increase in a turn.
key_facts: Centripetal force comes from the horizontal component of banked lift
Centripetal force = L sin(bank angle) = W tan(bank angle)
Vertical component of lift must still equal weight: L cos(bank) = W
Total lift required = W / cos(bank angle)
Bank angle alone determines the required load factor in a steady turn

syllabus_desc: Define load factor.
concept: Load factor (n) is the ratio of lift to weight: n = L/W. In straight and level flight, n = 1 (1g). In a turn, because total lift must increase to maintain the vertical component equal to weight, the load factor increases. Specifically, n = 1 / cos(bank angle). At 30 degrees bank, n = 1.15. At 45 degrees, n = 1.41. At 60 degrees, n = 2.0. At 75 degrees, n = 3.86. The load factor represents the apparent increase in weight that the wing and airframe must support. It's what you feel as G-force in the cockpit. Exceeding the aircraft's structural load factor limits (typically +3.8g and -1.52g for normal category) risks structural failure.
exam_tip: n = 1/cos(bank). 30 degrees = 1.15g, 45 degrees = 1.41g, 60 degrees = 2g, 75 degrees = 3.86g. Normal category limits: +3.8g / -1.52g. Know these values cold.
key_facts: Load factor n = L/W = 1/cos(bank angle)
30 degrees bank: n = 1.15g
45 degrees bank: n = 1.41g
60 degrees bank: n = 2.0g
Normal category structural limits: +3.8g / -1.52g

syllabus_desc: Describe how load factor increases with bank angle.
concept: Load factor increases non-linearly with bank angle, and it accelerates dramatically at steep bank angles. Up to about 30 degrees, the increase is gentle (1 to 1.15g). From 30 to 60 degrees, it doubles (1.15 to 2.0g). From 60 to 80 degrees, it nearly triples again (2.0 to 5.76g). The relationship is n = 1/cos(bank), and because cosine approaches zero as the angle approaches 90 degrees, the load factor theoretically goes to infinity at 90 degrees bank. This non-linear increase is why steep turns are so demanding: the structural loads, stall speed, and power requirements all escalate rapidly above 45-50 degrees of bank. A 60-degree bank is considered a steep turn for most GA operations.
exam_tip: Load factor vs bank is non-linear and accelerates at steep angles. The cosine function makes load factor increase slowly at first, then dramatically. 60+ degrees = steep turn territory.
key_facts: Load factor increases non-linearly with bank angle
Gentle increase up to 30 degrees; rapid acceleration above 45 degrees
At 90 degrees bank, load factor is theoretically infinite
The non-linearity explains why steep turns are so demanding
Every 15 degrees of additional bank adds increasingly more G-loading

syllabus_desc: Explain the connection between load factor and percentage increase in stalling speed.
concept: Since the stalling speed increases as the square root of the load factor, and load factor increases with bank angle, the stalling speed increases in a turn. The formula is: Vs(turn) = Vs(1g) x square root of n = Vs(1g) x square root of (1/cos(bank)). At 30 degrees bank: Vs increases by about 7%. At 45 degrees: 19%. At 60 degrees: 41%. This means if your level-flight stalling speed is 50 knots, in a 60-degree banked turn it's about 70.5 knots. The margin between your flying speed and the stall narrows dramatically in steep turns. This is why steep turns at low speed (think base-to-final turn) are so dangerous -- the stall speed has crept up toward your flying speed, and any additional load factor from turbulence or a steeper bank can trigger a stall.
exam_tip: Vs in a turn = Vs(1g) x sqrt(n) = Vs(1g) x sqrt(1/cos(bank)). At 60 degrees bank, stall speed is 41% higher. At 45 degrees, 19% higher. Critical for base-to-final turn safety.
key_facts: Stall speed increases as square root of load factor
At 30 degrees bank: stall speed increases ~7%
At 45 degrees bank: stall speed increases ~19%
At 60 degrees bank: stall speed increases ~41%
Base-to-final turn accidents often involve accelerated stalls from excessive bank

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors affecting rate and radius of turn.
concept: Rate of turn (how fast you change heading, in degrees per second) and radius of turn are determined by bank angle and TAS. Rate of turn = (g x tan(bank)) / V (in radians/sec, convertible to degrees/sec). Radius of turn = V squared / (g x tan(bank)). So rate of turn increases with steeper bank and decreases with higher speed. Radius of turn increases with higher speed (squared!) and decreases with steeper bank. A slow aircraft in a steep bank turns tightest and fastest; a fast aircraft in a shallow bank turns widest and slowest. These relationships explain why you can do a tight circuit at 80 knots that would require a huge pattern at 200 knots, and why steep turns at low speed can generate high G-loads.
exam_tip: Rate of turn increases with bank and decreases with speed. Radius increases with V squared and decreases with bank. Slow + steep bank = tightest turn. Speed is squared in the radius formula.
key_facts: Rate of turn = g x tan(bank) / TAS (increases with bank, decreases with speed)
Radius of turn = TAS squared / (g x tan(bank))
Speed is squared in the radius formula -- double speed = 4x the radius
Steeper bank gives higher rate and smaller radius
These formulae assume a coordinated, constant-altitude turn

syllabus_desc: Explain the conditions for a maximum rate/minimum radius turn.
concept: For a maximum rate turn (minimum radius), you want the steepest possible bank at the lowest possible speed. The limiting factor is either structural (G-limit) or aerodynamic (stall). At the manoeuvre speed (Va), these limits coincide: the aircraft reaches its structural G-limit at the stalling angle of attack simultaneously. Below Va, the aircraft stalls before reaching the G-limit, so the stall limits the turn. Above Va, the G-limit is reached before the stall, so the structural limit determines the maximum bank. The absolute tightest turn occurs at Va with bank angle at the structural limit -- this gives both maximum rate and minimum radius. For a normal category aircraft at Va, the 3.8g limit allows about 74.7 degrees of bank.
exam_tip: Tightest turn at Va: stall and structural limits coincide. Below Va: stall limits the turn. Above Va: structural limits the turn. At Va, n = 3.8g for normal category = ~74.7 degrees bank.
key_facts: Maximum rate/minimum radius turn occurs at Va
At Va: stall limit and structural G-limit coincide
Below Va: stall is the limiting factor
Above Va: structural G-limit is the limiting factor
Normal category at Va: 3.8g allows approximately 74.7 degrees bank

syllabus_desc: a constant-bank turn;
concept: In a constant-bank turn, the aircraft makes a perfect circle through the air mass at a uniform rate. But if there's wind, the air mass itself is moving. The ground track becomes a distorted shape -- wider on the downwind side (where groundspeed is higher) and tighter on the upwind side (where groundspeed is lower). The bank angle, load factor, and rate of turn (through the air) remain constant throughout. The only thing that changes is the ground track. This is what you get in a standard instrument turn or any turn where you hold constant bank angle. For most flying, this is acceptable -- the ground track distortion only matters when you need to fly a precise path over the ground.
exam_tip: Constant bank in wind: uniform circle through air mass, egg-shaped ground track. Wider downwind, tighter upwind. No change to bank angle, G-loading, or aerodynamic conditions.
key_facts: Constant bank gives a perfect circle relative to the air mass
Ground track becomes egg-shaped in wind
Wider radius over the ground on the downwind side
Tighter radius over the ground on the upwind side
Bank angle, load factor, and rate of turn through the air are all constant

syllabus_desc: a constant-radius turn around a ground feature.
concept: When you need to maintain a constant radius over the ground -- like flying a precise circle around a ground feature, or maintaining correct spacing on a circuit pattern in wind -- you must vary the bank angle throughout the turn. When the aircraft is heading downwind (groundspeed is high), you need steeper bank to prevent the wind from pushing you away from the feature. When heading upwind (groundspeed is low), you use shallower bank because the lower groundspeed naturally produces a tighter ground track. This means the load factor and stall speed vary throughout the turn -- higher when bank is steepest (downwind heading) and lower when bank is shallowest (upwind heading). This is important for circuit flying, where you need to manage these varying conditions.
exam_tip: Constant ground radius in wind: steepest bank when heading downwind (high GS), shallowest when heading upwind (low GS). The stall speed and G-loading vary throughout the turn.
key_facts: Steeper bank needed when heading downwind (higher groundspeed)
Shallower bank when heading upwind (lower groundspeed)
Load factor varies throughout the turn
Stall speed varies with the changing load factor
Most critical when downwind: steepest bank, highest stall speed, highest load

syllabus_desc: the effect on rate of climb/descent;
concept: In a climbing turn, the rate of climb decreases because the extra lift needed for the turn comes at the expense of excess power available for climbing. The wing must produce more total lift (to generate centripetal force plus support weight), which means more induced drag, which means more power is consumed overcoming drag and less is available for climbing. The steeper the bank, the greater the rate of climb reduction. In a descending turn, the same increased drag effect applies, but now it increases the rate of descent rather than decreasing it. In both cases, you should limit bank angle to preserve performance -- typically 15-20 degrees in the climb and moderate angles in the descent to maintain control and prevent excessive speed buildup or loss.
exam_tip: Climbing turn: ROC decreases because extra lift for the turn increases drag, consuming excess power. Descending turn: descent rate increases. Limit bank in climbing turns to 15-20 degrees.
key_facts: Climbing turn: rate of climb decreases due to increased drag from extra lift
Descending turn: rate of descent increases for the same reason
Steeper bank = greater performance penalty
Limit bank to 15-20 degrees in climbing turns to preserve performance
The load factor increases drag and consumes power that would otherwise go to climbing

syllabus_desc: the tendency to overbank/underbank.
concept: In a climbing turn, the outer (rising) wing travels faster than the inner wing, producing more lift on the outside. This creates a tendency to overbank -- the aircraft wants to roll into a steeper bank than you're holding. You need gentle opposite aileron to prevent the bank from increasing. In a descending turn, the situation reverses depending on the type of descent. In a powered descending turn at normal speeds, the same overbanking tendency applies. In a gliding descending turn, the inner (lower) wing may be at a higher angle of attack due to the descending relative airflow, which can create an underbanking tendency in some conditions. The key is to be aware of these tendencies and manage the bank angle with appropriate aileron input.
exam_tip: Climbing turns: overbanking tendency (outer wing faster = more lift). Descending turns: can be overbanking or underbanking depending on conditions. Monitor and correct bank actively.
key_facts: Climbing turn: overbanking tendency (outer wing moves faster, more lift)
Need opposite aileron to maintain desired bank in climbing turn
Descending turn: overbanking tendency in powered descent
Gliding descending turn: underbanking tendency possible in some conditions
Actively manage bank angle in all climbing and descending turns

syllabus_desc: Describe the forces acting during a manoeuvre in the looping plane; and
concept: In a manoeuvre in the looping plane (such as a pull-up or loop), the aircraft follows a curved path in the vertical plane. At the bottom of the curve (pulling up from a dive or at the bottom of a loop), the centripetal force required to curve the flight path upward must come from the lift exceeding the weight. The load factor at the bottom of a loop is: n = (V squared / (g x r)) + 1, where the +1 accounts for gravity. At the top of a loop, gravity assists the centripetal force, so n = (V squared / (g x r)) - 1. At a 'zero g' top of a loop, the aircraft maintains the curved path using gravity alone and the apparent weight is zero. The forces in looping manoeuvres can generate very high G-loads, especially at high speed and tight radius.
exam_tip: Bottom of loop: n = V squared/(g x r) + 1. Top of loop: n = V squared/(g x r) - 1. Load factor is highest at the bottom of a pull-up. Speed must be managed to stay within G-limits.
key_facts: In the looping plane, centripetal force comes from lift minus/plus weight component
Bottom of loop/pull-up: n = V squared/(g x r) + 1 (gravity opposes centripetal)
Top of loop: n = V squared/(g x r) - 1 (gravity assists centripetal)
Highest load factor occurs at the bottom of a pull-up
Speed and radius must be managed to stay within structural limits

syllabus_desc: Identify the factors affecting the radius of a looping manoeuvre.
concept: The radius of a looping manoeuvre depends on the speed and the load factor (which is controlled by the pull force). From the centripetal force equation: r = V squared / (g x (n - 1)) at the bottom of a loop, and r = V squared / (g x (n + 1)) at the top. So the radius increases with speed squared (faster = wider loop) and decreases with higher load factor (more G = tighter loop). For a given structural G-limit, the minimum radius loop occurs at the manoeuvre speed (Va) -- below Va, you can't pull enough G without stalling; above Va, the higher speed widens the radius even though you can pull the same G. The minimum loop radius is limited by either the structural G-limit or the stalling alpha, whichever is reached first.
exam_tip: Loop radius = V squared / (g x (n-1)) at bottom. Tighter loops: lower speed and higher G. Minimum radius at Va where stall and structural limits coincide.
key_facts: Loop radius = V squared / (g x (n +/- 1)) depending on position in loop
Higher speed = wider radius (V is squared)
Higher load factor = tighter radius
Minimum loop radius at Va (stall and G-limit coincide)
Below Va: stall limits G; above Va: higher speed widens the loop

syllabus_desc: Describe design manoeuvre speed (Va) and explain the features of a typical V-n (or V-g) diagram.
concept: Design manoeuvre speed (Va) is the maximum speed at which you can apply full abrupt control deflection without exceeding the structural limits of the airframe. At Va, the aircraft will stall before the structural limit is exceeded -- the stall acts as a 'safety valve' that limits the G-load. Above Va, the aircraft can generate enough lift to exceed structural limits before stalling, so abrupt control inputs risk structural failure. Va decreases with reduced weight (lighter = stalls at a lower speed = lower Va). The V-n diagram (also called the manoeuvring envelope or flight envelope) plots load factor against speed and shows all the structural and aerodynamic limits in one graph. It includes the positive and negative stall boundaries (curved lines), the structural G-limits (horizontal lines), and Va, Vne (never-exceed speed), and other reference speeds.
exam_tip: Va: full control deflection won't exceed structural limits (wing stalls first). Va decreases with lighter weight. The V-n diagram shows the complete flight envelope -- stall curves, G-limits, and speed limits.
key_facts: Va: maximum speed for full abrupt control deflection
At Va: wing stalls before structural limits are reached
Above Va: structural failure possible from abrupt inputs before stalling
Va decreases with reduced weight
V-n diagram plots load factor vs speed showing the complete manoeuvre envelope

syllabus_desc: Define blade face, blade angle, pitch (or helix) angle, helical twist, angle of attack.
concept: A propeller blade is essentially a rotating aerofoil, and it has its own terminology. The blade face is the flat (or nearly flat) surface facing forward -- the 'thrust' side. The blade back is the curved surface facing aft. The blade angle is the angle between the blade's chord line and its plane of rotation. The pitch angle (or helix angle) is the angle between the blade's chord line and the relative airflow at a given blade station. Helical twist means the blade angle varies along the span -- greater near the root and smaller near the tip. This twist ensures that each section of the blade meets the air at a similar angle of attack despite the fact that the tip moves much faster than the root. The angle of attack of each blade section is the difference between the blade angle and the helix angle. Blade thrust is the forward component of the aerodynamic force on the blade.
exam_tip: Blade angle is geometric (fixed for fixed-pitch). Helix angle depends on the helix of the airflow. Blade AOA = blade angle minus helix angle. Helical twist keeps AOA similar along the span.
key_facts: Blade face: flat side facing forward (thrust side)
Blade angle: angle between chord line and plane of rotation
Helix angle: angle between relative airflow and plane of rotation
Blade AOA = blade angle - helix angle
Helical twist: blade angle decreases from root to tip to equalise AOA

syllabus_desc: Describe the forces acting on a propeller blade; the rpm/airspeed relationship; and the most effective blade sections.
concept: The forces on a propeller blade are similar to those on a wing. The total aerodynamic reaction force on each blade section can be resolved into thrust (the forward component, perpendicular to the plane of rotation) and torque (the component opposing rotation, in the plane of rotation). The ratio of RPM to airspeed determines the angle at which air approaches the blade (the helix angle). At low speed and high RPM (like take-off), the helix angle is small, giving a large blade AOA and high thrust. At high speed and the same RPM, the helix angle increases, reducing blade AOA. If the advance per revolution is too great, the blade AOA becomes too small and thrust drops off. If the advance per revolution matches the geometric pitch, the blade is at zero AOA (zero thrust). Beyond that, the blade produces negative thrust (drag). Propeller efficiency = (thrust x TAS) / shaft power, and it peaks at a specific speed/RPM combination.
exam_tip: Propeller blade AOA depends on the ratio of forward speed to RPM. High speed/low RPM = large helix angle = low blade AOA = low thrust. Efficiency peaks at a specific advance ratio.
key_facts: Blade forces: thrust (forward) and torque (opposing rotation)
Helix angle increases with forward speed (at constant RPM)
Higher helix angle reduces blade AOA and thrust
Propeller efficiency = (thrust x TAS) / shaft power
Peak efficiency occurs at a specific advance ratio (V/nD)

syllabus_desc: Explain how propeller pitch affects efficiency at different speeds.
concept: A fixed-pitch propeller is designed to be efficient at one particular speed/RPM combination. At that design speed, the blade angle gives the ideal angle of attack for maximum efficiency. At speeds below the design speed (like take-off), the blade AOA is too high -- the blade is working hard but inefficiently, and the engine tends to over-rev if not throttled back. At speeds above the design speed (like a descent), the blade AOA is too low or even negative -- the propeller isn't producing much thrust and may even windmill. This is the fundamental limitation of a fixed-pitch prop: it's a compromise that can only be optimised for one flight condition. It's like being stuck in one gear of a car -- fine on the flat, but inefficient uphill and downhill.
exam_tip: Fixed-pitch prop: optimised for one speed. Below design speed: blade AOA too high, high torque, tendency to over-rev. Above design speed: blade AOA too low, low thrust, low efficiency.
key_facts: Fixed-pitch prop is efficient at only one speed/RPM combination
Below design speed: blade AOA too high, engine tends to over-rev
Above design speed: blade AOA too low, thrust drops off
Like being stuck in one gear -- a compromise across the speed range
Climb props: fine pitch (low blade angle) for take-off/climb; cruise props: coarser pitch

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the constant-speed (variable pitch) propeller.
concept: The constant-speed (variable-pitch) propeller solves the fixed-pitch problem by automatically adjusting the blade angle to maintain a pilot-selected RPM. Think of it as an automatic gearbox for the propeller. At low speed (take-off), the blades move to fine pitch (low blade angle) to keep the blade AOA optimal and allow the engine to develop maximum power at its best RPM. At high speed (cruise), the blades move to coarse pitch (high blade angle) to prevent the engine from over-revving and to maintain efficiency at the faster airflow. The result is higher efficiency across the entire speed range, better take-off performance, better cruise economy, and the ability to use the engine's best power RPM in every flight condition.
exam_tip: Constant-speed prop: automatically adjusts blade angle to maintain selected RPM. Fine pitch at low speed (take-off), coarse pitch at high speed (cruise). Higher efficiency across all flight conditions.
key_facts: Automatically adjusts blade angle to maintain pilot-selected RPM
Fine pitch (low blade angle) at low forward speed (take-off/climb)
Coarse pitch (high blade angle) at high forward speed (cruise)
Gives optimal efficiency across the entire speed range
The CSU (constant speed unit) governor controls blade angle automatically

syllabus_desc: Describe in broad terms the operation of the constant speed unit (CSU) with changes in power setting and airspeed.
concept: The constant speed unit (CSU) governor senses engine RPM and adjusts the propeller blade angle to maintain the RPM set by the pilot. If a change in conditions (speed, power, altitude) tends to increase RPM, the governor increases blade angle (coarsens the pitch), which increases the load on the engine and brings RPM back to the set value. If RPM tends to decrease, the governor decreases blade angle (fines the pitch), reducing the load and allowing RPM to recover. When the pilot changes the power lever (manifold pressure), the engine produces more or less torque, but the governor maintains constant RPM by adjusting blade angle. When speed changes (e.g., levelling off from a climb), the changing helix angle would change RPM, but the governor compensates by adjusting pitch.
exam_tip: CSU governor: senses RPM, adjusts blade angle to maintain it. RPM rising = coarsen pitch. RPM falling = fine pitch. Power changes alter manifold pressure; the governor maintains RPM.
key_facts: CSU governor senses RPM and adjusts blade angle automatically
RPM rising above set value: governor coarsens pitch (increases blade angle)
RPM falling below set value: governor fines pitch (decreases blade angle)
Power lever changes manifold pressure; prop lever sets desired RPM
The governor maintains constant RPM despite changes in speed, power, or altitude

syllabus_desc: Describe the correct procedure for handling manifold pressure and propeller controls.
concept: With a constant-speed propeller, there's a specific procedure for handling manifold pressure (MP) and RPM to protect the engine. When increasing power: increase RPM first (prop lever forward), then increase manifold pressure (throttle forward). This ensures the engine is running at a higher RPM before the extra load of higher MP is applied. When reducing power: reduce manifold pressure first (throttle back), then reduce RPM (prop lever back). This prevents the combination of high MP and low RPM, which creates high cylinder pressures and can cause detonation and engine damage. The general rule is: never let manifold pressure in inches exceed RPM in hundreds. For example, at 2300 RPM, don't exceed 23 inches MP.
exam_tip: Increasing power: RPM first, then MP. Decreasing power: MP first, then RPM. Never let MP in inches exceed RPM in hundreds (e.g., 2300 RPM max 23 inches MP). Protects against detonation.
key_facts: Increasing power: increase RPM first, then increase MP
Decreasing power: decrease MP first, then decrease RPM
Prevents high MP at low RPM (detonation risk)
Rule of thumb: MP in inches should not exceed RPM in hundreds
These procedures protect the engine from harmful cylinder pressures

syllabus_desc: windmilling;
concept: A windmilling propeller is being driven by the airflow rather than by the engine. The blade angle is too small for the current airspeed -- the relative airflow hits the blade at a negative angle of attack, and the aerodynamic force on the blade drives it to rotate. The engine is being turned by the propeller (like push-starting a car). A windmilling prop creates significant drag because the blades are effectively flat plates being pushed through the air, plus the engine is absorbing energy through compression and friction. In a multi-engine aircraft, a windmilling prop on the failed engine creates enormous asymmetric drag -- feathering the propeller is critical to reduce this drag and maintain controllability.
exam_tip: Windmilling: airflow drives the prop, blade at negative AOA, high drag. In a multi-engine aircraft, windmilling drag on the failed engine is the primary controllability concern. Feather promptly.
key_facts: Windmilling: propeller driven by airflow, not by engine
Blade at negative AOA -- airflow pushes the blade around
Creates significant drag (aerodynamic + engine friction)
Extremely problematic in multi-engine: massive asymmetric drag
Feathering the prop eliminates most of this drag

syllabus_desc: feathered;
concept: A feathered propeller has its blades turned to approximately 90 degrees blade angle -- edge-on to the airflow. In this position, the blades present minimum frontal area to the airflow and produce essentially zero thrust and minimum drag. Feathering is used after an engine failure in a multi-engine aircraft to minimise the drag from the inoperative engine's propeller. The difference between a windmilling prop and a feathered prop in terms of drag is dramatic -- feathering can reduce the drag from the failed engine by 80% or more. The feathering mechanism is typically spring-loaded or counterweight-driven to move the blades to feather when oil pressure is lost (as happens when the engine fails), with a manual feather button or lever as a backup.
exam_tip: Feathered: blades at ~90 degrees (edge-on), minimum drag, zero thrust. Reduces failed-engine drag by 80%+. Essential action after engine failure in multi-engine aircraft.
key_facts: Feathered: blades at approximately 90 degrees, edge-on to airflow
Produces minimum drag and zero thrust
Reduces failed-engine drag by approximately 80% compared to windmilling
Essential after engine failure in multi-engine aircraft
Feathering mechanism may be spring-loaded, counterweight, or manual

syllabus_desc: in reverse thrust.
concept: Reverse thrust is achieved by rotating the propeller blades past the flat (zero) pitch position to a negative blade angle. In this configuration, the propeller produces thrust in the rearward direction, acting as an aerodynamic brake. The airflow is pushed forward rather than backward. Reverse thrust is used on the ground after landing to assist braking, particularly on wet or contaminated runways where wheel braking alone may be insufficient. It's not available on all propeller aircraft -- it requires a propeller system capable of moving the blades through flat pitch to negative angles. In flight, reverse thrust is generally not used (and may be prohibited) because it creates unusual handling characteristics and high structural loads on the propeller mechanism.
exam_tip: Reverse thrust: blades past flat pitch to negative angle, thrust acts rearward. Used on ground for braking. Not normally used in flight. Requires specific prop system capability.
key_facts: Blades rotated past flat pitch to negative blade angle
Produces rearward-acting thrust for braking on the ground
Used after landing, especially on wet or contaminated runways
Not normally used in flight -- unusual handling and structural concerns
Requires a propeller system capable of negative (reverse) pitch

syllabus_desc: Explain propeller centrifugal and aerodynamic twisting moments.
concept: Two opposing twisting moments act on a propeller blade in flight. The centrifugal twisting moment (CTM) tends to rotate the blade toward fine pitch (flat). This happens because the centrifugal force on each tiny mass element of the blade acts outward from the hub, and because the blade's centre of mass is ahead of the pitch-change axis, this outward force creates a moment that twists the blade toward fine pitch. The aerodynamic twisting moment (ATM) tends to rotate the blade toward coarse pitch. The centre of pressure of each blade section is ahead of the pitch-change axis, and the aerodynamic force acting at the CP creates a moment that twists the blade toward coarser pitch. In most operating conditions, CTM is stronger than ATM, so the net tendency is toward fine pitch -- which is why constant-speed props use oil pressure to increase pitch (coarsen) and spring/counterweight forces to decrease pitch (fine).
exam_tip: CTM: toward fine pitch (centrifugal force effect). ATM: toward coarse pitch (aerodynamic force effect). CTM usually dominates. CSU uses oil pressure to coarsen, counterweights/springs to fine.
key_facts: CTM (centrifugal twisting moment): twists blade toward fine pitch
ATM (aerodynamic twisting moment): twists blade toward coarse pitch
CTM is usually stronger than ATM in normal flight
Net tendency is toward fine pitch
CSU uses oil pressure to move blades to coarse; loss of oil pressure drives toward fine

syllabus_desc: Describe asymmetric blade effect.
concept: Asymmetric blade effect (also called P-factor) occurs when the propeller disc is at an angle to the relative airflow -- typically at high angles of attack during slow flight or climbing. The descending blade (on the right side for a clockwise-rotating prop as seen from the cockpit) has a higher effective angle of attack and a higher resultant velocity than the ascending blade on the left. This means the descending blade produces more thrust than the ascending blade. The centre of thrust shifts from the propeller hub toward the descending blade side. For a clockwise-rotating prop, this means the centre of thrust moves to the right, creating a yawing moment to the left. This is one of the propeller effects that contribute to swing on take-off and require rudder correction during slow flight at high power.
exam_tip: Asymmetric blade effect: descending blade has higher AOA and velocity, produces more thrust. For clockwise prop (viewed from cockpit): thrust shifts right, yaw left. Most noticeable at high alpha and high power.
key_facts: Occurs when prop disc is at an angle to relative airflow (high alpha)
Descending blade: higher effective AOA and velocity = more thrust
Ascending blade: lower effective AOA and velocity = less thrust
Centre of thrust shifts toward descending blade side
For clockwise prop: yaw to the left; corrected with right rudder

syllabus_desc: Explain the terms power absorption and propeller solidity.
concept: Power absorption is the ability of a propeller to convert engine shaft power into useful thrust. A propeller that can absorb more power produces more thrust (up to its efficiency limit). Power absorption depends on blade area, number of blades, RPM, and blade design. Larger blade area or more blades absorb more power. If an engine is upgraded to produce more power, the existing propeller may not be able to absorb it all -- the excess power is wasted. Propeller solidity is the ratio of the total blade area to the disc area (the area swept by the propeller). Higher solidity means more blade area relative to the disc, better power absorption, and generally higher thrust -- but also more drag when not producing thrust. Adding blades (going from 2 to 3 or 4) increases solidity and power absorption, which is why higher-powered aircraft use multi-blade propellers.
exam_tip: Power absorption: propeller's ability to use engine power for thrust. Solidity = total blade area / disc area. More blades or larger blades = higher solidity = better power absorption.
key_facts: Power absorption: ability to convert shaft power into thrust
Depends on blade area, number of blades, RPM, and design
Solidity = total blade area / propeller disc area
Higher solidity = better power absorption but more drag
Multi-blade propellers are used on high-power engines for adequate power absorption

syllabus_desc: Explain static stability and dynamic stability.
concept: Stability describes an aircraft's tendency to return to its original condition after a disturbance. Static stability is the initial tendency: does the aircraft start moving back toward equilibrium (positive), continue away (negative), or stay where it ended up (neutral)? Dynamic stability describes what happens over time: does the aircraft oscillate with decreasing amplitude back to equilibrium (positive dynamic stability), oscillate with increasing amplitude (negative -- divergent oscillation), or oscillate at constant amplitude (neutral)? An aircraft can be statically stable but dynamically unstable -- it initially moves back toward equilibrium but overshoots and each oscillation gets worse. For safe flight, you want positive static AND positive dynamic stability.
exam_tip: Static stability: initial tendency after disturbance (positive = returns). Dynamic stability: behaviour over time (positive = oscillations decrease). Need both positive for safe flight.
key_facts: Static stability: initial response to disturbance
Positive static: tends to return to original state
Negative static: tends to diverge further from original state
Dynamic stability: response over time (oscillation behaviour)
Positive dynamic: oscillations decrease; negative dynamic: oscillations increase

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between stability and controllability.
concept: Stability and controllability (manoeuvrability) are opposing design requirements. A very stable aircraft strongly resists displacement from its trimmed condition -- good for hands-off flying but requiring more effort and larger control inputs to manoeuvre. A less stable (more manoeuvrable) aircraft responds readily to control inputs but may not return to its trimmed condition without pilot input. Fighter aircraft are designed with low stability (some are even statically unstable, relying on computers) for quick response. Training and transport aircraft are designed with positive stability for safety and predictability. The designer must compromise: too much stability makes the aircraft sluggish and hard to manoeuvre; too little makes it demanding to fly and potentially dangerous.
exam_tip: More stability = less manoeuvrability (and vice versa). Training/transport aircraft favour stability. Aerobatic/military aircraft favour manoeuvrability. It's always a design trade-off.
key_facts: Stability and controllability are opposing requirements
More stability = less manoeuvrability (harder to manoeuvre)
Less stability = more manoeuvrability (but harder to fly hands-off)
Training aircraft: positive stability for safety
The designer must find the right compromise for the aircraft's intended role

syllabus_desc: the action of the tailplane in maintaining longitudinal stability;
concept: The tailplane (horizontal stabiliser) is the primary source of longitudinal stability. When a gust pitches the aircraft nose-up, the angle of attack of the wing increases, but so does the angle of attack of the tailplane. The tailplane produces an increased downward force (or decreased upload), creating a nose-down restoring moment that tends to return the aircraft to its original attitude. The effectiveness of the tailplane depends on its area, its distance from the CG (the moment arm), and the dynamic pressure it experiences. A larger tail area and longer moment arm give more stability. The tailplane also experiences downwash from the wing, which reduces its effective angle of attack -- this is accounted for in the design.
exam_tip: Tailplane provides the restoring moment in pitch. Nose-up disturbance = increased tail download = nose-down restoring moment. Effectiveness depends on tail area x moment arm x dynamic pressure.
key_facts: Tailplane produces a restoring pitching moment after disturbances
Nose-up gust: tailplane increases its download, creating nose-down moment
Effectiveness depends on tail area, moment arm, and dynamic pressure
Longer moment arm (CG-to-tail distance) gives more stability per unit tail force
Wing downwash affects the tailplane's effective angle of attack

syllabus_desc: wing pitching moments;
concept: The wing itself produces pitching moments that affect longitudinal stability. A cambered wing has an inherent nose-down pitching moment about its aerodynamic centre (approximately the quarter-chord point). As angle of attack changes, the wing's pitching moment about the AC remains approximately constant, but the lift force changes, which alters the moment about the CG if the CG isn't at the AC. With the CG ahead of the AC (typical configuration), an increase in lift creates a nose-up moment about the CG -- this is destabilising. The wing alone is generally unstable or neutral in pitch for cambered aerofoils. The tailplane must provide enough stabilising moment to overcome the wing's destabilising tendency and produce overall positive longitudinal stability.
exam_tip: Wing pitching moment about the AC is roughly constant with alpha. With CG ahead of AC, wing lift increase is destabilising (nose-up). The tailplane must overcome this for net stability.
key_facts: Cambered wing has an inherent nose-down pitching moment about the AC
Wing lift changes create pitching moments about the CG
CG ahead of AC: lift increase is destabilising (nose-up moment about CG)
The wing alone is generally unstable or neutral in pitch
Tailplane must overcome the wing's destabilising contribution

syllabus_desc: the effect of CG position.
concept: CG position is the single most important variable affecting longitudinal stability. As the CG moves forward, the moment arm between the CG and the tailplane increases, giving the tail more leverage. The wing's destabilising moment arm (CG to AC) decreases. Both effects increase stability. As the CG moves aft, the opposite happens: the tail's moment arm decreases and the wing's destabilising arm increases -- stability decreases. At some point (the aft CG limit), the aircraft becomes neutrally stable -- the tailplane can no longer provide a net restoring moment. Beyond this, the aircraft becomes unstable in pitch, which is extremely dangerous. The forward CG limit is set by the requirement for adequate elevator authority to flare for landing.
exam_tip: Forward CG = more stable, heavier stick forces, higher stall speed. Aft CG = less stable, lighter forces, lower stall speed. Aft limit: neutral stability point. Forward limit: elevator authority for landing.
key_facts: Forward CG: more stable, heavier control forces, longer tail moment arm
Aft CG: less stable, lighter control forces, shorter tail moment arm
Aft CG limit: set by neutral stability (no restoring moment in pitch)
Forward CG limit: set by adequate elevator authority for landing flare
CG must remain within limits for safe flight

syllabus_desc: Define directional stability and explain the factors affecting it.
concept: Directional stability is stability in yaw about the normal (vertical) axis. It's the tendency of the aircraft to weathervane back to its original heading after a yaw disturbance. The primary source is the fin (vertical stabiliser). When a gust yaws the aircraft, the fin is presented at an angle to the airflow, creating a sideways aerodynamic force that produces a restoring yawing moment. The larger the fin and the further aft it is from the CG, the stronger the restoring moment. The fuselage ahead of the CG acts as a destabilising influence because it tends to weathervane the nose further away from the original heading. The fin must be large enough to overcome this destabilising fuselage effect. Dorsal fins and strakes help prevent the fin from stalling at large sideslip angles.
exam_tip: Directional stability: yaw about normal axis, provided mainly by the fin. Fin area and moment arm determine strength. Forward fuselage is destabilising. Dorsal fins prevent fin stall at large sideslip.
key_facts: Directional stability: tendency to return to original heading after yaw disturbance
Primary source: fin (vertical stabiliser)
Fin effectiveness depends on area and distance from CG
Forward fuselage is destabilising in yaw (weathervane effect)
Dorsal fins and strakes help at large sideslip angles

syllabus_desc: Define lateral stability and explain the factors affecting it (dihedral, shielding, wing position, keel surface/fin area, sweepback).
concept: Lateral stability is stability in roll about the longitudinal axis. When a disturbance rolls the aircraft and the pilot doesn't correct it, the aircraft sideslips toward the lower wing (gravity pulls it sideways). Lateral stability mechanisms convert this sideslip into a restoring roll back toward wings-level. The main factors are: dihedral (wings angled upward from root to tip) -- in a sideslip, the lower wing has a higher effective AOA and produces more lift, rolling the aircraft back to level. High wing position -- acts like dihedral because the CG is below the wing, creating a pendulum-like restoring effect. Sweepback -- the forward (lower) wing in a sideslip has a higher effective span perpendicular to the flow, creating more lift. The keel effect from high fuselage and fin surfaces above the CG also contributes.
exam_tip: Lateral stability factors: dihedral (most important), high wing, sweepback, keel effect. All work by converting sideslip into a restoring roll. Dihedral effect is the general term for this roll-from-sideslip response.
key_facts: Lateral stability: tendency to return to wings-level after roll disturbance
Works through sideslip: disturbance causes roll, roll causes sideslip, sideslip creates restoring roll
Dihedral: lower wing in sideslip has higher effective AOA = more lift
High wing position: pendulum effect, CG below wing
Sweepback and keel effect also contribute to lateral stability

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirement to match lateral and directional stability.
concept: Lateral and directional stability must be matched in strength for good handling qualities. If lateral stability is too strong relative to directional stability, the aircraft will respond to a yaw disturbance with an excessive roll correction. This can lead to dutch roll -- an oscillatory coupling of yaw and roll where the aircraft waddles from side to side. If directional stability is too strong relative to lateral stability, the aircraft will yaw back to heading efficiently but may not roll the wings level, potentially leading to spiral divergence (a slowly tightening descending spiral). The ideal is a balanced match where yaw and roll disturbances are damped together without excessive coupling or divergence.
exam_tip: Lateral stability too strong vs directional: dutch roll tendency. Directional too strong vs lateral: spiral instability. The two must be matched for good handling.
key_facts: Lateral and directional stability must be matched in strength
Too much lateral relative to directional: dutch roll tendency
Too much directional relative to lateral: spiral instability tendency
The balance affects handling qualities and pilot workload
Designers adjust fin size, dihedral, and wing design to achieve the right match

syllabus_desc: Explain the conditions of spiral instability, dutch roll, and snaking.
concept: Spiral instability occurs when directional stability is stronger than lateral stability. After a yaw disturbance, the aircraft yaws back to heading (good directional stability) but the wing-down attitude persists (weak lateral stability). The banked aircraft then turns, which creates more sideslip, which causes more yaw correction but not enough roll correction, and the bank angle slowly increases. The aircraft enters a slowly tightening descending spiral with increasing speed and bank. It's not dramatic -- it develops gradually, often unnoticed in IMC. Dutch roll is the opposite: lateral stability dominates. After a disturbance, the aircraft oscillates in a combined yaw-roll motion. Snaking is a yaw-only oscillation (directional stability overshooting), often caused by turbulence or a fin that's slightly too responsive.
exam_tip: Spiral instability: bank slowly increases, tightening descending turn -- dangerous in IMC. Dutch roll: yaw-roll oscillation, feels like waddling. Snaking: yaw oscillation only.
key_facts: Spiral instability: directional > lateral stability; slowly tightening descending spiral
Dutch roll: lateral > directional stability; combined yaw-roll oscillation
Snaking: yaw oscillation, directional instability or weak damping
Spiral instability is the more dangerous -- develops insidiously in IMC
Dutch roll is uncomfortable but usually not as dangerous

syllabus_desc: the importance of CG position;
concept: CG position relative to the main wheels is critical for ground stability. In a nosewheel aircraft, the CG is ahead of the main wheels. If the aircraft starts to swerve, the main wheel reaction produces a moment that brings the nose back toward the original direction -- this is directionally stable on the ground. In a tailwheel aircraft, the CG is behind the main wheels. If a swerve starts, the momentum of the heavy CG behind the pivot point tends to swing the tail further around -- this is directionally unstable (like pushing a shopping trolley backward). This is why tailwheel aircraft are more demanding to taxi and take-off/land, and why ground loops are a tailwheel hazard.
exam_tip: CG ahead of main wheels (nosewheel): stable on ground. CG behind main wheels (tailwheel): unstable on ground (ground loop tendency). This is why tailwheel aircraft need more rudder skill.
key_facts: Nosewheel: CG ahead of main wheels = directionally stable on ground
Tailwheel: CG behind main wheels = directionally unstable (ground loop tendency)
Ground loop: uncontrolled yaw causing the aircraft to spin around on the ground
Tailwheel aircraft require more skill and attention to directional control
CG-to-mainwheel relationship is the key to understanding ground stability

syllabus_desc: the differences between nosewheel and tailwheel configurations;
concept: Nosewheel aircraft are inherently directionally stable on the ground because the CG is ahead of the main wheels. A swerve creates a restoring moment. The nosewheel also provides direct steering through rudder pedal linkage or differential braking. Nosewheel aircraft are easier to taxi, take-off, and land, which is why most training aircraft use this configuration. Tailwheel aircraft have the CG behind the main wheels, making them directionally unstable. Any swerve tends to amplify into a ground loop if not corrected immediately with rudder. Tailwheel aircraft typically have better prop clearance, shorter take-off runs (the inclined attitude helps), and are more common on rough strips, but they demand constant attention to directional control during all ground operations.
exam_tip: Nosewheel: stable, easier handling, CG ahead of mains. Tailwheel: unstable, ground loop risk, CG behind mains, requires constant rudder attention. Most trainers are nosewheel for good reason.
key_facts: Nosewheel: CG ahead of main wheels, directionally stable, easier to handle
Tailwheel: CG behind main wheels, directionally unstable, ground loop risk
Nosewheel advantages: stability, easier training, better forward visibility on ground
Tailwheel advantages: better prop clearance, shorter take-off, rough strip capability
Tailwheel requires constant rudder input and attention during ground operations

syllabus_desc: handling of controls in strong crosswinds.
concept: In a crosswind on the ground, the wind tries to weathervane the aircraft into wind (yaw), lift the upwind wing (roll), and push the aircraft sideways. Correct control positioning is essential. During taxi in a crosswind, turn the control column into the wind (aileron into wind) and hold neutral or slight forward elevator (to keep the nosewheel or tailwheel firmly on the ground). On take-off in a crosswind, start with full into-wind aileron and progressively reduce it as speed builds and the controls become more effective. On landing in a crosswind, use either the crab method (approach crabbed into wind, kick straight just before touchdown) or the wing-low method (lower the upwind wing and hold opposite rudder throughout the approach). After touchdown, progressively increase into-wind aileron and control positioning as speed decreases.
exam_tip: Crosswind taxi: aileron into wind, forward elevator. Take-off: start with full into-wind aileron, reduce as speed builds. Landing: crab or wing-low method. After landing: increase into-wind controls as speed decreases.
key_facts: Crosswind tries to weathervane, lift upwind wing, and push aircraft sideways
Taxi: control column into wind (aileron up on upwind side)
Take-off: start full into-wind aileron, reduce progressively with increasing speed
Landing: crab or wing-low method, depending on technique
After touchdown: increase into-wind controls as speed decreases

syllabus_desc: For a single-engine propeller aircraft, explain the factors affecting swing on take-off.
concept: On take-off, a single-engine propeller aircraft tends to swing (yaw) to one side due to several propeller effects acting together. Torque reaction: the engine turns the prop clockwise (from the cockpit), and the equal and opposite reaction tends to roll the aircraft to the left, putting more weight on the left wheel and creating more friction on that side, yawing left. Slipstream effect: the helical slipstream spirals around the fuselage and strikes the fin from the left side, pushing the tail right and yawing the nose left. Asymmetric blade effect (P-factor): at the high angle of attack during take-off, the descending blade (right side) produces more thrust, yawing left. Gyroscopic precession: during the take-off rotation (pitching nose-up), the spinning propeller reacts 90 degrees in the direction of rotation, creating a yaw to the left. All four effects combine to produce a left yaw tendency on aircraft with clockwise-rotating propellers.
exam_tip: Four causes of swing: torque reaction, slipstream effect, asymmetric blade (P-factor), gyroscopic precession. All cause left yaw for a clockwise prop. Corrected with right rudder on take-off.
key_facts: Torque reaction: rolls left, more weight on left wheel, yaws left
Slipstream: spirals around fuselage, hits fin from left, yaws left
P-factor: descending blade (right) has more thrust, yaws left
Gyroscopic precession: nose-up pitch input creates left yaw
All four effects require right rudder correction during take-off

syllabus_desc: Describe cross-wind take-off and landing techniques.
concept: Crosswind take-offs and landings require specific techniques to maintain directional control and prevent drift. For take-off: hold full into-wind aileron initially, use rudder to keep straight, and progressively reduce aileron deflection as speed increases. Rotate at the normal speed but be ready for drift after liftoff -- establish a crab angle or maintain a wing-low correction. For landing, the two main techniques are the crab (fly a heading that compensates for drift, then align with the runway just before touchdown) and the wing-low (bank slightly into wind with opposite rudder to prevent turning, tracking straight down the runway). The wing-low method is generally preferred for light aircraft because you touch down already aligned with the runway. Maximum demonstrated crosswind components are in the POH -- they're advisory, not a hard limit, but respect them.
exam_tip: Crosswind take-off: aileron into wind, reduce with speed, track centreline with rudder. Crosswind landing: crab or wing-low (bank into wind, opposite rudder). Know maximum demonstrated crosswind for your type.
key_facts: Take-off: into-wind aileron, rudder for directional control, reduce aileron as speed builds
Two landing techniques: crab method and wing-low (sideslip) method
Wing-low: lower upwind wing, opposite rudder to maintain track
Crab: approach crabbed into wind, align just before touchdown
Respect the maximum demonstrated crosswind component

syllabus_desc: Explain ground effect, and relate it to take-off and landing.
concept: Ground effect is the increase in aerodynamic efficiency that occurs when the aircraft flies within approximately one wingspan of the surface. The ground disrupts the formation of wingtip vortices, reducing induced downwash and therefore induced drag. Lift is effectively increased (or the same lift is produced at a lower angle of attack) and induced drag is reduced -- sometimes by up to 50% when very close to the surface. On take-off, an aircraft may become airborne in ground effect but not have enough performance to climb out of it -- it can fly along in ground effect but sinks back if it tries to climb away. On landing, ground effect can cause the aircraft to float further down the runway than expected because the reduced drag and increased lift at the same airspeed make the aircraft reluctant to stop flying. Understanding ground effect is essential for accurate take-off and landing planning.
exam_tip: Ground effect: within one wingspan of surface, induced drag decreases up to 50%, lift increases. Take-off: may become airborne early but can't climb out. Landing: aircraft floats, extending landing distance.
key_facts: Ground effect occurs within approximately one wingspan of the surface
Reduces induced drag by up to 50% when very close to the ground
Reduces induced downwash, effectively increasing lift
Take-off: aircraft may fly in ground effect but not climb out of it
Landing: aircraft floats, potentially overshooting the touchdown point

syllabus_desc: Explain the consideration involved in coping with asymmetric thrust/drag and reduced power.
concept: When one engine fails on a multi-engine aircraft, the remaining engine produces thrust on one side while the failed engine produces drag (especially if the propeller is windmilling). This creates a massive yawing moment toward the failed engine. Additionally, the asymmetric drag creates a rolling moment toward the dead engine side. The pilot must immediately apply rudder to counter the yaw and aileron to prevent the roll. The key considerations are: which engine has failed (the critical engine produces the worst situation), maintaining directional control, reducing drag from the failed engine (feathering the prop), and maintaining adequate airspeed -- never letting speed decay below Vmca. The reduction in performance is dramatic: loss of one engine on a twin doesn't halve the climb performance, it can reduce it by 80% or more because you've lost half the power but still have most of the drag plus extra asymmetric drag.
exam_tip: Engine failure on multi: massive yaw toward dead engine, rolling moment, dramatic performance loss. Feather the failed engine's prop ASAP. Never go below Vmca. Loss of one engine = ~80% loss of climb performance.
key_facts: Asymmetric thrust creates yawing and rolling moments toward the failed engine
Windmilling propeller on failed engine creates enormous additional drag
Feathering the prop reduces this drag by approximately 80%
Loss of one engine reduces climb performance by approximately 80%, not 50%
Maintaining speed above Vmca is critical for directional control

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors affecting yawing and rolling moments.
concept: The yawing moment from an engine failure depends on the thrust of the operating engine multiplied by the arm from the aircraft centreline to the engine. More thrust and a wider engine spacing create a larger yawing moment. The rolling moment is caused by two factors: the asymmetric lift from the slipstream of the operating engine (it washes over one wing but not the other), and the yaw itself causing one wing to move faster than the other (the advancing wing generates more lift). Both the yawing and rolling moments are worse at high power settings (more thrust asymmetry), at low speeds (less rudder and aileron effectiveness to counteract the moments), and with a windmilling propeller (maximum drag asymmetry). Managing these moments requires prompt rudder and aileron inputs and maintaining adequate airspeed.
exam_tip: Yawing moment = thrust x arm from centreline. Worse at high power, low speed, windmilling prop. Rolling moment from differential slipstream and yaw-induced speed difference. High speed = more control authority to manage.
key_facts: Yawing moment = operating engine thrust x distance from centreline
Rolling moment from differential slipstream and yaw-induced asymmetric lift
Worse at high power (more thrust asymmetry)
Worse at low speed (less control authority)
Windmilling prop maximises the drag asymmetry

syllabus_desc: Define critical engine.
concept: The critical engine is the engine whose failure creates the most adverse yawing moment and is therefore hardest to control. On conventional twins with both engines rotating in the same direction (clockwise from the cockpit), the left engine is the critical engine. Here's why: both engines produce P-factor (asymmetric blade effect), which shifts the centre of thrust toward the descending blade. On the left engine, the descending blade is inboard (close to centreline), so its thrust is close to the CG. On the right engine, the descending blade is outboard (far from centreline), so its thrust acts at a greater arm from the CG. When the left engine fails, the right engine (with its greater thrust arm) creates the worst yawing moment. On counter-rotating engines (each engine rotates in opposite directions), there is no critical engine because both have the same thrust arms.
exam_tip: Critical engine: the one whose failure produces the worst yaw. For same-rotation props (both clockwise), the left engine is critical. Counter-rotating props: no critical engine. P-factor determines which arm is longer.
key_facts: Critical engine: the one whose failure creates the most adverse yaw
For both-clockwise props: left engine is critical (right engine has longer thrust arm)
P-factor shifts centre of thrust toward the descending blade
Counter-rotating propellers eliminate the critical engine concept
