The critical engine determines the most demanding engine-failure scenario

syllabus_desc: Recall immediate actions and techniques for identifying the failed engine.
concept: The immediate actions after an engine failure follow the 'dead foot, dead engine' principle. When an engine fails, the aircraft yaws toward the failed engine. You instinctively apply rudder to stop the yaw, and the foot that's NOT pushing a rudder pedal is on the failed-engine side. Hence 'dead foot, dead engine.' The immediate actions are: maintain directional control (full rudder as needed), identify the failed engine (dead foot, dead engine), verify by retarding the suspected dead engine's throttle (if performance doesn't change, you've correctly identified it), then feather the propeller on the confirmed dead engine. The sequence is: control, identify, verify, feather. Never rush to feather without verifying -- shutting down the wrong engine turns a manageable single-engine situation into a no-engine emergency.
exam_tip: Dead foot = dead engine. Sequence: control (rudder), identify (dead foot), verify (throttle check), feather. NEVER feather without verifying -- shutting the wrong engine is catastrophic.
key_facts: Dead foot, dead engine: the foot not pushing rudder indicates the failed side
Sequence: control, identify, verify, feather
Verify by retarding the throttle on the suspected dead engine
If no performance change: correct identification confirmed
Never feather without verification -- wrong engine shutdown is catastrophic

syllabus_desc: Explain the three modes of constant-heading asymmetric flight.
concept: There are three modes of constant-heading asymmetric flight, each with different trade-offs. Wings-level with rudder: the aircraft is held on heading with rudder alone, wings level. The sideslip required creates extra drag and the raised dead-engine wing reduces the climb-limiting effect of the yaw, but the asymmetric lift from the banked slipstream is not being used. This gives the simplest control but the most drag. Bank toward the live engine with rudder: the aircraft is banked slightly (typically 3-5 degrees) toward the operating engine. This uses the horizontal component of lift to help counter the yaw, reducing the rudder required and the sideslip, which reduces drag. This is the most efficient configuration and gives the best climb performance. Zero sideslip: the aircraft is flown with zero sideslip (balanced) using a combination of bank and rudder. This also gives good performance and is used as the reference for Vmca determination.
exam_tip: Three modes: wings level (most drag), bank toward live engine (best performance), zero sideslip (reference for Vmca). Bank 3-5 degrees toward the live engine for best single-engine climb performance.
key_facts: Wings level with rudder: simplest but most drag from sideslip
Bank toward live engine (3-5 degrees): least drag, best climb performance
Zero sideslip: balanced, used as reference for Vmca
The bank-toward-live-engine technique reduces rudder required and sideslip drag
Best single-engine performance requires the correct technique for the aircraft type

syllabus_desc: Define Vmca and Vmcg.
concept: Vmca is the minimum control speed in the air -- the lowest speed at which it's possible to maintain directional control with one engine inoperative, the operating engine at maximum power, and no more than 5 degrees of bank toward the operating engine. Below Vmca, the rudder cannot produce enough force to counteract the yawing moment from the asymmetric thrust, and the aircraft will yaw uncontrollably toward the dead engine. Vmca is typically marked as a red radial line on the ASI. Vmcg is the minimum control speed on the ground -- the minimum speed during the take-off roll at which directional control can be maintained on the runway with one engine failed. Vmcg is usually lower than Vmca because the wheels provide additional directional control through nosewheel steering and differential braking. Both speeds increase with more power on the live engine, aft CG, and lower altitude.
exam_tip: Vmca: minimum airborne control speed, one engine out, max power, 5 degrees bank. Red line on ASI. Vmcg: minimum ground control speed. Vmcg < Vmca. Never fly below Vmca with an engine failed.
key_facts: Vmca: minimum control speed in the air, one engine inoperative
Defined with max power on live engine, failed engine feathered or windmilling, 5 degrees bank max
Vmcg: minimum control speed on the ground (lower than Vmca)
Below Vmca: uncontrollable yaw toward dead engine
Both speeds increase with more power, aft CG, lower altitude, and windmilling (vs feathered)

syllabus_desc: Define specific air range (SAR) and specific fuel consumption (SFC).
concept: Specific air range (SAR) is the distance covered per unit of fuel consumed -- typically expressed as nautical miles per kilogram or per litre of fuel. A higher SAR means you go further on each unit of fuel. Specific fuel consumption (SFC) is the fuel consumed per unit of power output per unit of time -- typically expressed as kg per kW per hour (or pounds per HP per hour). A lower SFC means the engine is more efficient at converting fuel into power. SAR depends on both the airframe efficiency (L/D ratio) and the engine efficiency (SFC). For maximum range, you want both the best L/D ratio from the airframe and the lowest SFC from the engine. These two requirements may not occur at the same speed, so the practical best range speed is a compromise.
exam_tip: SAR = distance per unit fuel (higher is better). SFC = fuel per unit power per hour (lower is better). Best range needs both good L/D and low SFC. These may not optimise at the same speed.
key_facts: SAR (specific air range): distance per unit fuel (NM/kg or NM/litre)
SFC (specific fuel consumption): fuel per unit power per unit time (kg/kW/hr)
Higher SAR = more efficient range performance
Lower SFC = more efficient engine
Best range requires optimising both airframe efficiency (L/D) and engine efficiency (SFC)

syllabus_desc: State the general conditions for achieving maximum SAR.
concept: For maximum SAR (best range), you need to fly at the speed that gives the most distance per unit of fuel. For a piston-propeller aircraft where SFC is roughly constant with speed, this means flying at the speed for minimum drag (VMD), which gives the best L/D ratio. However, in practice, the best range speed is typically slightly faster than VMD because SFC improves slightly at slightly higher power settings, and the region around VMD is speed-unstable (back of the drag curve). For a jet aircraft (where fuel flow is roughly proportional to thrust), the best range speed is faster than VMD -- it occurs where the product of speed and L/D ratio is maximised. Other conditions for maximum range include flying at the optimum altitude (typically high), leaning the mixture correctly, and minimising weight.
exam_tip: Piston aircraft: best range near VMD (best L/D). Jet: best range faster than VMD (best V x L/D). Fly high, lean correctly, minimise weight for maximum range.
key_facts: Best range requires maximum distance per unit fuel (maximum SAR)
Piston aircraft: best range speed is near VMD (best L/D ratio)
Jet aircraft: best range speed is faster than VMD (maximum V x L/D)
Flying at optimum altitude improves range (lower density = less parasite drag)
Correct mixture leaning and minimum weight also optimise range

syllabus_desc: Explain the airframe and engine considerations of flying for range (piston engine).
concept: For a piston-propeller aircraft, range is determined by the interaction of airframe and engine considerations. The airframe wants to fly at the speed for best L/D (VMD) to minimise drag and therefore thrust required. The engine's SFC varies somewhat with power setting -- it's typically best at about 45-65% power. Since power = drag x TAS, and drag is minimum at VMD, the best range speed often ends up slightly above VMD where the engine is operating at a more efficient power setting. The propeller also has a peak efficiency at a certain advance ratio. Altitude helps because parasite drag decreases with density while the IAS (and therefore lift/drag relationship) can be maintained at a higher TAS. However, normally aspirated engines lose power with altitude, so there's an optimum altitude beyond which the engine can't maintain the required power. The practical best range profile is a cruise-climb at reducing weight as fuel burns off.
exam_tip: Piston range: fly near VMD (adjusted slightly for engine SFC), use correct altitude, lean the mixture, and cruise-climb as weight reduces. Higher altitude helps until the engine can't maintain required power.
key_facts: Airframe consideration: minimum drag speed (VMD) for best L/D
Engine consideration: SFC best at moderate power settings (45-65%)
Practical best range speed is slightly faster than VMD
Higher altitude reduces parasite drag but NA engines lose power
Optimum altitude exists where these factors balance; cruise-climb is ideal

syllabus_desc: Apply performance tables or graphs from an aircraft manual to determine best SAR under given conditions.
concept: Aircraft flight manuals (AFMs) and POHs provide performance tables or graphs for determining best range conditions for specific aircraft. These typically show fuel flow versus speed at various altitudes and power settings, allowing you to find the speed that gives the best nautical miles per unit of fuel. Some manuals present range profiles directly, showing the distance achievable at different speeds, altitudes, and weights. To use these tables: enter with your weight, altitude, and temperature to find the recommended power setting and airspeed for best range. Apply corrections for wind (headwind reduces ground range, tailwind increases it). Always cross-check that the recommended conditions are within the aircraft's operating limitations and that you have adequate fuel reserves. These tables are specific to the aircraft type and should be used for planning every cross-country flight.
exam_tip: Use the POH/AFM performance tables for your specific aircraft. Enter with weight, altitude, and temperature. Apply wind corrections. Always maintain required fuel reserves. Tables give the most accurate range data.
key_facts: AFM/POH provides aircraft-specific range performance data
Enter tables with weight, altitude, and temperature
Apply wind corrections (headwind reduces range, tailwind increases)
Cross-check that conditions are within operating limitations
Always maintain required fuel reserves per NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Define flying for endurance and differentiate between range flying and endurance flying (piston engine).
concept: Flying for endurance means maximising the time you can stay airborne on a given fuel load. This is different from range: range maximises distance, endurance maximises time. Endurance is determined by fuel quantity divided by fuel flow rate. To maximise endurance, you need the lowest possible fuel flow rate, which means the minimum power required. The speed for best endurance is therefore VMP (minimum power speed), which is slower than VMD (best range speed). For a piston aircraft, minimum power corresponds to minimum RPM and fuel flow. The key distinction: range flying = fly at VMD (minimum drag), endurance flying = fly at VMP (minimum power). VMP is about 76% of VMD. You'd fly for endurance when holding for weather to clear, waiting for a slot, or in an emergency requiring maximum time aloft.
exam_tip: Endurance = time in the air. Best endurance at VMP (minimum power speed). VMP is slower than VMD. Range = distance; endurance = time. Range at VMD, endurance at VMP.
key_facts: Endurance: maximum time airborne on available fuel
Best endurance at VMP (minimum power speed)
VMP is about 76% of VMD
For piston aircraft: minimum power = minimum fuel flow = maximum endurance
Range maximises distance (VMD); endurance maximises time (VMP)

syllabus_desc: State the factors affecting endurance and explain practical endurance flying techniques.
concept: Factors affecting endurance include fuel quantity (more fuel = more time), aircraft weight (heavier = more power needed = higher fuel flow), altitude (for a piston aircraft, lower altitude is generally better for endurance because the engine is more efficient at higher density), mixture leaning (proper leaning reduces fuel flow), and configuration (clean = less drag = less power needed). Practical endurance flying means flying at VMP with the correct power setting and mixture, at an altitude appropriate for the engine, and clean (no flaps, undercarriage up). Note that VMP increases with weight, so a heavier aircraft's best endurance speed is faster. As fuel burns and weight decreases, VMP decreases and you should slow down to maintain the optimum. Wind has no effect on endurance (only on range) because endurance is about time, not distance.
exam_tip: Endurance factors: fuel quantity, weight, altitude (low is better for piston), mixture leaning, configuration. Wind does NOT affect endurance (only range). Fly at VMP, clean, properly leaned.
key_facts: More fuel and lower weight improve endurance
Lower altitude generally better for piston endurance (engine efficiency)
Proper mixture leaning reduces fuel flow
Clean configuration (no flaps, gear up) minimises power required
Wind does not affect endurance -- only affects range

syllabus_desc: Take-off distance required (TODR), take-off safety speed, and screen height (or barrier);
concept: Take-off distance required (TODR) is the distance from the start of the take-off roll to the point where the aircraft reaches screen height above the runway surface. Screen height is typically 50 feet for light aircraft (35 feet for transport category). Take-off safety speed is the minimum speed at which the aircraft can safely become airborne and climb -- it provides adequate margin above the stall speed in the take-off configuration. TODR must be less than TODA for the take-off to be legal. These distances are calculated from the POH using the actual conditions of weight, altitude, temperature, wind, and runway surface.
exam_tip: TODR: from start of roll to screen height (50 ft for light, 35 ft for transport). TODR must be less than TODA. Safety speed provides stall margin in take-off configuration.
key_facts: TODR: distance from brake release to reaching screen height
Screen height: 50 ft (light aircraft) or 35 ft (transport category)
Take-off safety speed: minimum speed for safe climb-out with stall margin
TODR must not exceed TODA for a legal take-off
Calculated from POH using actual weight, altitude, temperature, wind, runway conditions

syllabus_desc: Take-off distance available (TODA) and clearway;
concept: Take-off distance available (TODA) is the physical distance available for the take-off, measured from the start of the take-off run to the end of any clearway. A clearway is an area beyond the runway end that is free of obstacles above a specified height and over which the aircraft can climb through the screen height. The clearway is NOT part of the runway -- you can't roll on it, but you can fly over it. TODA = TORA + clearway. If there's no clearway, TODA equals TORA. The clearway allows you to count the distance during which the aircraft is airborne and climbing toward screen height, giving you more take-off distance available without needing a longer runway.
exam_tip: TODA = TORA + clearway. Clearway: obstacle-free area beyond runway for climb-out (can fly over, can't roll on). No clearway means TODA = TORA.
key_facts: TODA = TORA + clearway length
Clearway: obstacle-free area beyond the runway end
Aircraft can fly over clearway but cannot use it for ground roll
If no clearway exists, TODA = TORA
TODA is the total distance available for the take-off to screen height

syllabus_desc: Take-off run available (TORA);
concept: Take-off run available (TORA) is the length of runway declared available for the ground run of the take-off. This is the physical runway length from the start of roll to the end of the runway suitable for take-off. TORA may be less than the total runway length if there are displaced thresholds or other restrictions. TORA is the distance available for the aircraft to accelerate on the ground before becoming airborne.
exam_tip: TORA = runway length available for ground roll. May be less than physical runway length due to displaced thresholds. TORA does not include clearway or stopway.
key_facts: TORA: runway length available for the take-off ground roll
Measured from brake release point to end of usable take-off runway
May be less than total runway length due to displaced thresholds
Does not include clearway or stopway
TORA is always less than or equal to TODA

syllabus_desc: Accelerate-stop distance available (ASDA) and stopway;
concept: Accelerate-stop distance available (ASDA) is the distance available for the aircraft to accelerate to the decision point and then stop in the event of a rejected take-off. ASDA = TORA + stopway. A stopway is a paved area beyond the end of the runway that's strong enough to support the aircraft during a rejected take-off but not necessarily suitable for normal operations. You can decelerate on a stopway but can't take off from it. If there's no stopway, ASDA equals TORA. The accelerate-stop distance required (ASDR) is the distance needed to accelerate to V1 (or the decision speed) and then brake to a stop. ASDR must not exceed ASDA.
exam_tip: ASDA = TORA + stopway. Stopway: paved area beyond runway for emergency stopping (can stop on, can't take off from). ASDR must not exceed ASDA.
key_facts: ASDA = TORA + stopway
Stopway: area beyond runway for deceleration during rejected take-off
Stopway must be capable of supporting the aircraft but need not meet runway standards
If no stopway, ASDA = TORA
ASDR (required) must not exceed ASDA for a legal take-off

syllabus_desc: Gradient and gross and net flight paths;
concept: Gradient is the ratio of height gained (or lost) to horizontal distance covered, expressed as a percentage. A 3% gradient means 3 feet of height gained for every 100 feet of horizontal distance. The gross flight path is the actual demonstrated performance of the aircraft type in certification testing. The net flight path is the gross flight path reduced by a safety margin specified in the regulations -- typically the gradient is reduced by a fixed percentage. The net flight path is what must be used for obstacle clearance calculations, ensuring that even with performance below the average test conditions, the aircraft can still clear obstacles. For single-engine aircraft, the gross climb gradient is the only one typically specified; net flight path is more relevant to multi-engine operations.
exam_tip: Gradient = height/distance as %. Gross flight path = actual demonstrated performance. Net flight path = gross minus safety margin. Use NET for obstacle clearance calculations.
key_facts: Gradient: height gained per horizontal distance, expressed as percentage
Gross flight path: actual demonstrated aircraft performance
Net flight path: gross path reduced by regulatory safety margin
Net flight path must clear all obstacles in the take-off/climb path
Net path accounts for performance below the average demonstrated level

syllabus_desc: Landing distance available (LDA), landing distance required (LDR) and landing threshold;
concept: Landing distance available (LDA) is the runway length declared suitable for landing, measured from the threshold to the end of the runway. LDA may be less than the total runway length due to displaced thresholds or other restrictions. Landing distance required (LDR) is the distance needed from the screen height (50 ft or 35 ft) to come to a complete stop. Landing threshold speed is the target speed at the threshold, typically 1.3 times the stalling speed in the landing configuration (Vso). The LDR must not exceed the LDA. Performance calculations must account for the actual conditions: weight, altitude, temperature, wind, runway slope, and surface condition. Safety factors are applied to demonstrated landing distances to provide margin for real-world conditions.
exam_tip: LDA: runway available for landing. LDR: distance from screen height to stop. LDR must not exceed LDA. Threshold speed typically 1.3 Vso. Safety factors are applied to demonstrated distances.
key_facts: LDA: runway length available from threshold to runway end
LDR: distance from screen height to complete stop
Landing threshold speed: typically 1.3 x Vso
LDR must not exceed LDA
Safety factors are applied to account for real-world variability

syllabus_desc: Dry, wet, and contaminated (in relation to runway surface);
concept: Runway surface condition significantly affects both take-off and landing performance. A dry runway provides full braking effectiveness and predictable friction. A wet runway reduces braking effectiveness by typically 15-30% and may require landing distance increases of 15-40% depending on the regulatory framework and aircraft type. A contaminated runway has standing water, slush, snow, or ice on the surface, which can dramatically reduce braking effectiveness and increase landing distances by 200-300% or more. Contamination also affects take-off through increased rolling resistance (slush/water) and reduced braking if a rejected take-off is needed. Performance data in the POH is usually for dry or wet conditions; contaminated conditions may require special calculations or techniques.
exam_tip: Dry: normal braking. Wet: braking reduced ~15-30%, landing distance increased 15-40%. Contaminated: braking dramatically reduced, landing distance can double or triple. Always factor runway condition into performance planning.
key_facts: Dry runway: full braking effectiveness, baseline performance
Wet runway: reduced braking, landing distance increased by 15-40%
Contaminated (water, slush, snow, ice): braking dramatically reduced
Contaminated runway landing distances can be 200-300%+ of dry figures
Always factor runway surface condition into take-off and landing calculations

syllabus_desc: Drift down.
concept: Drift down refers to the gradual descent of a multi-engine aircraft after an engine failure at altitude when the aircraft cannot maintain its current altitude on the remaining engine(s). After securing the failed engine, the aircraft drifts down from its cruise altitude at the best engine-out speed to the single-engine ceiling -- the maximum altitude at which level flight can be maintained on the remaining engine(s). Drift-down planning is important for routes that cross high terrain or have minimum safe altitudes above the single-engine ceiling. The drift-down flight path must be planned to ensure the aircraft can clear all obstacles along the route. The pilot must know the single-engine ceiling for the current weight, altitude, and temperature to plan the drift-down profile.
exam_tip: Drift down: gradual descent after engine failure when altitude can't be maintained. Must plan to clear obstacles during drift-down. Critical for routes over high terrain. Fly at best engine-out speed.
key_facts: Drift down: descent from cruise altitude after engine failure
Aircraft descends to the single-engine ceiling at best engine-out speed
Must be planned to ensure obstacle clearance throughout the descent
Critical consideration for routes over mountainous terrain
Single-engine ceiling depends on weight, temperature, and altitude

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors affecting take-off and landing performance.
concept: Take-off and landing performance are affected by numerous factors, most of which work through changes in air density, aircraft weight, or the forces acting during the ground roll and climb-out/approach. Weight: heavier = longer take-off distance (more speed needed), reduced climb gradient, and longer landing distance (more kinetic energy to dissipate). Altitude and temperature: higher altitude and temperature mean lower density, less engine power, less propeller efficiency, and higher TAS for the same IAS. Pressure altitude and temperature combine to give density altitude, which is the single best predictor of performance. Wind: headwind reduces ground speed at rotation, shortening the take-off distance; tailwind does the opposite. Runway slope: uphill increases take-off distance, downhill decreases it. Runway surface: grass or contaminated surfaces increase rolling friction (longer take-off) and reduce braking (longer landing). Flap setting: small flap reduces take-off distance by lowering Vs, but too much flap increases drag.
exam_tip: Performance degrades with: higher weight, altitude, temperature, tailwind, uphill slope, soft/contaminated surface. Headwind helps take-off and landing. Know how each factor affects TODR and LDR.
key_facts: Weight: heavier = longer distances (both take-off and landing)
Density altitude: combines pressure altitude and temperature effects
Headwind: reduces take-off and landing distance
Tailwind: increases take-off and landing distance significantly
Slope, surface condition, and flap setting all affect distances

syllabus_desc: Given an elevation, QNH and ambient temperature, calculate pressure altitude and density altitude.
concept: To calculate pressure altitude: take the aerodrome elevation and correct for the difference between the actual QNH and 1013 hPa. Each 1 hPa difference from 1013 equals approximately 30 feet of pressure altitude correction. If QNH is below 1013, pressure altitude is higher than field elevation (the atmosphere is 'lower' than standard). If QNH is above 1013, pressure altitude is lower than elevation. Formula: Pressure altitude = elevation + (1013 - QNH) x 30. Then to get density altitude, apply the temperature correction. If the actual temperature is higher than ISA for that pressure altitude, density altitude is higher than pressure altitude (about 120 ft per degree C above ISA). If cooler, density altitude is lower. Example: elevation 500 ft, QNH 1000 hPa, temperature 25C. Pressure altitude = 500 + (1013-1000) x 30 = 500 + 390 = 890 ft. ISA temp at 890 ft = 15 - (890/1000 x 2) = 13.2C. Actual is 25C, so ISA deviation = +11.8C. Density altitude approximately 890 + (11.8 x 120) = 2,306 ft.
exam_tip: PA = elevation + (1013 - QNH) x 30. DA = PA + (temperature deviation from ISA x 120). Lower QNH = higher PA. Higher temp = higher DA. Practice these calculations -- they appear in every performance question.
key_facts: Pressure altitude = elevation + (1013 - QNH) x 30 feet
QNH below 1013: pressure altitude higher than elevation
QNH above 1013: pressure altitude lower than elevation
Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for temperature deviation from ISA
Each degree C above ISA adds approximately 120 ft to density altitude

syllabus_desc: Express an ambient temperature as a deviation from ISA temperature (and vice versa).
concept: ISA deviation is the difference between the actual ambient temperature and the ISA standard temperature at a given pressure altitude. ISA temperature at sea level is 15C, decreasing at 2C per 1,000 ft. So at pressure altitude 5,000 ft, ISA temperature = 15 - (5 x 2) = 5C. If the actual temperature is 15C at that altitude, the ISA deviation is +10C (10 degrees warmer than standard). To express an ambient temperature as an ISA deviation: calculate the ISA temperature for the pressure altitude, then subtract it from the actual temperature. Conversely, if given an ISA deviation, add it to the ISA temperature at that altitude to get the actual temperature. ISA deviation is commonly used in performance charts and flight planning because it separates the temperature effect from the altitude effect.
exam_tip: ISA temp at PA = 15 - (PA/1000 x 2). ISA deviation = actual temp - ISA temp. Positive deviation = warmer than standard = worse performance. Used in performance charts and flight planning.
key_facts: ISA temperature at sea level: 15C, decreasing 2C per 1,000 ft
ISA deviation = actual temperature - ISA temperature at that pressure altitude
Positive ISA deviation: warmer than standard, worse performance
Negative ISA deviation: cooler than standard, better performance
ISA deviation is used in performance charts to standardise temperature effects

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate an ability to use wind-component graphs, and to apply runway slope and surface correction factors.
concept: Wind-component graphs allow you to resolve the reported wind into headwind/tailwind and crosswind components relative to the runway. Enter the graph with the angle between the wind direction and the runway heading and the wind speed. Read off the headwind (or tailwind) component and crosswind component. For performance calculations, only the headwind/tailwind component is used -- headwind reduces take-off and landing distances, tailwind increases them. Regulations typically require that only 50% of a headwind be used (conservative) and 150% of a tailwind (also conservative). Runway slope is factored in separately: an upslope increases take-off distance and decreases landing distance; a downslope does the opposite. Typical correction: 5% increase in take-off distance per 1% of upslope.
exam_tip: Wind component: use graph to resolve wind into headwind and crosswind. Use 50% of headwind (conservative) and 150% of tailwind. Upslope: +5% take-off distance per 1% slope. Know how to use these graphs.
key_facts: Wind component graphs resolve wind into headwind/tailwind and crosswind
Use 50% of reported headwind for performance calculations (conservative)
Use 150% of reported tailwind for performance calculations (conservative)
Runway slope: ~5% increase in TODR per 1% upslope
Crosswind component must be within aircraft and pilot limitations

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate an ability to calculate take-off and landing performance in accordance with CAR Part 135 Subpart D using representative aeroplane take-off and landing performance charts (P-charts).
concept: Demonstrating the ability to calculate take-off and landing performance means being able to use the POH performance charts with the correct inputs and apply all required corrections. The process for take-off: determine pressure altitude and temperature, enter the take-off distance chart with these values plus the aircraft weight, read the base distance, then apply corrections for wind (headwind reduces, tailwind increases), runway slope (upslope increases), and runway surface (grass adds a percentage). The final TODR must be less than TODA. For landing: enter the landing distance chart with pressure altitude, temperature, and weight. Apply corrections for wind, slope, surface, and condition (wet factor). The final LDR must be less than LDA. Always apply the regulatory safety factors for the operation type. Cross-check your result -- if the answer looks unreasonable, recheck your inputs.
exam_tip: Process: PA + temp -> base distance -> wind correction -> slope correction -> surface correction -> safety factor -> compare to available distance. TODR must be less than TODA. LDR must be less than LDA.
key_facts: Enter charts with pressure altitude, temperature, and weight
Apply corrections: wind, slope, surface condition
Apply regulatory safety factors (typically 1.15 for take-off, 1.43 for landing)
TODR must not exceed TODA; LDR must not exceed LDA
Always sanity-check the result against the available distances

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate an ability to calculate enroute engine inoperative performance using a representative single-engine service ceiling graph.
concept: Enroute engine-inoperative performance calculations determine whether the aircraft can maintain a safe altitude along the planned route with one engine failed. Using the POH performance data, you determine the single-engine rate of climb (or descent) at the current weight, altitude, and temperature. From this, you can calculate the drift-down profile and determine whether the aircraft can clear all obstacles along the route with the required minimum clearance margin. The calculation considers weight at various points along the route (as fuel burns), the terrain and obstacle heights, the single-engine service ceiling, and any emergency procedures such as fuel dumping. For routes over mountainous terrain -- like crossing the Southern Alps in New Zealand -- this calculation is critical. If the single-engine performance is insufficient for obstacle clearance, an alternate route may be required.
exam_tip: Engine-out enroute: use POH to calculate single-engine climb/descent performance. Must clear all obstacles with required margin. Critical for mountain crossings. Plan alternate routes if clearance is insufficient.
key_facts: Calculate single-engine rate of climb/descent from POH at actual conditions
Determine drift-down profile and compare with obstacle heights along route
Must maintain required obstacle clearance margin throughout
Weight decreases as fuel burns, improving performance along the route
Critical for mountainous routes -- plan alternates if clearance is insufficient
